problem: Section 7, Exercise 1: Show that Q is countably infinite.
steps: Observe that the rationals Q can be written as the set of fractions p/q with p, q integers and q nonzero, so Q ⊆ Z × (Z \ {0}) under the map (p, q) → p/q.
Note that Z is countable and Z \ {0} is countable because removing a finite set from a countable set preserves countability.
Use the fact that the Cartesian product of two countable sets is countable, so Z × (Z \ {0}) is countable.
Since each rational number has at least one representation as p/q with q ≠ 0, and the mapping (p, q) → p/q surjects onto Q, we conclude that Q is a countable image of a countable set.
Therefore Q is countable, and since Q has infinitely many distinct elements, it is countably infinite.

problem: Section 7, Exercise 4: (a) A real number x is said to be algebraic (over the rationals) if it satisfies some polynomial equation of positive degree x^n + a_{n-1}x^{n-1} + ··· + a_1x + a_0 = 0 with rational coefficients a_i. Assuming that each polynomial equation has only finitely many roots, show that the set of algebraic numbers is countable. (b) A real number is said to be transcendental if it is not algebraic. Assuming the reals are uncountable, show that the transcendental numbers are uncountable. (It is a somewhat surprising fact that only two transcendental numbers are familiar to us: e and π. Even proving these two numbers transcendental is highly nontrivial.)
steps: Let P be the collection of all nonzero polynomials with rational coefficients of positive degree; each such polynomial can be described by a finite tuple (an, …, a0) of rationals with an ≠ 0, so P is the union over n ∈ ℕ of the set of n+1-tuples of rationals with last coordinate nonzero.

Since ℚ is countable, the finite Cartesian product ℚ^{n+1} is countable, and hence each subset of such tuples determined by a fixed n is countable, so P is a countable union of countable sets and therefore countable.
Each polynomial in P has only finitely many roots, so the set of algebraic numbers is the union, over the countable set P, of finitely many roots per polynomial.
A countable union of finite (and hence countable) sets is countable, hence the algebraic numbers form a countable subset of ℝ.
The transcendental numbers are defined as ℝ minus the algebraic numbers, so they are the complement of a countable set inside the real numbers.
Since ℝ is assumed to be uncountable, removing a countable subset cannot leave a countable remainder (otherwise ℝ would be countable), so the transcendental numbers must be uncountable.

problem: Section 13, Exercise 5: Show that if A is a basis for a topology on X, then the topology generated by A equals the intersection of all topologies on X that contain A. Prove the same if S is a subbasis.
steps: Let A be a basis for a topology on X and let τA denote the topology it generates, namely the collection of all unions of elements of A that satisfy the basis axioms.
If τ is any topology on X that contains A, then τ must also contain every union of elements of A because topologies are closed under arbitrary unions, so τA⊆τ.
This shows that τA is contained in every topology on X that contains A, hence τA is contained in the intersection of all such topologies.

On the other hand, the intersection of all topologies on X containing A is itself a topology, and it obviously contains A, so it contains every union of elements of A, hence τA equals that intersection.
Now let S be a subbasis for a topology on X and let τS be the topology it generates, namely the collection of unions of finite intersections of elements of S.

If τ is a topology on X containing S, then since τ is closed under finite intersections and arbitrary unions, it must also contain every finite intersection of elements of S and every union of those, so τS⊆τ.
Once again τS is contained in every topology on X containing S, so it lies inside the intersection of all such topologies, which is itself a topology containing S and therefore contains τS.
Hence the topology generated by the subbasis S equals the intersection of all topologies on X that contain S.

problem: Section 16, Exercise 1: Show that if Y is a subspace of X, and A is a subset of Y, then the topology A inherits as a subspace of Y is the same as the topology it inherits as a subspace of X.
steps: Let X carry its given topology and let Y be a subspace, so open sets in Y are intersections of open sets in X with Y itself.
The subspace topology on A inherited from Y consists of all sets of the form V∩A where V is open in Y.
For each such V there exists some open set W in X with V=Y∩W, since Y is a subspace of X.
Thus any U=V∩A can be rewritten as U=(Y∩W)∩A=A∩W, showing that U is open in A when A is considered as a subspace of X.
Conversely, beginning with a set U=A∩W where W is open in X, observe that W∩Y is open in Y because Y is a subspace.
Then U=A∩W also equals A∩(W∩Y), so U is open in A as a subspace of Y.
Since every open set in the subspace topology coming from Y arises this way and every such set coincides with an open set coming from X, the two topologies agree on A.
Therefore the subspace topology on A is the same whether we view A as a subset of Y or directly as a subset of X.

problem: Section 18, Exercise 3: Let X and X' denote a single set in the two topologies T and T', respectively. Let i : X' -> X be the identity function. (a) Show that i is continuous if and only if T' is finer than T. (b) Show that i is a homeomorphism if and only if T' = T.
steps: Let i: X' → X be the identity map, so for each point x ∈ X we have i(x) = x, and observe that for any subset U ⊆ X the preimage i⁻¹(U) is exactly U but now considered as a subset of X'.
By definition, i is continuous if for every open set U ∈ T the set i⁻¹(U) is open in X' with respect to T', which is equivalent to demanding U ∈ T'.
Hence i is continuous precisely when every open set of T is also open in T', i.e., when T′ is finer than T, so part (a) is proved.
Moving to part (b), i is bijective with inverse i⁻¹: X → X' also being the identity map, so i will be a homeomorphism if and only if both i and i⁻¹ are continuous.
Applying the same reasoning to i⁻¹ shows that continuity of i⁻¹ requires each open set of T′ to belong to T, that is, T is finer than T′.
Therefore i is a homeomorphism if and only if T′ is finer than T and T is finer than T′, which happens exactly when T′ = T, completing the proof.

problem: Section 20, Exercise 3: Let X be a metric space with metric d. (a) Show that d : X × X -> R is continuous. (b) Let X' denote a space having the same underlying set as X. Show that if d : X' × X' -> R is continuous, then the topology of X' is finer than the topology of X.
steps: Recall that the product topology on X×X has as a basis the sets U×V with U and V open in X, and that the metric topology on X has the usual open balls as a basis.

To show d is continuous from X×X to ℝ it suffices to verify that for every point (x,y)∈X×X and every ε>0, there exists an open neighborhood W×Z of (x,y) such that the image under d is contained in (d(x,y)−ε,d(x,y)+ε).

Given ε>0 and the point (x,y), use the triangle inequality to note that for any pair (x′,y′), |d(x′,y′)−d(x,y)|≤d(x′,x)+d(y′,y), so if we force d(x′,x)<ε/2 and d(y′,y)<ε/2 then d(x′,y′) lies within ε of d(x,y).

Thus take W=B(x,ε/2) and Z=B(y,ε/2); these are open in X, hence W×Z is an open neighborhood of (x,y) in the product topology, and the triangle inequality argument guarantees d(W×Z)⊂(d(x,y)−ε,d(x,y)+ε), completing continuity.
For part (b), suppose d: X'×X'→ℝ is continuous when X' has some topology τ'. Then for any point x∈X and arbitrary ε>0, the preimage of the open interval (0,ε) under the map z↦d(x,z) is open in X'.
But that map is just the restriction of d to {x}×X', which is continuous as a composition of inclusion and d, and its preimage of (0,ε) is the open ball B_d(x,ε) in X, so B_d(x,ε)∈τ'.
Since the metric topology on X is generated by such balls, every basic open set of X is open in X', showing τ' contains the metric topology and hence is finer.

problem: Section 21, Exercise 1: Let A ⊂ X. If d is a metric for the topology of X, show that d|_{A × A} is a metric for the subspace topology on A.
steps: Define d|_{A×A} by restricting the original metric d to pairs of points in A; this function is intended to be the metric for the subspace topology on A.

Verify that d|_{A×A} satisfies the metric axioms relative to A: positivity and definiteness follow from those of d because we only consider the same expressions on a smaller domain, symmetry is inherited, and the triangle inequality holds because any triple of points in A also lie in X where d obeys the triangle inequality.

Conclude that balls in the restricted metric, of the form B_{d|_{A×A}}(a,r), consist of all points b in A such that d(a,b)<r, so these are literally the intersections with A of the balls B_d(a,r) in X.

Use the characterization of the metric topology: a set U ⊂ A is open for d|_{A×A} iff for each point a∈U there is an r>0 with B_{d|_{A×A}}(a,r) ⊂ U, which is equivalent to requiring that the corresponding ball B_d(a,r) in X satisfies B_d(a,r)∩A ⊂ U.

Thus every open set in the restricted metric topology is an intersection of an open set in X with A, so it is open in the subspace topology, and conversely every open subset of A in the subspace topology contains for each point a a ball in X whose intersection with A lies inside, hence is open in the restricted metric topology.
Therefore the topology induced by d|_{A×A} coincides with the subspace topology on A, so d|_{A×A} is a metric for that topology.

problem: Section 30, Exercise 2: Show that if X has a countable basis {B_n}, then every basis C for X contains a countable basis for X. [Hint: For every pair of indices n, m for which it is possible, choose C_{n,m} ∈ C such that B_n ⊂ C_{n,m} ⊂ B_m.]
steps: Let {B_n: n ∈ ℕ} be the countable basis for X that we are given, and let C be an arbitrary basis for X; our goal is to show that C contains a countable subcollection that still forms a basis for X.

For each ordered pair of indices (n, m) for which there exists some C ∈ C with B_n ⊂ C ⊂ B_m, fix and pick one such element and denote it by C_{n,m}; note that such a choice is possible as soon as the containment pattern holds.
Define the chosen collection D = {C_{n,m}: there exists C ∈ C with B_n ⊂ C ⊂ B_m}, which by construction is a subset of C.
Because the index pairs (n, m) range over a subset of ℕ×ℕ, which is countable, it follows that D is at most countable; hence C contains a countable collection D.
To see that D is a basis, take an arbitrary open set U and x ∈ U; since {B_n} is a basis there is B_m with x ∈ B_m ⊂ U, and since C is a basis there is C ∈ C with x ∈ C ⊂ B_m.

Again using the basis {B_n}, choose B_n containing x such that B_n ⊂ C, so that B_n ⊂ C ⊂ B_m; by the definition of D the pair (n, m) corresponds to some C_{n,m} ∈ D with B_n ⊂ C_{n,m} ⊂ B_m, hence x ∈ C_{n,m} ⊂ B_m ⊂ U.
Therefore D satisfies the basis criterion and is countable, so C contains the desired countable basis for X.

problem: Section 34, Exercise 3: Let X be a compact Hausdorff space. Show that X is metrizable if and only if X has a countable basis.
steps: Suppose first that X is compact Hausdorff and has a countable basis; since X is already regular by virtue of being Hausdorff compact, it suffices to apply Urysohn’s metrization theorem for compact regular spaces with a countable base to conclude that X is metrizable.

More concretely, the countable basis for X gives rise to a countable family of continuous real-valued functions that separate points and closed sets, ensuring the existence of an embedding into a separable metric cube, and the compactness of X guarantees that this embedding is a homeomorphism onto its image, which inherits the metric.

Conversely, if X is metrizable then it is first countable and every metric space has a countable base formed by balls with rational radii centered at points from a countable dense subset, so X possesses a countable basis of open sets.

Metric spaces are Hausdorff, so the metrizable hypothesis already implies X is Hausdorff; assuming compactness only reinforces the hypotheses required for the forward direction but is not needed here because the second countability argument comes directly from the metric.
Thus compactness and Hausdorff together with a countable basis imply metrizability, and metrizability gives a countable basis, completing the equivalence.

problem: Section 30, Exercise 3: Let X have a countable basis; let A be an uncountable subset of X. Show that uncountably many points of A are limit points of A.
steps: Let B be a countable basis for X and recall that a point x∈A is a limit point of A precisely when every basis element containing x intersects A in some point other than x itself.

If x∈A is not a limit point, then there is a basis element B_x with x∈B_x and B_x∩A={x}; this shows that non-limit points of A are isolated in the sense that some basis neighborhood around them meets A only at the point itself.
Because B_x∩A={x}, no two distinct isolated points can share the same basis element, so the assignment x↦B_x is injective from isolated points of A into the countable collection B.
Therefore the set of isolated points of A is at most countable, since it injects into the countable basis.

Since A itself is uncountable, the complement of the isolated points in A is uncountable, and every point in that complement must be a limit point (otherwise it would have been isolated). Hence uncountably many points of A are limit points of A, as required.

problem: Section 7, Exercise 6: We say that two sets A and B have the same cardinality if there is a bijection of A with B. (a) Show that if B ⊂ A and if there is an injection f : A -> B, then A and B have the same cardinality. [Hint: Define A_1 = A, B_1 = B, and for n > 1, A_n = f(A_{n-1}) and B_n = f(B_{n-1}). (Recursive definition again!) Note that A_1 ⊃ B_1 ⊃ A_2 ⊃ B_2 ⊃ A_3 ⊃ ···. Define a bijection h : A -> B by the rule h(x) = f(x) if x ∈ A_n - B_n for some n, and h(x) = x otherwise.]
steps: We are given B ⊆ A and an injection f: A → B, and we want to show there is a bijection between A and B, so A and B have the same cardinality.

Define the sequences of subsets recursively: A1 = A, B1 = B, and for n > 1 let An = f(An−1) and Bn = f(Bn−1); by construction each A_n and B_n are subsets of A and B respectively and A1 ⊇ B1 ⊇ A2 ⊇ B2 ⊇ ···.

Observe that for every n, the restriction f: An → Bn+1 is bijective because f is injective on the larger set An, and the images lie exactly in Bn+1, so these maps give bijections between consecutive terms.
Let A0 = ⋃_{n≥1} (An \ Bn), noting that these sets A_n \ B_n are disjoint by construction; on each of these components define h(x)=f(x) which moves the point one step down the chain.
On the remaining part A \ A0 the elements never leave the intersection of all Bn, so we can define h(x)=x there, giving the identity map on that tail.

The resulting map h: A → B is well-defined and injective because on each disjoint part it is either f (injective) or the identity, and it is surjective since every element of B lies either in some Bn+1 and is hit as f(x) for some x∈An, or lies in all Bn and hence equals itself.
Thus h is a bijection from A onto B, proving that A and B have the same cardinality.

problem: Section 13, Exercise 1: Let X be a topological space; let A be a subset of X. Suppose that for each x ∈ A there is an open set U containing x such that U ⊂ A. Show that A is open in X.
steps: Fix an arbitrary point x in A, and invoke the hypothesis to produce an open set U_x with x∈U_x⊆A.
Observe that each U_x is open in X because the hypothesis explicitly gives an open neighborhood for every point of A.
Consider the collection 𝒰={U_x | x∈A}; every element of 𝒰} is open and covers its corresponding point in A.
Because each point of A is contained in some U_x, the union of all members of 𝒰 contains A, so A⊆⋃_{x∈A}U_x.
Conversely, each U_x⊆A, so the union ⋃_{x∈A}U_x is actually a subset of A, hence A=⋃_{x∈A}U_x.
Now ⋃_{x∈A}U_x is a union of open sets, therefore it is open in X.
Since A equals this union, we conclude that A is open in X.

problem: Section 16, Exercise 2: If T and T' are topologies on X and T' is strictly finer than T, what can you say about the corresponding subspace topologies on the subset Y of X?
steps: Recall that the subspace topology on Y coming from a topology S on X is defined as S_Y = {U ∩ Y : U ∈ S}.
Since T is a subset of T′, every set U ∩ Y with U open in T is also a set of the form U ∩ Y with U open in T′, so T_Y ⊆ T′_Y.
Thus the subspace topology induced by T′ on Y is at least as fine as the subspace topology induced by T; the finer topology has every open set of the coarser one.
Because T′ is strictly finer than T, there exists some open set V ∈ T′ that is not in T, and hence V ∩ Y ∈ T′_Y but a priori may not lie in T_Y.
If V ∩ Y cannot be written as U ∩ Y for any open U ∈ T, then the inclusion T_Y ⊂ T′_Y is strict and the subspace topology from T′ is strictly finer than that from T on Y.

On the other hand, if every such extra open set in T′ either misses Y or intersects Y in a set already arising from some U ∈ T, then the two subspace topologies coincide even though T′ is strictly finer on X.
In summary, the subspace topology coming from T′ is finer than that from T, and it is strictly finer precisely when some T′-open set cuts out a new open subset of Y that T cannot produce.

problem: Section 18, Exercise 1: Prove that for functions f : R -> R, the ε-δ definition of continuity implies the open set definition.
steps: Fix a function f: R → R that satisfies the ε-δ definition at every point; that is, for each x₀ in R and each ε>0 there is a δ>0 such that |x - x₀|<δ implies |f(x) - f(x₀)|<ε.
Take an arbitrary open set U⊆R and an arbitrary point x₀∈f⁻¹(U); then f(x₀) lies in the open set U, so there exists some ε>0 with the open ε-ball B(f(x₀), ε) wholly contained in U.
Apply the ε-δ continuity at x₀ to find δ>0 such that whenever |x - x₀|<δ we have |f(x) - f(x₀)|<ε, hence f(x) lies in B(f(x₀), ε).
Conclude that the δ-ball B(x₀, δ) is mapped by f into B(f(x₀), ε) ⊆ U, so every point of B(x₀, δ) belongs to f⁻¹(U).
Therefore B(x₀, δ)⊆f⁻¹(U), showing x₀ is an interior point of f⁻¹(U).
Since x₀ was an arbitrary point of f⁻¹(U), the preimage is open; that is, f⁻¹(U) is a union of such δ-balls.
Because U was any open subset of R, f satisfies the open set definition of continuity.

problem: Section 20, Exercise 1: (a) In R^n, define d'(x, y) = |x_1 - y_1| + ··· + |x_n - y_n|. Show that d' is a metric that induces the usual topology of R^n. Sketch the basis elements under d' when n = 2. (b) More generally, given p ≥ 1, define d'(x, y) = [Σ_{i=1}^n |x_i - y_i|^p]^(1/p) for x, y ∈ R^n. Assume that d' is a metric. Show that it induces the usual topology on R^n.
steps: For part (a) begin by checking that d′(x,y)=∑|xi−yi| indeed satisfies the metric axioms: nonnegativity and definiteness follow from the absolute values summing to zero only when all coordinates agree, symmetry is immediate, and the triangle inequality holds since each coordinate difference obeys |xi−zi|≤|xi−yi|+|yi−zi| and summing over i gives the desired inequality.

Next argue that the topology induced by d′ contains the standard Euclidean basis: given any Euclidean open ball Bε(x) under the usual metric, one can choose δ=ε so that the l1-ball {y: ∑|yi−xi|<δ} is contained in the Euclidean ball because the Euclidean norm is bounded above by the l1 norm, hence any Euclidean-open set is open in the d′-topology.

Conversely, any d′-ball centered at x with radius r is contained in a Euclidean ball of the same radius because the Euclidean norm is at most the l1 norm, so d′-open sets are also Euclidean-open, showing the topologies coincide.
In the n=2 case, describe basis elements as diamond-shaped regions aligned with the coordinate axes, since {y: |y1−x1|+|y2−x2|<ε} is a 45-degree rotated square, so the sketches are those diamonds.

For part (b), the first step is to note that the pseudonorm ‖x−y‖p= (∑|xi−yi|^p)^(1/p) satisfies the metric axioms whenever p≥1, in particular by the generalized Minkowski inequality, so d′ is a metric under the assumption.

To show the induced topology equals the Euclidean one, compare the metric balls: using the relations between the p-norm and the standard Euclidean norm, we can bound ∥x−y∥2 by powers of ∥x−y∥p, hence any p-ball contains an Euclidean ball and vice versa, proving the topologies coincide.

problem: Section 21, Exercise 2: Let X and Y be metric spaces with metrics d_X and d_Y, respectively. Let f : X -> Y have the property that for every pair of points x_1, x_2 of X, d_Y(f(x_1), f(x_2)) = d_X(x_1, x_2). Show that f is an imbedding. It is called an isometric imbedding of X in Y.
steps: Recall that an imbedding is a continuous injection whose inverse is continuous when viewed as a map from the image of f (with the subspace topology coming from Y) back to X.
First note that if x1≠x2 then dX(x1,x2)>0, and since the given condition says dY(f(x1),f(x2))=dX(x1,x2), we deduce dY(f(x1),f(x2))>0, so f(x1)≠f(x2); hence f is injective.
To check continuity of f, fix x∈X and ε>0; taking δ=ε we see that whenever dX(x,y)<δ we have dY(f(x),f(y))=dX(x,y)<ε, which shows f is continuous.

Let Z=f(X) be the image of f, and consider Z with the subspace metric inherited from dY; the associated topology on Z is just the restriction of the Y-topology, so we need to show the inverse map f^{-1}:Z→X is continuous in this topology.

Given a point z=f(x) in Z and ε>0, choose δ=ε; whenever z′∈Z satisfies dY(z,z′)<δ we can write z′=f(y) for some y∈X and then dX(f^{-1}(z),f^{-1}(z′))=dX(x,y)=dY(f(x),f(y))=dY(z,z′)<ε, so f^{-1} is continuous at z, hence continuous globally.
Thus f is an injective, continuous map with continuous inverse on its image, that is, f is an imbedding as required.

problem: Section 23, Exercise 1: Let T and T' be two topologies on X. If T' ⊃ T, what does connectedness of X in one topology imply about connectedness in the other?
steps: We are given two topologies τ′ ⊂ τ on the same set X, so every τ′-open set is also τ-open; in other words τ is finer and τ′ is coarser.
Assume for contradiction that X is connected in τ but not in τ′, i.e. there exist nonempty disjoint sets U′, V′ that are open in τ′ and satisfy U′ ∪ V′ = X.
Since τ′ ⊂ τ, the sets U′ and V′ are also open in τ, so U′ and V′ would provide a separation of X with respect to τ.
This contradicts the assumption that X is τ-connected, therefore no such disconnection can exist in τ′.
We conclude that if X is connected in the finer topology τ, then it must also be connected in the coarser topology τ′.
The converse need not hold because coarser topologies have fewer open sets and so fewer potential separations; a τ′-connected space can become disconnected when we add more opens to get τ.

problem: Section 24, Exercise 1: (a) Show that no two of the spaces (0,1), (0,1], and [0,1] are homeomorphic. [Hint: What happens if you remove a point from each of these spaces?] (b) Suppose that there exist imbeddings f : X -> Y and g : Y -> X. Show by means of an example that X and Y need not be homeomorphic. (c) Show R^n and R are not homeomorphic if n > 1.
steps: For (a) note that removing a point from (0,1) still leaves it connected and with two components, but removing an interior point from [0,1] gives a space with two connected components while removing an endpoint gives a single connected component, so no homeomorphism can match all such behaviors; similarly (0,1) and (0,1] differ because the latter loses compactness after removing 0 but the former remains noncompact, so none of the three pairs can be homeomorphic.

In detail: remove the point 1/2 from each of (0,1), (0,1], and [0,1]; (0,1) remains disconnected with two open arcs, (0,1] loses a point but stays connected at 0, while [0,1] loses an interior point and has two closed intervals, so the number and types of components differ and no homeomorphism can preserve those structures.

For (b) give an example: take X = (0,1) and Y = [0,1], define f : X → Y as the inclusion map, and g : Y → X as a map squeezing [0,1] into (0,1) for example g(t) = (t+1)/3, both are continuous injections with continuous inverses onto their images, so they are embeddings but X and Y are not homeomorphic because Y is compact while X is not.

Explain that embeddings exist because we can injectively map X into Y and Y into X with continuous maps that are homeomorphisms onto their images, yet the entire spaces differ in compactness, which is a topologically invariant property preventing a homeomorphism.

For (c) if n>1 then R^n minus a point remains connected, while R minus a point is disconnected into two rays, so any homeomorphism would preserve the number of components after point-removal and hence cannot exist when n>1.

problem: Section 27, Exercise 1: Prove that if X is an ordered set in which every closed interval is compact. then X has the least upper bound property.
steps: Let A be a nonempty subset of X that is bounded above, choose some fixed a∈A and let b be an upper bound, so the closed interval [a,b] is compact by hypothesis.
Define U to be the set of all upper bounds of A; since every element of U is ≥a and ≤b, we know U⊆[a,b].

Each condition a′≤x (with a′∈A) defines a closed set, so U as an intersection of such sets is closed, hence U is compact as a closed subset of the compact interval [a,b]; note U is nonempty because b∈U.
Assume for contradiction that U has no minimum; then for every x∈U we can pick y_x∈U with y_x<x, and the family of open sets (y_x,∞) covers U in the order topology.

If a finite subcollection (y_{x1},∞),…,(y_{xn},∞) covered U then their union is (min_i y_{xi},∞), but the assumption of no minimum guarantees some z∈U with z≤min_i y_{xi}, so z is not covered, contradicting compactness.
Thus U must have a minimum c, and since c∈U it is an upper bound of A.
Minimality of c implies that c≤u for every other upper bound u, so c is the least upper bound of A.
Therefore every nonempty subset of X that is bounded above has a supremum, and X has the least upper bound property.

problem: Section 34, Exercise 1: Give an example showing that a Hausdorff space with a countable basis need not be metrizable.
steps: Let K={1/n: n∈N}⊂R and equip R with the topology whose basis consists of the usual open intervals (a,b) together with the sets (a,b)\K; this is the classical K-topology which refines the standard topology on R.

Because every open set in the new topology contains an open interval from the usual topology, the space is still Hausdorff; any two distinct points can be separated by standard intervals, hence by neighborhoods in the finer topology.

To see that the space has a countable basis, observe that the collection of sets (a,b) and (a,b)\K with a,b∈Q already forms a basis: they generate the same topology by refinement arguments, and since Q² is countable the union of these families is countable.
The set K itself is closed: if x∉K then either x=0 or x lies in an interval disjoint from K, so there is a basis neighborhood around x avoiding K; hence R\K is open.

If the space were regular, then the closed set K and the point 0∉K could be separated by disjoint neighborhoods. But every neighborhood of 0 contains some (−ε,ε)\K, and any neighborhood of K must, for each 1/n, contain a standard interval (a_n,b_n) with 1/n∈(a_n,b_n); choosing n large enough so that 1/n<ε, the irrational points in (a_n,b_n) lie in (−ε,ε) as well, so the neighborhood of K intersects the neighborhood of 0.
Thus no pair of disjoint neighborhoods separates 0 and K, so the space fails to be regular even though it is Hausdorff with a countable basis.
Since every metric space is regular, the K-topology example shows that a Hausdorff space with a countable basis need not be metrizable.

problem: Section 1C, Exercise 10: Suppose 𝑉1 and 𝑉2 are subspaces of 𝑉. Prove that the intersection 𝑉1 ∩ 𝑉2 is a subspace of 𝑉.
steps: Recall that to prove a subset of V is a subspace we must show it is nonempty and closed under addition and scalar multiplication while inheriting the operations from V.
Observe that since V₁ and V₂ are subspaces of V, they each contain the zero vector of V, so 0 belongs to V₁ ∩ V₂, making the intersection nonempty.
Take any two vectors u and v in V₁ ∩ V₂; by definition both u and v are in V₁ and in V₂ simultaneously.
Since V₁ is a subspace it is closed under vector addition, so u + v lies in V₁, and the same reasoning in V₂ shows u + v lies there as well, implying u + v is in the intersection.
Next, pick any scalar c and any vector u in V₁ ∩ V₂; u is again in both subspaces, and scalar closure in each shows c u lies in V₁ and in V₂, hence in the intersection.
Having shown the intersection contains zero and is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, we conclude that V₁ ∩ V₂ is itself a subspace of V.

problem: Section 1C, Exercise 11: Prove that the intersection of every collection of subspaces of 𝑉 is a subspace of 𝑉.
steps: Let {W_i | i in I} be a collection of subspaces of V indexed by some set I, and define their intersection as ∩_{i in I} W_i = {v in V | v in W_i for every i in I}.
Because every W_i is a subspace, each contains the zero vector of V, so 0 is in every W_i and hence 0 belongs to the intersection, which shows the intersection is nonempty.
Now take any two vectors u and v in the intersection; by definition of the intersection, u and v lie in each W_i.
Since each W_i is closed under addition, the sum u + v lies in every W_i, so u + v is also in the intersection.
Next take any scalar c in the field over which V is defined and any vector u in the intersection; again u lies in each W_i.
Because each W_i is closed under scalar multiplication, the scalar multiple c u lies in each W_i, so c u belongs to the intersection.
Having shown that the intersection contains 0 and is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, we conclude that the intersection is a subspace of V.

problem: Section 1C, Exercise 12: Prove that the union of two subspaces of 𝑉 is a subspace of 𝑉 if and only if one of the subspaces is contained in the other.
steps: Let U and W be subspaces of V and assume their union U ∪ W is itself a subspace; we aim to show that one of U or W contains the other.
Since U ∪ W is a subspace, it is closed under addition. Take arbitrary vectors u ∈ U and w ∈ W, and consider their sum u + w which must lie in U ∪ W.

Because u + w is in the union, it lies either in U or in W. Without loss of generality suppose u + w ∈ U and observe that u ∈ U already, so W must be contained in U because any w ∈ W can be written as (u + w) − u, and the difference of two vectors in U is still in U.
Hence W ⊆ U, establishing that if the union is a subspace, then one subspace is contained in the other.
Conversely, assume W ⊆ U; then U ∪ W = U, and since U is a subspace the union is also a subspace.
The reverse containment case W ⊆ U is symmetric, so either way the union being a subspace forces containment, and containment guarantees the union is a subspace.
Therefore the union of the two subspaces is a subspace if and only if one of them is contained in the other.

problem: Section 2A, Exercise 3: Suppose 𝑣1, …, 𝑣𝑚 is a list of vectors in 𝑉. For 𝑘 ∈ {1, …, 𝑚}, let 𝑤𝑘 = 𝑣1 + ⋯ + 𝑣𝑘. Show that span(𝑣1, …, 𝑣𝑚 ) = span(𝑤1, …, 𝑤𝑚 ).
steps: Introduce the two spans and note that each wk is defined as a partial sum of the vi, so every wk is a linear combination of v1,…,vk with all coefficients equal to 1, hence wk lies in span(v1,…,vm), which shows span(w1,…,wm) ⊆ span(v1,…,vm).

Conversely, express each vk in terms of the wk; specifically, observe that v1 = w1 and for k > 1 we have vk = wk − wk−1, so each original vk is a linear combination of consecutive w’s with coefficients 1 and −1.
From this relation deduce that every vk ∈ span(w1,…,wm), which gives span(v1,…,vm) ⊆ span(w1,…,wm).
Combine the two inclusions to conclude span(v1,…,vm) = span(w1,…,wm).
Mention that the equality of spans is thus established by showing that each generating set can be written in terms of the other, completing the argument.

problem: Section 2A, Exercise 8: Suppose 𝑣1, 𝑣2, 𝑣3, 𝑣4 is linearly independent in 𝑉. Prove that the list 𝑣1 − 𝑣2, 𝑣2 − 𝑣3, 𝑣3 − 𝑣4, 𝑣4 is also linearly independent.
steps: Assume a linear combination of the list v1 − v2, v2 − v3, v3 − v4, v4 equals the zero vector: a(v1 − v2) + b(v2 − v3) + c(v3 − v4) + d v4 = 0.
Distribute scalars to rewrite this as a v1 + (−a + b) v2 + (−b + c) v3 + (−c + d) v4 = 0.
Because v1, v2, v3, v4 are given to be linearly independent, the coefficients in this combination must all be zero, so a = 0, −a + b = 0, −b + c = 0, and −c + d = 0.
From a = 0 we immediately get b = 0, and then from −b + c = 0 we get c = 0.
Finally, −c + d = 0 with c = 0 forces d = 0, so all scalars in the assumed combination are zero.
Since the only solution to the homogeneous combination is trivial, the list v1 − v2, v2 − v3, v3 − v4, v4 is linearly independent.

problem: Section 2C, Exercise 12: Suppose that 𝑈 and 𝑊 are subspaces of 𝐑8 such that dim 𝑈 = 3, dim 𝑊 = 5, and 𝑈 + 𝑊 = 𝐑8. Prove that 𝐑8 = 𝑈 ⊕ 𝑊.
steps: Since U and W are subspaces of R^8 with dim U=3 and dim W=5, the sum U+W is also a subspace of R^8 and its dimension satisfies dim(U+W)=dim U+dim W - dim(U∩W).
We are given that U+W=R^8, so dim(U+W)=dim R^8=8.
Substituting into the dimension formula gives 8=3+5 - dim(U∩W).
Solving this equation yields dim(U∩W)=0.
A subspace with dimension zero contains only the zero vector, so U∩W={0}.
Because U+W=R^8 and U∩W={0}, every vector in R^8 has a unique representation as a sum of vectors from U and W.
Therefore R^8=U⊕W, as required.

problem: Section 3B, Exercise 9: Suppose 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉, 𝑊) is injective and 𝑣1, …, 𝑣𝑛 is linearly independent in 𝑉. Prove that 𝑇𝑣1, …, 𝑇𝑣𝑛 is linearly independent in 𝑊.
steps: Assume there is a linear relation c₁T(v₁)+⋯+cₙT(vₙ)=0 in W and aim to show all the scalars c_j are zero, proving the images are linearly independent.
Use linearity of T to rewrite the relation as T(c₁v₁+⋯+cₙvₙ)=0_W, so the combination inside T maps to the zero vector in W.
Injectivity of T means the only vector sent to zero is zero itself, so we deduce c₁v₁+⋯+cₙvₙ=0_V.
Since v₁,…,vₙ are given to be linearly independent in V, the relation c₁v₁+⋯+cₙvₙ=0_V forces each c_j to be zero.
Thus the only linear relation among T(v₁),…,T(vₙ) is the trivial one, which shows T(v₁),…,T(vₙ) are linearly independent in W.

problem: Section 3B, Exercise 10: Suppose 𝑣1, …, 𝑣𝑛 spans 𝑉 and 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉, 𝑊). Show that 𝑇𝑣1, …, 𝑇𝑣𝑛 spans range 𝑇.
steps: Since v1,…,vn spans V, every vector v∈V can be written as a linear combination v=∑ajvj for some scalars aj.
Apply T to an arbitrary v∈V to get T(v)=T(∑ajvj).
Use linearity of T to expand T(v)=∑ajT(vj), showing that each T(v) belongs to the span of T(v1),…,T(vn).
This shows range T⊆span{T(v1),…,T(vn)}, because every vector in range T is T(v) for some v and hence a linear combination of the T(vj).
For the reverse inclusion, each T(vj) is obviously in range T because range T contains all outputs T(v) for v∈V.
Therefore span{T(v1),…,T(vn)}⊆range T, since the range already contains all T(vj) and hence all combinations of them.
Combining these containments, we conclude range T=span{T(v1),…,T(vn)}, so T(v1),…,T(vn) indeed span range T.

problem: Section 3B, Exercise 11: Suppose that 𝑉 is finite-dimensional and that 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉, 𝑊). Prove that there exists a subspace 𝑈 of 𝑉 such that 𝑈 ∩ null 𝑇 = {0} and range 𝑇 = {𝑇𝑢 ∶ 𝑢 ∈ 𝑈}.
steps: Start by choosing a basis {n1,…,nk} for null T, which is possible since V is finite-dimensional.
Extend this basis of null T to a basis of V by adding vectors {u1,…,um}, so {n1,…,nk,u1,…,um} is a basis of V.
Define U to be the span of {u1,…,um}; by construction U ∩ null T = {0} because the combined basis has no nontrivial linear relations.
Now show range T is contained in {T u : u∈U} by taking an arbitrary v∈V, writing v as a linear combination of the basis, and noting that T annihilates the ni’s, so T(v) depends only on the u’s.
Conversely, any vector of the form T(u) with u∈U lies in range T by definition, so range T equals {T u : u∈U}, completing the proof.

problem: Section 3B, Exercise 12: Suppose 𝑇 is a linear map from 𝐅4 to 𝐅2 such that null 𝑇 = {(𝑥1, 𝑥2, 𝑥3, 𝑥4 ) ∈ 𝐅4 ∶ 𝑥1 = 5𝑥2 and 𝑥3 = 7𝑥4 }. Prove that 𝑇 is surjective.
steps: Since T is a linear map from F^4 to F^2, the Rank-Nullity Theorem tells us that dim(range T) + dim(null T) = 4.
The given description null T = {(x1,x2,x3,x4) ∈ F^4 : x1 = 5x2 and x3 = 7x4} shows that every vector in null T is determined by the free entries x2 and x4.
Hence null T = {x2 (5,1,0,0) + x4 (0,0,7,1) : x2, x4 ∈ F}, so null T is spanned by the two vectors (5,1,0,0) and (0,0,7,1).
These two spanning vectors are linearly independent because they have disjoint support in the coordinates where they are nonzero, so dim(null T) = 2.
Applying Rank-Nullity, we get dim(range T) = 4 − dim(null T) = 2.
Since range T is a subspace of F^2 with dimension 2, it must equal F^2 and therefore T is surjective.

problem: Section 3C, Exercise 5: Suppose 𝑉 and 𝑊 are finite-dimensional and 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉, 𝑊). Prove that there exist a basis of 𝑉 and a basis of 𝑊 such that with respect to these bases, all entries of ℳ(𝑇) are 0 except that the entries in row 𝑘, column 𝑘, equal 1 if 1 ≤ 𝑘 ≤ dim range 𝑇.
steps: Let r = dim range T, and choose a basis w₁,...,w_r of range T; extend this to a basis of W by adding vectors w_{r+1},...,w_m so that W has dimension m.
Since each w_k for k≤r is in the range of T, pick v_k ∈ V with T(v_k)=w_k; then the vectors v₁,...,v_r are linearly independent because any relation would give a relation among the w_k.
Extend v₁,...,v_r to a basis of V by adding vectors v_{r+1},...,v_n so that V has dimension n, which is possible since V is finite-dimensional.
Compute T on the basis of V: for k≤r we already have T(v_k)=w_k, and for k>r we can write T(v_k)=∑_{j=1}^m a_{jk} w_j since the w_j span W.

With respect to the chosen bases of V and W, the matrix of T has the first r columns equal to e_k (the standard basis vectors) in the sense that the only nonzero entry in column k for k≤r is a 1 in row k, because T(v_k)=w_k.

The remaining columns k>r have some entries but can be arranged so that their first r rows may vanish by subtracting suitable linear combinations of the first r columns, or more directly, we accept that the span of T(v_{r+1}),...,T(v_n) lies in W so those columns only contribute beyond the first r rows.
Thus the entire matrix has zeros outside the first r rows and columns, and has 1s on the diagonal entries (k,k) for k≤r, giving the desired form with only r nonzero rows and columns.
We conclude that with these bases the matrix of T has all entries zero except that the (k,k) entries equal 1 for 1≤k≤ dim range T, as required.

problem: Section 5A, Exercise 8: Suppose 𝑃 ∈ ℒ(𝑉) is such that 𝑃2 = 𝑃. Prove that if 𝜆 is an eigenvalue of 𝑃, then 𝜆 = 0 or 𝜆 = 1.
steps: Assume λ is an eigenvalue of P with corresponding eigenvector v ≠ 0, so Pv = λv.
Apply P to both sides of this equation to get P(Pv) = P(λv).
The left-hand side is P^2 v, while the right-hand side is λPv = λ^2 v.
Because P^2 = P we know P^2 v = Pv, so Pv = λ^2 v.
Substitute Pv = λv into that equation to obtain λv = λ^2 v, which becomes (λ^2 − λ)v = 0.
Since v ≠ 0, the scalar factor must vanish, so λ(λ − 1) = 0, forcing λ = 0 or λ = 1.

problem: Section 5A, Exercise 10: Define 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝒫4 (𝐑)) by (𝑇𝑝)(𝑥) = 𝑥𝑝′(𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝐑. Find all eigenval- ues and eigenvectors of 𝑇.
steps: We are working in the vector space P4(R) of real polynomials of degree at most 4, and T is defined by (T p)(x) = x p′(x). Since T involves only differentiation and multiplying by x, it preserves the degree of polynomials up to one less but also scales in a predictable way for monomials.
Consider a basis of monomials p(x) = x^k for 0 ≤ k ≤ 4. Then p′(x) = k x^{k−1}, so (T p)(x) = x * k x^{k−1} = k x^k. Thus each monomial x^k is simply scaled by k.
The computation shows that x^k is an eigenvector with eigenvalue k, for each k from 0 to 4. In particular, T(x^0) = 0 so 0 is an eigenvalue, and T(x^k) = k x^k for k ≥ 1 gives eigenvalues 1, 2, 3, 4.
Since the monomials x^0, x^1, x^2, x^3, x^4 are linearly independent and span P4(R), we have found five linearly independent eigenvectors, so T is diagonalizable with respect to this basis.
Therefore, the set of eigenvalues of T is {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}, each with corresponding eigenvector x^k for the eigenvalue k.
Finally, the eigenspaces are one-dimensional, spanned by x^k for each eigenvalue k, completing the classification of eigenvalues and eigenvectors of T.

problem: Section 5A, Exercise 12: Suppose 𝑉 = 𝑈 ⊕ 𝑊, where 𝑈 and 𝑊 are nonzero subspaces of 𝑉. Define 𝑃 ∈ ℒ(𝑉) by 𝑃(𝑢 + 𝑤) = 𝑢 for each 𝑢 ∈ 𝑈 and each 𝑤 ∈ 𝑊. Find all eigenvalues and eigenvectors of 𝑃.
steps: Because V=U⊕W, every v∈V has a unique decomposition v=u+w with u∈U and w∈W, and the definition P(u+w)=u makes P a well-defined projection map from V onto U along W.
Check that P is linear: given v1=u1+w1 and v2=u2+w2, and a scalar α, we compute P(v1+v2)=P((u1+u2)+(w1+w2))=u1+u2=P(v1)+P(v2) and P(αv1)=P(αu1+αw1)=αu1=αP(v1).
For any u∈U we have P(u)=u, so u is an eigenvector of P with eigenvalue 1, showing U⊆E1 where E1 denotes the eigenspace for λ=1.
For any w∈W we have P(w)=0, so w is an eigenvector with eigenvalue 0, showing W⊆E0.
If v is an eigenvector with eigenvalue λ, then writing v=u+w we have P(v)=u=λ(u+w), i.e., u=λu+λw and hence (1−λ)u=λw.
Because U∩W={0}, the equation (1−λ)u=λw implies that both sides must be zero separately; this forces λw=0 and (1−λ)u=0, so λ=0 or λ=1 depending on whether u=0 or w=0.
If λ=1 then (1−λ)u=0 holds automatically and λw=0 forces w=0, which means v=u∈U; conversely if λ=0 then λw=0 is automatic and (1−λ)u=u=0 forces u=0 and hence v=w∈W.
Therefore the only eigenvalues are 1 and 0, with eigenspaces E1=U and E0=W respectively, proving we have found all eigenpairs.

problem: Section 5A, Exercise 13: Suppose 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉). Suppose 𝑆 ∈ ℒ(𝑉) is invertible. (a) Prove that 𝑇 and 𝑆−1 𝑇𝑆 have the same eigenvalues. (b) What is the relationship between the eigenvectors of 𝑇 and the eigen- vectors of 𝑆−1 𝑇𝑆?
steps: Assume λ is an eigenvalue of T with corresponding eigenvector v ≠ 0, so T v = λ v.
Set w = S^{-1} v; then S w = v and we compute (S^{-1} T S) w = S^{-1} T (S w) = S^{-1} T v = S^{-1} (λ v) = λ S^{-1} v = λ w, showing w ≠ 0 is an eigenvector of S^{-1} T S with eigenvalue λ.
Thus every eigenvalue of T is an eigenvalue of S^{-1} T S, giving one inclusion of the spectra.

Conversely, suppose λ is an eigenvalue of S^{-1} T S with eigenvector w, so (S^{-1} T S) w = λ w; then S w ≠ 0 and T (S w) = S (S^{-1} T S w) = S (λ w) = λ (S w), proving λ is also an eigenvalue of T.
This shows the spectra of T and S^{-1} T S coincide, completing part (a).

For part (b), the calculations already provide the relationship: v is an eigenvector of T with eigenvalue λ if and only if w = S^{-1} v is an eigenvector of S^{-1} T S with the same λ, so S and S^{-1} give the bijection between the corresponding eigenspaces.

problem: Section 5A, Exercise 15: Suppose 𝑉 is finite-dimensional, 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉), and 𝜆 ∈ 𝐅. Show that 𝜆 is an eigenvalue of 𝑇 if and only if 𝜆 is an eigenvalue of the dual operator 𝑇 ′ ∈ ℒ(𝑉 ′ ).
steps: Recall that λ is an eigenvalue of T precisely when T−λI fails to be injective, i.e., when there exists a nonzero v with (T−λI)v=0, so Ker(T−λI)≠{0}.
Since V is finite-dimensional, its dual V′ has the same dimension, and taking the dual of the operator T−λI gives (T−λI)′=T′−λI′, so λ is an eigenvalue of T′ exactly when Ker(T′−λI′)≠{0}.
The operators T−λI and its dual T′−λI′ have the same rank because taking duals preserves rank when the space is finite-dimensional, so nullity must match as well.
Thus Ker(T−λI)≠{0} if and only if Ker(T′−λI′)≠{0}, because rank-nullity on both sides enforces that a drop in injectivity of T−λI shows up in its dual.
Therefore λ is an eigenvalue of T if and only if λ is an eigenvalue of T′, completing the proof.

problem: Section 5B, Exercise 1: Suppose 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉). Prove that 9 is an eigenvalue of 𝑇 2 if and only if 3 or −3 is an eigenvalue of 𝑇.
steps: Assume 9 is an eigenvalue of T^2, so there exists a nonzero vector v with T^2 v = 9v, and hence v is in the nullspace of the operator T^2 − 9I.
Observe that T^2 − 9I factors as (T − 3I)(T + 3I), so v is in the kernel of the product (T − 3I)(T + 3I).

Since v ≠ 0, the operator (T − 3I)(T + 3I) is not injective, and therefore not invertible; but the product of two operators is invertible exactly when both factors are, so at least one of T − 3I or T + 3I fails to be invertible.
If T − 3I is not invertible then it has a nontrivial kernel, i.e., there exists a nonzero eigenvector with eigenvalue 3; if instead T + 3I is not invertible then −3 is an eigenvalue of T.
Conversely, if 3 is an eigenvalue of T then T − 3I fails to be injective and hence is not invertible, so the product (T − 3I)(T + 3I) = T^2 − 9I is also not invertible and 9 is an eigenvalue of T^2.
Similarly, if −3 is an eigenvalue of T then T + 3I is not invertible, implying again that T^2 − 9I is not invertible and 9 is an eigenvalue of T^2.
Thus 9 is an eigenvalue of T^2 if and only if either 3 or −3 is an eigenvalue of T.

problem: Section 6A, Exercise 6: Suppose 𝑢, 𝑣 ∈ 𝑉. Prove that ⟨𝑢, 𝑣⟩ = 0 ⟺ ‖𝑢‖ ≤ ‖𝑢 + 𝑎𝑣‖ for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐅.
steps: We are given that u and v lie in an inner product space V, and since the norm satisfies ‖w‖^2 = ⟨w,w⟩ for every w, it suffices to compare the squares of the norms in the inequality.
For an arbitrary scalar a ∈ F we expand the square of the norm: ‖u + av‖^2 = ⟨u + av, u + av⟩ = ⟨u,u⟩ + a⟨v,u⟩ + ā⟨u,v⟩ + |a|^2⟨v,v⟩.

Because the inner product is linear in the first argument and conjugate linear in the second, the mixed terms combine as 2 Re(a⟨v,u⟩), giving the identity ‖u + av‖^2 = ‖u‖^2 + 2 Re( a ⟨v,u⟩ ) + |a|^2 ‖v‖^2.
If ⟨u,v⟩ = 0 then the mixed term vanishes and we obtain ‖u + av‖^2 = ‖u‖^2 + |a|^2 ‖v‖^2, which is clearly at least ‖u‖^2 for every a, so the inequality holds in this direction.
To prove the converse, assume that the inequality holds for every a and aim to deduce ⟨u,v⟩ = 0; the case v = 0 is immediate because then ⟨u,v⟩ = 0 and ‖u + av‖ = ‖u‖ in all cases.

If v ≠ 0 and we suppose ⟨u,v⟩ is some nonzero scalar s, then we can choose a = -s̄/‖v‖^2 so that the linear term becomes −2|s|^2/‖v‖^2, while the quadratic term contributes |s|^2/‖v‖^2, giving ‖u + av‖^2 = ‖u‖^2 − |s|^2/‖v‖^2.
That computation contradicts the assumed inequality because the right-hand side is strictly less than ‖u‖^2 whenever s ≠ 0, so we must have s = ⟨u,v⟩ = 0.
Thus, the inequality holds for all a if and only if ⟨u,v⟩ = 0, completing the proof.

problem: Section 6A, Exercise 9: Suppose 𝑢, 𝑣 ∈ 𝑉 and ‖𝑢‖ = ‖𝑣‖ = 1 and ⟨𝑢, 𝑣⟩ = 1. Prove that 𝑢 = 𝑣.
steps: We are given that u and v are vectors in an inner-product space V with norms ∥u∥ = ∥v∥ = 1 and inner product ⟨u,v⟩ = 1.
Recall that the squared norm of the difference of two vectors is ∥u−v∥² = ⟨u−v, u−v⟩.
Expanding the inner product gives ⟨u−v, u−v⟩ = ⟨u,u⟩ − ⟨u,v⟩ − ⟨v,u⟩ + ⟨v,v⟩.
Since the inner product is conjugate symmetric and u and v are real (or we can note the real part of ⟨u,v⟩ is 1), the middle terms combine to −2⟨u,v⟩ = −2.
Therefore ∥u−v∥² = 1 − 2 + 1 = 0.
A norm is nonnegative and vanishes only at the zero vector, so ∥u−v∥² = 0 implies u−v = 0.
Thus u = v, which completes the proof.

problem: Section 6A, Exercise 11: Find vectors 𝑢, 𝑣 ∈ 𝐑2 such that 𝑢 is a scalar multiple of (1, 3), 𝑣 is orthog- onal to (1, 3), and (1, 2) = 𝑢 + 𝑣.
steps: We seek u in the span of (1,3) and v orthogonal to (1,3) such that their sum equals (1,2).
Because u is a scalar multiple of (1,3), write u = λ(1,3) for some real λ and aim to choose λ so that v = (1,2) - λ(1,3) is orthogonal to (1,3).
Orthogonality means (1,2) - λ(1,3) dot (1,3) = 0, so compute (1,2)·(1,3) - λ(1,3)·(1,3) = 0.
Calculate the dot products: (1,2)·(1,3) = 1 + 6 = 7 and (1,3)·(1,3) = 1 + 9 = 10, giving 7 - 10λ = 0.
Check orthogonality: (3/10, -1/10)·(1,3) = 3/10 - 3/10 = 0, confirming v is perpendicular to (1,3).
Therefore the desired decomposition is (1,2) = (7/10, 21/10) + (3/10, -1/10) with u = (7/10, 21/10) and v = (3/10, -1/10).

problem: Section 7B, Exercise 10: Suppose 𝑉 is a complex inner product space. Prove that every normal operator on 𝑉 has a square root. An operator 𝑆 ∈ ℒ(𝑉) is called a square root of 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉) if 𝑆2 = 𝑇. We will discuss more about square roots of operators in Sections 7C and 8C.
steps: Since T is normal on the complex inner product space V, the spectral theorem gives an orthonormal basis {v1, …, vn} of V consisting of eigenvectors of T with corresponding eigenvalues λ1, …, λn in C.
Define scalars μi by choosing one of the square roots of each eigenvalue, for example the principal square root, so that μi^2 = λi for all i.
Define a linear operator S by specifying Svi = μi vi for each basis vector and extending linearly to all of V.
This definition makes S well defined because the vi form a basis, and linearity ensures S is in L(V).
Compute S^2 on each basis vector: S^2vi = S(μi vi) = μi Svi = μi^2 vi = λi vi, which matches Tvi.
Because both S^2 and T agree on the basis {vi}, they agree on all of V, so S^2 = T and S is a square root of T.
Moreover, S is normal because it is diagonal with respect to the same orthonormal eigenbasis, so its adjoint is obtained by conjugating the diagonal entries and still commutes with S.

problem: Section 7B, Exercise 15: Suppose 𝐅 = 𝐂 and 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉). Prove that 𝑇 is normal if and only if all pairs of eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues of 𝑇 are orthogonal and 𝑉 = 𝐸(𝜆1, 𝑇) ⊕ ⋯ ⊕ 𝐸(𝜆𝑚, 𝑇), where 𝜆1, …, 𝜆𝑚 denote the distinct eigenvalues of 𝑇.
steps: Assume T is normal, so T*T=TT*. By Schur’s Theorem over C we can find an orthonormal basis of V relative to which the matrix of T is upper triangular, and the diagonal entries are the eigenvalues λ1,…,λn (with repetitions).

In this basis T* is lower triangular, so TT* is upper triangular while T*T is lower triangular; since they coincide by normality, all off-diagonal entries of T must vanish, forcing the matrix of T to be diagonal.

Hence the orthonormal basis supplied by Schur consists entirely of eigenvectors of T, and the eigenvectors associated with distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal because they are distinct basis vectors in an orthonormal basis.
From the fact that this orthonormal eigenbasis spans V, we see that V is the direct sum of the eigenspaces E(λ1,T)⊕⋯⊕E(λm,T), with m equal to the number of distinct eigenvalues.
Conversely, suppose V decomposes as the orthogonal direct sum of the eigenspaces corresponding to the distinct eigenvalues and that eigenvectors from different eigenspaces are orthogonal.
Inside each eigenspace we choose an orthonormal basis, and since the eigenspaces are orthogonal and span V we obtain a full orthonormal basis of V consisting of eigenvectors of T.
Relative to this orthonormal eigenbasis the matrix of T is diagonal with the eigenvalues λ1,…,λm along the diagonal, so T* is diagonal with the conjugates ¯λ1,…,¯λm along the diagonal.
Diagonal matrices commute, hence TT*=T*T, so T is normal.

problem: Section 8A, Exercise 15: Suppose 𝐅 = 𝐂 and 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉). Prove that 𝑇 is diagonalizable if and only if every generalized eigenvector of 𝑇 is an eigenvector of 𝑇. For 𝐅 = 𝐂, this exercise adds another equivalence to the list of conditions for diagonalizability in 5.55.
steps: Since we are over ℂ, the minimal polynomial of T splits completely, and T is diagonalizable exactly when this minimal polynomial has no repeated roots, i.e. when it is a product of distinct linear factors.

If v is a generalized eigenvector for eigenvalue λ then (T−λI)ᵏv=0 for some k, and when the minimal polynomial has distinct factors the polynomial (T−λI) kills every such v already, because (T−λI) is a factor of the minimal polynomial and hence annihilates v; therefore (T−λI)v=0 and v is an eigenvector.
This argument shows that whenever T is diagonalizable every generalized eigenvector is actually an eigenvector, giving the forward implication.

Conversely suppose every generalized eigenvector of T is an eigenvector; then for each eigenvalue λ the generalized eigenspace ker((T−λI)ᵏ) equals ker(T−λI) for sufficiently large k, because any vector satisfying the higher power equation already satisfies the first power equation.

Hence on each generalized eigenspace the nilpotent part (T−λI) acts as zero, so there is no Jordan block of size greater than one for any eigenvalue, which means the minimal polynomial cannot have repeated factors.
Therefore the minimal polynomial is a product of distinct linear factors, implying T is diagonalizable and completing the converse direction.

problem: Section 1C, Exercise 3: Show that the set of differentiable real-valued functions 𝑓 on the interval (−4, 4) such that 𝑓 ′(−1) = 3 𝑓 (2) is a subspace of 𝐑(−4,4).
steps: The ambient vector space R(−4,4) consists of all real-valued functions defined and differentiable on (−4,4) with the pointwise addition and scalar multiplication inherited from the usual operations on functions.
The subset under consideration is V = {f ∈ R(−4,4) | f ′(−1) = 3 f(2)}, so any member of V must satisfy this linear relation between its derivative at −1 and its value at 2.
The zero function z(x) = 0 is differentiable on (−4,4), with z ′(−1) = 0 and z(2) = 0, so the condition z ′(−1) = 3 z(2) is satisfied and hence the zero vector lies in V.

If f and g are in V then f ′(−1) = 3 f(2) and g ′(−1) = 3 g(2); linearity of the derivative gives (f + g) ′(−1) = f ′(−1) + g ′(−1) = 3 f(2) + 3 g(2) = 3(f(2) + g(2)), so f + g still satisfies the defining relation and lies in V.
For any scalar c and any f in V we have (c f) ′(−1) = c f ′(−1) = c · 3 f(2) = 3(c f(2)), hence scalar multiples of elements of V remain in V.
Having shown that V contains the zero vector and is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, we conclude that V is a subspace of R(−4,4).

problem: Section 2A, Exercise 1: Find a list of four distinct vectors in 𝐅3 whose span equals {(𝑥, 𝑦, 𝑧) ∈ 𝐅3 ∶ 𝑥 + 𝑦 + 𝑧 = 0}.
steps: The set {(x,y,z) : x+y+z=0} is the kernel of the linear functional (x,y,z)↦x+y+z, so it is a two-dimensional subspace of F^3, and every vector in it satisfies x=−y−z.
Using the relation x=−y−z, any vector in the plane can be written as y(−1,1,0)+z(−1,0,1), so {v1=(−1,1,0), v2=(−1,0,1)} is a basis for the subspace.
To produce four distinct vectors in this subspace, take v3=v1+v2=(−2,1,1) and v4=2v1=(−2,2,0); each is still in the plane because their coordinates add to zero.
The four vectors v1, v2, v3, v4 are distinct and all lie in the plane x+y+z=0, so their span is contained in the plane.
Because v1 and v2 from the spanning set already form a basis, the span of {v1,v2,v3,v4} contains the whole plane, hence the span equals {(x,y,z): x+y+z=0}.

problem: Section 2A, Exercise 2: Prove or give a counterexample: If 𝑣1, 𝑣2, 𝑣3, 𝑣4 spans 𝑉, then the list 𝑣1 − 𝑣2, 𝑣2 − 𝑣3, 𝑣3 − 𝑣4, 𝑣4 also spans 𝑉.
steps: Assume v1, v2, v3, v4 spans V, so every vector in V is a linear combination of these four vectors.
To prove that the list v1 − v2, v2 − v3, v3 − v4, v4 spans V, it suffices to show that each of v1, v2, v3, v4 is in the span of the new list.
Note that v4 is already one of the vectors in the new list.
Since v3 − v4 is another vector in the list, we can express v3 as (v3 − v4) + v4, showing v3 belongs to the span of the new list.
Using the same idea, v2 can be written as (v2 − v3) + v3, and because v3 is in the span from the previous step, v2 is also in the span.
Finally, v1 = (v1 − v2) + v2, so v1 lies in the span because v2 does.
Therefore every vector in the original spanning list is a linear combination of v1 − v2, v2 − v3, v3 − v4, v4, hence these four vectors span V.

problem: Section 2C, Exercise 1: Show that the subspaces of 𝐑2 are precisely {0}, all lines in 𝐑2 containing the origin, and 𝐑2.
steps: Recall that a subspace of ℝ² must contain the zero vector and be closed under addition and scalar multiplication, so its dimension can only be 0, 1, or 2.
If the subspace has dimension 0 then it contains only the zero vector, so the only possibility is {0}.
If the subspace has dimension 2 then it coincides with ℝ² itself because any two linearly independent vectors span the whole plane.
The remaining possibility is dimension 1, meaning the subspace is spanned by a single nonzero vector v.
A span of a single vector v consists of all scalar multiples of v, so it is a line through the origin in ℝ².
Thus any one-dimensional subspace is a line through the origin, since closure under scalar multiplication forces the line to pass through the origin.
We have exhausted all possible dimensions of subspaces of ℝ², hence every subspace is either {0}, a line through the origin, or ℝ² itself.
Therefore the subspaces of ℝ² are precisely {0}, all lines containing the origin, and the entire space ℝ².

problem: Section 2C, Exercise 2: Show that the subspaces of 𝐑3 are precisely {0}, all lines in 𝐑3 containing the origin, all planes in 𝐑3 containing the origin, and 𝐑3.
steps: Let V be a subspace of ℝ^3; by definition V is closed under addition and scalar multiplication. Consider the dimension of V, which must be one of 0,1,2,3 since ℝ^3 is three-dimensional.
If dim V=0 then V contains only the zero vector, so V={0} which is one of the listed subspaces.
If dim V=1 then V is spanned by a single nonzero vector v, hence V consists of all scalar multiples of v and is a line through the origin in ℝ^3.
If dim V=2 then V has a basis of two linearly independent vectors, and therefore V is a plane through the origin consisting of all linear combinations of those basis vectors.
If dim V=3 then V equals ℝ^3 itself, since any basis of ℝ^3 spans the whole space.
Thus every subspace of ℝ^3 is either {0}, a line through the origin, a plane through the origin, or all of ℝ^3, showing the classification is complete.

problem: Section 3B, Exercise 2: Suppose 𝑆, 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉) are such that range 𝑆 ⊆ null 𝑇. Prove that (𝑆𝑇)2 = 0.
steps: We are given operators S, T on V with range(S) ⊆ null(T), so every vector of the form S(v) is sent to zero by T.
Thus for any vector y in range(S) we have T(y)=0, in particular T(S(z))=0 for every z ∈ V.
The square of ST is (ST)^2 = STST, so for arbitrary v we compute (ST)^2(v) = S(T(S(T(v)))).
Since T(v) is some vector in V, S(T(v)) lies in range(S), hence T(S(T(v))) = 0 by the range-null hypothesis.
Therefore (ST)^2(v) = S(0) = 0 for every v ∈ V, showing that (ST)^2 is the zero operator.
Hence (ST)^2 = 0 as required.

problem: Section 3B, Exercise 5: Give an example of 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝐑4 ) such that range 𝑇 = null 𝑇.
steps: We seek a linear operator T on R^4 whose image equals its kernel, so we first fix the subspace W = span{e1,e2} where e1,…,e4 is the standard basis, and aim for both range and null T to be W.
Define T on the basis vectors by setting T(e1)=0, T(e2)=0, T(e3)=e1, and T(e4)=e2, and extend linearly; this prescription defines a linear operator T∈L(R^4) because it is specified on a basis.
Because T sends every vector in W to 0, we immediately have W⊆null T, and because T sends e3,e4 to e1,e2 it is clear that T(R^4) contains W, so it looks like range T=W as well.

More precisely, for an arbitrary vector v = a e1 + b e2 + c e3 + d e4, we compute T(v) = c e1 + d e2, which lies in W, so range T is contained in W; combined with the previous observation, range T = W.
The same computation shows that T(v)=0 exactly when c=d=0, hence v lies in W, so null T = W as well.
Thus we have constructed T such that range T = null T = span{e1,e2}, satisfying the requested condition.

problem: Section 3B, Exercise 6: Prove that there does not exist 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝐑5 ) such that range 𝑇 = null 𝑇.
steps: Assume for contradiction that there exists a linear operator T on ℝ⁵ whose range equals its null space, and let d denote the common dimension of these subspaces.
Since the null space of T has dimension d, the rank-nullity theorem gives dim(range T) + dim(null T) = dim ℝ⁵, so the shared dimension d must satisfy d + d = 5.
The equation 2d = 5 forces d to be a half-integer, but dimensions of subspaces are integers, so no integer d satisfies this equation.
Therefore our assumption that such an operator T exists leads to an impossible dimension count, contradicting the rank-nullity theorem.
We conclude that there cannot exist T ∈ ℒ(ℝ⁵) with range T equal to null T.

problem: Section 5A, Exercise 1: Suppose 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉) and 𝑈 is a subspace of 𝑉. (a) Prove that if 𝑈 ⊆ null 𝑇, then 𝑈 is invariant under 𝑇. (b) Prove that if range 𝑇 ⊆ 𝑈, then 𝑈 is invariant under 𝑇.
steps: Recall that U is invariant under T when T(u) belongs to U for every u in U, so to establish invariance it suffices to show that T maps arbitrary elements of U back into U.
Assume U is a subset of null T, which means for each vector u in U we have T(u) = 0 by definition of the null space.
Since U is a subspace, it contains the zero vector, so T(u) = 0 belongs to U for every u in U.
Therefore T sends every element of U to a vector in U, and U is invariant under T in this case.
Now suppose range T is contained in U, so every vector of the form T(v) for v in V already lies in U.
Take an arbitrary u in U; since T(u) is by definition an element of range T, and range T lies in U, we conclude that T(u) belongs to U.
Thus with range T ⊆ U we also have T(U) ⊆ U, showing U is invariant under T in this case as well.

problem: Section 5A, Exercise 3: Suppose 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉). Prove that the intersection of every collection of subspaces of 𝑉 invariant under 𝑇 is invariant under 𝑇.
steps: Let C be the given collection of subspaces of V and set W=⋂{U∈C}U, so W consists of all vectors common to every subspace in the collection.
Choose an arbitrary vector v∈W; by definition v lies in each subspace U∈C because W⊆U for every such U.
Since each U∈C is invariant under T, applying T to v keeps us inside U: T(v)∈U for every U in the collection.
So T(v) belongs to every U∈C, which means T(v)∈⋂{U∈C}U=W.
Thus T sends every vector of W back into W, showing that W is invariant under T.
Therefore the intersection of the family of T-invariant subspaces is itself T-invariant, as required.

problem: Section 5A, Exercise 4: Prove or give a counterexample: If 𝑉 is finite-dimensional and 𝑈 is a sub- space of 𝑉 that is invariant under every operator on 𝑉, then 𝑈 = {0} or 𝑈 = 𝑉.
steps: Assume V is finite-dimensional and U is a subspace that is invariant under every linear operator on V.
Suppose for contradiction that U is neither {0} nor V; then there is a nonzero u in U and a vector v in V that is not in U.
Extend the singleton {u} to a basis {u, w2, ..., wn} of V, which is possible because V is finite-dimensional and u is nonzero.
Define a linear operator T on V by sending u to v and sending each remaining basis vector w2, ..., wn to the zero vector; this determines T uniquely on all of V by linearity.
By construction T(u)=v, so v belongs to T(U) but v is not in U, hence T does not map U into itself.
This contradicts the assumption that U is invariant under every operator, so the hypothesis that U is neither {0} nor V must be false.
Therefore U must equal {0} or V.

problem: Section 5B, Exercise 2: Suppose 𝑉 is a complex vector space and 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉) has no eigenvalues. Prove that every subspace of 𝑉 invariant under 𝑇 is either {0} or infinite- dimensional.
steps: Let W be a nonzero subspace of V that is invariant under T, and suppose for contradiction that W is finite-dimensional.

Since W is invariant, the restriction T|_W is a linear operator on the finite-dimensional space W, so by the fundamental theorem of finite-dimensional linear algebra it has a characteristic polynomial and therefore at least one eigenvalue over ℂ.
An eigenvalue λ of T|_W gives a vector w ≠ 0 in W with T(w) = λw, which also makes w an eigenvector of T because T agrees with its restriction on W.
This contradicts the assumption that T has no eigenvalues, so our supposition that a nonzero invariant W could be finite-dimensional must be false.
Hence every invariant subspace other than {0} must be infinite-dimensional.

problem: Section 6A, Exercise 4: Suppose T ∈ ℒ(V) is such that ‖Tv‖ ≤ ‖v‖ for every v ∈ V. Prove that T − √2 I is injective.
steps: We aim to show that T−√2 I is injective, which is equivalent to showing its kernel contains only the zero vector, so suppose (T−√2 I)v=0 for some v∈V.
The equation (T−√2 I)v=0 rearranges to T v=√2 v, so v is an eigenvector of T with eigenvalue √2 when it is nonzero.
Taking norms of both sides gives ‖T v‖=‖√2 v‖, and since √2 is a scalar we have ‖√2 v‖=√2‖v‖.
Now use the hypothesis that ‖T v‖≤‖v‖ for every v; combining that with the equality yields √2‖v‖=‖T v‖≤‖v‖.
Subtracting gives (√2−1)‖v‖≤0, but √2−1>0, so the only way this holds is if ‖v‖=0.
Hence v=0, which means the kernel of T−√2 I is {0}, proving T−√2 I is injective.

problem: Section 7B, Exercise 3: Suppose 𝐅 = 𝐂 and 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉) is normal. Prove that the set of eigenvalues of 𝑇 is contained in {0, 1} if and only if there is a subspace 𝑈 of 𝑉 such that 𝑇 = 𝑃𝑈.
steps: Suppose the spectrum of T lies in {0,1}; then the minimal polynomial of T divides x(x−1), so T satisfies the polynomial equation T^2 − T = 0 and hence is idempotent.

Normality ensures V admits an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors for T, and because the only eigenvalues are the real numbers 0 and 1, the adjoint T* acts on each eigenvector with the same eigenvalue, so T* = T; in other words, T is self-adjoint as well as idempotent.

For any vector v in Range T we have T v = v, and for any vector w in Ker T we have T w = 0; these two subspaces are orthogonal because T is self-adjoint, so V decomposes as Range T ⊕ Ker T with T acting as identity on Range T and zero on Ker T.
Thus T coincides with the orthogonal projection onto the subspace U = Range T, showing that T = P_U in this direction.
Conversely, if T = P_U for some subspace U, then by definition T^2 = T and T is self-adjoint, so T is normal and the space decomposes as U ⊕ U^⊥ with T acting as 1 on U and 0 on U^⊥.
Therefore every eigenvalue of T must be either 0 (from U^⊥) or 1 (from U), proving that the spectrum is contained in {0,1} in this direction as well.

problem: Section 7B, Exercise 4: Prove that a normal operator on a complex inner product space is skew (meaning it equals the negative of its adjoint) if and only if all its eigenvalues are purely imaginary (meaning that they have real part equal to 0).
steps: Let T be a normal operator on the complex inner product space, so T commutes with its adjoint and admits an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors, and say T is skew means T* = -T; we want to show this is equivalent to every eigenvalue having zero real part.
Assume T is skew; pick any eigenvector v and eigenvalue λ so that Tv = λv, with <v,v> ≠ 0, and compute <Tv,v> = λ<v,v> while <Tv,v> also equals <v,T*v> = <v,-T v> = -<v,λ v> = -λ̄<v,v>.
Equating those expressions gives λ<v,v> = -λ̄<v,v>, hence λ = -λ̄, which forces the real part of λ to vanish; since the choice of eigenvalue was arbitrary, all eigenvalues are purely imaginary.

Now suppose T is normal and all its eigenvalues λ_i are purely imaginary; by normality we can decompose T as T = ∑ λ_i P_i where the P_i are the orthogonal projections onto the eigenspaces of an orthonormal eigenbasis.
Because the projections P_i are self-adjoint and T* shares the same eigenspaces, T* = ∑ λ̄_i P_i holds, and since each λ_i is purely imaginary, λ̄_i = -λ_i.
Therefore T* = ∑ (-λ_i) P_i = -∑ λ_i P_i = -T, completing the equivalence.

problem: Section 8A, Exercise 1: Suppose 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉). Prove that if dim null 𝑇 4 = 8 and dim null 𝑇 6 = 9, then dim null 𝑇 𝑚 = 9 for all integers 𝑚 ≥ 5.
steps: Let W_k = null T^k for k ≥ 0; then the chain W_0 ⊆ W_1 ⊆ W_2 ⊆ ··· gives dim W_k ≤ dim W_{k+1} for every k, so each increment dim W_{k+1} − dim W_k is a nonnegative integer.

For each k, consider the linear map induced by T from the quotient W_{k+2}/W_{k+1} to W_{k+1}/W_k via v + W_{k+1} ↦ T v + W_k; the image lies in W_{k+1}/W_k because T(W_{k+2}) ⊆ W_{k+1} and T(W_{k+1}) ⊆ W_k.
If v ∈ W_{k+2} satisfies T v ∈ W_k then T^{k+1} v = 0, so v ∈ W_{k+1}; thus the above map has trivial kernel and hence is injective, which forces dim(W_{k+2}/W_{k+1}) ≤ dim(W_{k+1}/W_k).
Thus the sequence of increments dim W_{k+1} − dim W_k is nonincreasing; in particular dim W_6 − dim W_5 ≤ dim W_5 − dim W_4.

We are given dim W_4 = 8 and dim W_6 = 9, so dim W_6 − dim W_4 = (dim W_6 − dim W_5) + (dim W_5 − dim W_4) = 1; with nonincreasing increments the only possibility is dim W_5 − dim W_4 = 1 and dim W_6 − dim W_5 = 0.
Therefore dim W_5 = 9, and since the increment at 5 is zero, all subsequent increments are zero as well, so dim W_m = 9 for every m ≥ 5.
Thus dim null T^m = 9 for all integers m ≥ 5, as required.

problem: Section 8A, Exercise 2: Suppose 𝑇 ∈ ℒ(𝑉), 𝑚 is a positive integer, 𝑣 ∈ 𝑉, and 𝑇 𝑚−1 𝑣 ≠ 0 but 𝑇 𝑚 𝑣 = 0. Prove that 𝑣, 𝑇𝑣, 𝑇 2 𝑣, …, 𝑇 𝑚−1 𝑣 is linearly independent. The result in this exercise is used in the proof of 8.45.
steps: Assume for contradiction that a linear relation exists, so there are scalars a_0,…,a_{m-1} with a_0 v + a_1 T v + ⋯ + a_{m-1} T^{m-1} v = 0.
Apply T^{m-1} to both sides of this equation; since T is linear we get a_0 T^{m-1} v + a_1 T^{m} v + ⋯ + a_{m-1} T^{2m-2} v = 0.
Because T^{m} v = 0 the terms with T-power at least m vanish, leaving a_0 T^{m-1} v = 0.
Since T^{m-1} v ≠ 0, we conclude that a_0 = 0.
Now consider the original relation with the a_0 term gone; apply T^{m-2} to the remaining sum a_1 T v + ⋯ + a_{m-1} T^{m-1} v = 0.
Again, most terms vanish because any term involving T^{m} or higher applied to v gives zero, so we obtain a_1 T^{m-1} v = 0, and hence a_1 = 0.
Iterate this argument: at the k-th step consider the relation a_k T^k v + ⋯ + a_{m-1} T^{m-1} v = 0, apply T^{m-1-k}, and conclude a_k = 0 since T^{m-1} v ≠ 0.
After m steps every coefficient a_j is zero, so the assumed relation was trivial.
Therefore v, T v, …, T^{m-1} v are linearly independent.

problem: Section 1.6, Exercise 3: If φ: G → H is an isomorphism, prove that G is abelian if and only if H is abelian. If φ: G → H is a homomorphism, what additional conditions on φ (if any) are sufficient to ensure that if G is abelian, then so is H?
steps: Assume first that q: G → H is an isomorphism, so in particular q is a bijective homomorphism between the two groups.
If G is abelian let h₁ and h₂ be arbitrary elements of H; surjectivity of q guarantees the existence of g₁, g₂ in G with q(g₁) = h₁ and q(g₂) = h₂.
The homomorphism property gives q(g₁g₂) = q(g₁)q(g₂) = h₁h₂ and similarly q(g₂g₁) = h₂h₁.
Since G is abelian, g₁g₂ = g₂g₁, hence q(g₁g₂) = q(g₂g₁), which shows h₁h₂ = h₂h₁, proving H is abelian.

The converse follows by applying the same argument to q⁻¹: if H is abelian then any two elements of G have images in H that commute, so pulling back by the inverse homomorphism yields commutativity in G.

Now suppose q is merely a homomorphism; the image q(G) is a subgroup of H and the relation q(xy) = q(x)q(y) shows that commutativity in G forces commutativity in q(G), so q(G) is abelian whenever G is abelian.
Thus to ensure H itself is abelian it suffices to know that q is surjective, since then H = q(G) and inherits the abelian property from G.

problem: Section 2.1, Exercise 6: Let G be an abelian group. Prove that {g ∈ G | |g| < ∞} is a subgroup of G (called the torsion subgroup of G). Give an explicit example where this set is not a subgroup when G is non-abelian.
steps: Let T be the set {g in G | |g| < ∞}; since G is abelian we will check that T satisfies the subgroup criteria by exhibiting identity, closure under products, and inverses.
The identity e ∈ G satisfies e^1 = e, so |e| = 1 < ∞ and hence e ∈ T, ensuring T is nonempty.
Take arbitrary g, h ∈ T with finite orders m and n respectively, so g^m = e and h^n = e, and use the abelian hypothesis to manipulate powers of products.
Compute (gh)^{mn} = g^{mn}h^{mn} = (g^m)^n(h^n)^m = e^n e^m = e, showing gh has finite order and thus belongs to T, so T is closed under multiplication.
For a finite order element g ∈ T with g^m = e, its inverse satisfies (g^{-1})^m = (g^m)^{-1} = e^{-1} = e, so g^{-1} ∈ T as well.
Having verified that T contains the identity, is closed under products, and contains inverses, we conclude T is a subgroup of G when G is abelian.
To see that the abelian hypothesis is essential, consider the infinite dihedral group D∞ = ⟨r, s | s^2 = e, srs = r^{-1}⟩, which is non-abelian and has finite-order reflections.

In D∞ the reflections s and sr both have order 2, yet their product (sr)s = r is the infinite-order rotation, so the set of torsion elements fails to be closed and hence is not a subgroup in the non-abelian case.

problem: Section 2.3, Exercise 2: If x is an element of the finite group G and |x| = |G|, prove that G = ⟨x⟩. Give an explicit example to show that this result need not be true if G is an infinite group.
steps: Let G be a finite group and suppose x is an element whose cyclic subgroup ⟨x⟩ satisfies |⟨x⟩| = |G|; the goal is to deduce that ⟨x⟩ = G.
Lagrange’s Theorem says that the order of any subgroup divides the order of the finite group containing it, so here |⟨x⟩| divides |G|.
Since the two orders are equal, the index of ⟨x⟩ in G is |G|/|⟨x⟩| = 1, hence ⟨x⟩ is the whole group G.
Therefore G = ⟨x⟩ as required.
To show the result can fail in infinite groups, take G = ℤ under addition and x = 2, so ⟨x⟩ = 2ℤ is the subgroup of even integers.
Both G and ⟨x⟩ are countably infinite, so |⟨x⟩| = |G| despite ⟨x⟩ being a proper subgroup of ℤ.
This example shows the finite-group hypothesis is essential, since in the infinite case we can have equal cardinalities without equality of the groups.

problem: Section 2.4, Exercise 5: Prove that the subgroup generated by any two distinct elements of order 2 in S3 is all of S3.
steps: Observe that S3 has order 6 and is generated by a transposition and a 3-cycle; recall that it contains three elements of order 2, namely the transpositions (12), (13), and (23), each of which is its own inverse.
Let σ and τ be two distinct elements of order 2 in S3, so they are two different transpositions; then the subgroup H = ⟨σ, τ⟩ contains both σ and τ and hence their product στ.

Compute the product of two distinct transpositions: since σ and τ are transpositions with disjoint or overlapping supports, their composition is a 3-cycle, e.g. (12)(13) = (132) up to order, so στ has order 3 and is not the identity.

Using the fact that H contains both σ and τ and also στ, we see that H contains σ(στ) = τ (which is already known) and (στ)σ, so in particular H contains the 3-cycle and its inverse (since (στ)^2 = σ and (στ)^3 = e), thus H contains a 3-cycle and all its powers.

Now H has at least one order 2 element σ, another order 2 element τ, and the order 3 element στ; together these elements already account for six distinct elements, namely e, σ, τ, στ, (στ)^2, and the remaining transposition obtained from the product of τ and στ, so H has order 6.
Since S3 is the unique group of order 6 up to isomorphism, and H is a subgroup of S3 of order 6, it follows that H = S3.

problem: Section 3.1, Exercise 1: Let φ: G → H be a homomorphism and let E be a subgroup of H. Prove that φ^-1(E) ≤ G (i.e., the preimage or pullback of a subgroup under a homomorphism is a subgroup). If E ◁ H prove that φ^-1(E) ◁ G. Deduce that ker φ ◁ G.
steps: Let φ: G → H be a homomorphism and let E be a subgroup of H; define φ⁻¹(E) = { g ∈ G | φ(g) ∈ E } and observe that e_G ∈ φ⁻¹(E) because φ(e_G) = e_H and e_H ∈ E, so the preimage is nonempty.
To verify subgroup closure, take g₁, g₂ ∈ φ⁻¹(E); then φ(g₁g₂^{-1}) = φ(g₁)φ(g₂)^{-1} and since E is a subgroup this product lies in E, so g₁g₂^{-1} ∈ φ⁻¹(E).
Hence φ⁻¹(E) is closed under taking products and inverses, which shows φ⁻¹(E) is itself a subgroup of G.
Now assume E is normal in H; for arbitrary g ∈ G and x ∈ φ⁻¹(E) we compute φ(gxg^{-1}) = φ(g)φ(x)φ(g)^{-1}, and because φ(x) ∈ E and E is normal, this conjugation stays in E.
Therefore gxg^{-1} ∈ φ⁻¹(E) for every g ∈ G and x ∈ φ⁻¹(E), showing that φ⁻¹(E) is normal in G whenever E ⫴ H.
In particular, taking E = {e_H}, which is normal in H, gives φ⁻¹({e_H}) = ker φ ≼ G, so the kernel of a homomorphism is a subgroup (and actually normal) in G.

problem: Section 3.1, Exercise 5: Use the preceding exercise to prove that the order of the element gN in G/N is n, where n is the smallest positive integer such that g^n ∈ N (and gN has infinite order if no such positive integer exists). Give an example to show that the order of gN in G/N may be strictly smaller than the order of g in G.
steps: Let N be a normal subgroup of G and consider the quotient G/N; the order of gN in G/N is by definition the smallest positive integer k for which (gN)^k equals the identity coset N.
Using the preceding exercise, we know (gN)^k = g^kN for each positive integer k, so the condition (gN)^k = N is equivalent to g^k belonging to N.
Therefore the least positive k with (gN)^k = N is the same as the least positive n such that g^n ∈ N, and if there is no such n, then (gN)^k ≠ N for every k so gN has infinite order.
This proves that the order of gN in G/N is exactly the smallest positive n with g^n ∈ N, with infinite order otherwise.
To illustrate that the order of gN can be strictly smaller than the order of g, take G = ℤ under addition, N = 4ℤ, and g = 2.
Then g has infinite order in ℤ, but gN = 2 + 4ℤ satisfies 2(2 + 4ℤ) = 4 + 4ℤ = 0 + 4ℤ, so the order of gN is 2 in ℤ/N, showing the quotient order can be smaller.

problem: Section 1.7, Exercise 4: Let G be a group acting on a set A and fix some a ∈ A. Show that the following sets are subgroups of G (cf. Exercise 26 of Section 1): (a) the kernel of the action, (b) {g ∈ G | ga = a} - this subgroup is called the stabilizer of a in G.
steps: Let K denote the kernel of the action, namely the set of g∈G such that g⋅b=b for every b∈A; since e⋅b=b for all b, the identity lies in K.
If g and h are in K, then for every b∈A we have (gh)⋅b=g⋅(h⋅b)=g⋅b= b, so gh fixes all elements of A and therefore belongs to K.
For g∈K the equation g⋅b=b for all b implies g^{-1}⋅b=g^{-1}⋅(g⋅b)=(g^{-1}g)⋅b=e⋅b=b, so g^{-1} is also in K, establishing that K is a subgroup.
Now let G_a denote the stabilizer of a, i.e. the set of g∈G with g⋅a=a; again e⋅a=a shows the identity lies in G_a.
If g and h are in G_a then (gh)⋅a=g⋅(h⋅a)=g⋅a=a, so the product gh fixes a and is in G_a, showing closure under the group operation.
If g⋅a=a then applying g^{-1} to this equality gives a=g^{-1}⋅(g⋅a)=(g^{-1}g)⋅a=e⋅a=a, hence g^{-1} also stabilizes a and lies in G_a.
Thus the stabilizer G_a satisfies the subgroup criteria and both the kernel of the action and the stabilizer of a are subgroups of G.

problem: Section 4.1, Exercise 1: Let G act on the set A. Prove that if a, b ∈ A and b = g · a for some g ∈ G, then G_b = gG_a g^{-1} (G_a is the stabilizer of a). Deduce that if G acts transitively on A then the kernel of the action is ⋂_{g ∈ G} gG_a g^{-1}.
steps: Let Ga denote {h in G | h·a = a}; similarly Gb = {h | h·b = b} when b = g·a.
If h ∈ Gb then h·b = b, so h·(g·a) = g·a and thus (g^{-1}hg)·a = a, meaning g^{-1}hg ∈ Ga and hence h ∈ gGa g^{−1}.
Conversely, if h = gk g^{−1} with k ∈ Ga then h·b = gk g^{−1}(g·a) = gk·a = g·a = b, so h ∈ Gb; this completes the equality Gb = gGa g^{−1}.
