The kernel of the action is the intersection of all point stabilizers, K = ⋂_{x∈A} Gx, and transitivity implies any point stabilizer is conjugate to Ga.
From Gb = gGa g^{−1} we see the kernel equals ⋂_{g∈G} gGa g^{−1}, since every point stabilizer arises as such a conjugate under transitivity.
Thus the kernel is the normal core of Ga, i.e. the intersection of all conjugates gGa g^{−1}, as required.

problem: Section 4.5, Exercise 3: Use Sylow's Theorem to prove Cauchy's Theorem. (Note that we only used Cauchy's Theorem for abelian groups - Proposition 3.21 - in the proof of Sylow's Theorem so this line of reasoning is not circular.)
steps: Let G be a finite group whose order is divisible by the prime p, and write |G| = p^k m where k ≥ 1 and p ∤ m; we want to show G has an element of order p.
Apply Sylow’s Theorem to find at least one Sylow p-subgroup P of order p^k in G; by definition, P is a subgroup of order the highest power of p dividing |G|.
Since P is a p-group, the possible orders of its elements are powers of p, and in particular the center Z(P) is nontrivial, so there must be an element x in Z(P) of order p.
The element x lies in P and hence in G, so G contains an element of order p, which is precisely what Cauchy’s Theorem asserts.
Thus every finite group whose order is divisible by p has an element of order p, completing the proof using Sylow’s existence result.

problem: Section 5.1, Exercise 1: Show that the center of a direct product is the direct product of the centers: Z(G_1 × G_2 × ··· × G_n) = Z(G_1) × Z(G_2) × ··· × Z(G_n). Deduce that a direct product of groups is abelian if and only if each of the factors is abelian.
steps: Let G=G₁×G₂×⋯×Gₙ denote the direct product, and observe that an element lies in Z(G) exactly when it commutes with every element of G.
Write a generic element g=(g₁,…,gₙ) in G and another element h=(h₁,…,hₙ); then gh=(g₁h₁,…,gₙhₙ) and hg=(h₁g₁,…,hₙgₙ).
Conclude that g commutes with every h precisely when each coordinate gᵢ commutes with all elements of Gᵢ, so gᵢ∈Z(Gᵢ) for every i.

This shows Z(G)⊆Z(G₁)×⋯×Z(Gₙ), and the reverse inclusion holds because if every coordinate lies in the respective center then the whole tuple commutes with every element of G by coordinatewise multiplication.
Hence Z(G)=Z(G₁)×⋯×Z(Gₙ).
For the abelianity claim, note that G is abelian if and only if Z(G)=G, so the equality of centers implies G=G₁×⋯×Gₙ is abelian exactly when each Gᵢ=Z(Gᵢ), i.e., each factor is abelian.

problem: Section 7.1, Exercise 4: Prove that the intersection of any nonempty collection of subrings of a ring is also a subring.
steps: Let {R_i}_{i ∈ I} be a nonempty collection of subrings of a ring R, and set S = ⋂_{i ∈ I} R_i; we will check that S satisfies the subring criteria.
Since each R_i contains the additive identity 0 of R, we have 0 ∈ R_i for all i, hence 0 belongs to S; thus S is nonempty.
Pick arbitrary elements a, b ∈ S, which means a, b ∈ R_i for every i, because S is a subset of each R_i.
Each R_i is closed under subtraction, so a - b ∈ R_i for every i, which implies a - b ∈ S, showing S is closed under subtraction.
Also each R_i is closed under multiplication, so a b ∈ R_i for each i and therefore ab ∈ S, showing closure under multiplication.
We have now verified that S is nonempty and closed under subtraction and multiplication, so S is a subring of R by the standard subring test.

problem: Section 7.4, Exercise 4: Assume R is commutative. Prove that R is a field if and only if 0 is a maximal ideal.
steps: Recall that an ideal I of commutative ring R is maximal if I ⊂ R and there is no intermediate ideal between I and R, and R is a field exactly when its only ideals are 0 and R.

Assume R is a field; take any ideal I with 0 ⊆ I ⊂ R. Since R is a field, every nonzero element is invertible, so any ideal containing a nonzero element must be all of R, hence the only proper ideal is 0 itself.
Thus 0 is indeed the unique proper ideal of R, and there is no ideal lying strictly between 0 and R, so 0 is maximal by definition.
For the converse, suppose 0 is a maximal ideal in R. The quotient ring R/0 is isomorphic to R itself, but it is also a field whenever the ideal we quotient by is maximal.
Since 0 is maximal and commutativity holds, R/0 is a field, and the natural isomorphism R ≅ R/0 shows that R inherits this field structure.
Therefore R is a field, completing the equivalence.

problem: Section 7.4, Exercise 6: Prove that R is a division ring if and only if its only left ideals are (0) and R. (The analogous result holds when "left" is replaced by "right".)
steps: Recall that a division ring is a ring with 1 in which every nonzero element has a two-sided multiplicative inverse, and a left ideal is an additive subgroup L of R such that rL⊆L for every r∈R; we want to show that the only left ideals are (0) and R exactly when R is a division ring.
Suppose R is a division ring and let L be any left ideal; if L≠{0} pick a nonzero a∈L, so a has an inverse a^{-1} in R.
Then for any r∈R we can write r=(r a^{-1})a, and since a∈L and L is a left ideal we have (r a^{-1})a∈L, so r∈L; thus L=R, showing the only left ideals are (0) and R when R is a division ring.
Conversely assume the only left ideals are (0) and R, and take a nonzero element x∈R; the left ideal Rx is then nonzero, so Rx=R and there exists r∈R with rx=1.

To prove x is also a right unit, consider the left ideal generated by xr−1: if xr−1≠0 then R(xr−1)=R, so there exists s such that s(xr−1)=1; multiplying this equation on the right by x gives s(xr−1)x=s(xr x−x)=s(x−x)=0, but the left-hand side is 1·x=x, forcing x=0, contradiction; hence xr−1=0 and so xr=1 as well.
Hence every nonzero element of R has both a left and a right inverse, so the nonzero elements form a group under multiplication and R is a division ring, completing the equivalence.

problem: Section 8.1, Exercise 4: Let R be a Euclidean Domain. (a) Prove that if (a, b) = 1 and a divides bc, then a divides c. More generally, show that if a divides bc with nonzero a, b then a/(a, b) divides c. (b) Consider the Diophantine equation ax + by = N where a, b and N are integers and a, b are nonzero. Suppose x0, y0 is a solution: ax0 + by0 = N. Prove that the full set of solutions to this equation is given by x = x0 + m·b/(a, b), y = y0 - m·a/(a, b) as m ranges over the integers. [If x, y is a solution to ax + by = N, show that a(x - x0) = b(y0 - y) and use (a).]
steps: Let a and b be nonzero elements of the Euclidean domain R and write d = (a,b); since R is Euclidean it is a PID so d can be expressed as ua + vb for some u,v in R.
If furthermore d = 1 and a divides bc, then multiplying the equation 1 = ua + vb by c shows c = uac + vbc, so a divides both terms on the right-hand side and hence divides c itself.

For the general case, write a = d a′ and b = d b′ with gcd(a′, b′) = 1; if a divides bc, then setting bc = a t gives d b′ c = d a′ t, so b′ c = a′ t, and by the coprimality from the previous step a′ divides c.
Thus a/d = a′ divides c, which completes the proof of the general divisibility claim that a/(a,b) divides c whenever a divides bc.

Now turn to the Diophantine equation ax + by = N and suppose (x0, y0) is a particular solution; subtracting the equations ax + by = N and ax0 + by0 = N yields a(x − x0) + b(y − y0) = 0, i.e. a(x − x0) = b(y0 − y).
Setting d = (a,b) again, we see that a(x − x0) is divisible by b, so by part (a) a/d divides y0 − y; hence there exists an integer m such that y0 − y = (a/d) m.
Substituting back into a(x − x0) = b(y0 − y) gives a(x − x0) = b(a/d) m, whence x − x0 = (b/d) m, and we conclude that the full solution set is x = x0 + (b/d) m, y = y0 − (a/d) m for m ∈ ℤ.

problem: Section 8.2, Exercise 1: Prove that in a Principal Ideal Domain two ideals (a) and (b) are comaximal (cf. Section 7.6) if and only if a greatest common divisor of a and b is 1 (in which case a and b are said to be coprime or relatively prime).
steps: Work inside a principal ideal domain R, so every ideal is generated by a single element, and gcds exist up to associates.
Suppose a and b have greatest common divisor 1, so there is an element d with d | a, d | b, and any other common divisor is a unit multiple of d; in particular d=1 up to a unit.
Since d=1 in the gcd sense, we can find elements x and y in R with ax+by=1, by the usual Bezout identity valid in a PID.
But ax+by=1 shows that 1 ∈ (a)+(b), so the sum ideal is R, proving that (a) and (b) are comaximal in this direction.
Conversely, assume the ideals (a) and (b) are comaximal, so (a)+(b)=R, hence there exist s,t ∈ R with sa+tb=1.
Now any common divisor of a and b also divides every R-linear combination, so it divides 1, showing that the only common divisors are units.
Therefore the gcd of a and b is a unit, which means it is 1 in the sense of gcds up to associates, so a and b are coprime.

problem: Section 9.2, Exercise 1: Let f(x) ∈ F[x] be a polynomial of degree n ≥ 1 and let bars denote passage to the quotient F[x]/(f(x)). Prove that for each g(x) there is a unique polynomial g0(x) of degree ≤ n - 1 such that g(x) = g0(x) in F[x]/(f(x)) (equivalently, the elements 1, x, ..., x^(n-1) are a basis of the vector space F[x]/(f(x)) over F - in particular, the dimension of this space is n). [Use the Division Algorithm.]
steps: By the Division Algorithm in F[x], given any g(x) there exist unique polynomials q(x), r(x) with either r=0 or deg r < deg f = n such that g(x)=q(x)f(x)+r(x).
Passing to the quotient F[x]/(f(x)), the equality becomes ḡ(x)=q̄(x)f̄(x)+r̄(x); but f̄(x)=0 since f(x)∈(f(x)), so ḡ(x)=r̄(x).
Therefore every coset ḡ(x) has a representative r(x) whose degree is at most n-1; we can take g₀(x)=r(x) with deg g₀<n.
This shows existence of a degree <n representative for each coset in the quotient.
To see uniqueness, suppose ḡ(x)=h̄(x) with deg h<n, then g(x)-h(x)∈(f(x)), so f divides g-h, but deg(g-h)<n unless g=h, hence g=h.
Thus the representative of degree <n is unique, and the cosets of 1,x,…,xⁿ⁻¹ span F[x]/(f(x)).
Since these n elements are linearly independent modulo f(x) by the uniqueness just shown, they form a basis and the dimension of F[x]/(f(x)) is n.

problem: Section 9.2, Exercise 2: Let F be a finite field of order q and let f(x) be a polynomial in F[x] of degree n ≥ 1. Prove that F[x]/(f(x)) has q^n elements. [Use the preceding exercise.]
steps: Recall from the preceding exercise that for any nonzero polynomial f(x) in F[x], polynomial division by f(x) shows every g(x) in F[x] can be written as g(x)=q(x)f(x)+r(x) with r(x)=0 or deg r<deg f, and such a remainder r(x) is uniquely determined by g(x).

In the quotient ring F[x]/(f(x)), two polynomials are in the same coset precisely when their difference is divisible by f(x), so the division algorithm ensures each coset contains exactly one polynomial of degree less than deg f.
Let n=deg f, so the permissible remainders are polynomials of degree at most n-1; denote by S the set of all polynomials in F[x] with degree less than n.
Because each coefficient of a polynomial in S can be chosen independently from F and there are n coefficients (including the constant term up to x^{n-1}), the set S has |F|^n=q^n elements.

Define the map φ:S→F[x]/(f(x)) by sending r(x) to the coset r(x)+(f(x)); this map is well defined and surjective because every coset has a representative in S, and it is injective because the remainder representing a coset is unique.
Thus φ is a bijection, so F[x]/(f(x)) has the same number of elements as S, namely q^n.

problem: Section 9.2, Exercise 3: Let f(x) be a polynomial in F[x]. Prove that F[x]j(f(x)) is a field if and only if f(x) is irreducible. [Use Proposition 7, Section 8.2.]
steps: Let I = (f(x)) be the ideal generated by f(x) in F[x]. If F[x]/I is a field, then I must be maximal because rescaling by a nonzero coset gives inverses for every nonzero element.
Since F[x] is a principal ideal domain, the containment relations among ideals correspond to divisibility among generators, so maximality of I places a strong restriction on f(x).

Assume for contradiction that f(x) is reducible, say f(x) = g(x)h(x) with both g and h of positive degree. Then (f) is properly contained in (g) because g divides f but is not a unit, contradicting the maximality of (f).
Therefore the maximality of (f) forces f(x) to be irreducible.

Conversely, suppose f(x) is irreducible. Take any ideal J with (f) ⊂ J ⊂ F[x]; since each ideal is principal we have J = (g) for some g dividing f, and irreducibility means g is either a unit or a unit multiple of f.
Thus the only ideals containing (f) are (f) itself and the whole ring, so (f) is maximal.
Hence F[x]/(f) is a field, completing the equivalence.

problem: Section 9.4, Exercise 5: Find all the monic irreducible polynomials of degree ≤ 3 in F2[x], and the same in F3[x].
steps: We tackle the two base fields in turn, remembering that a monic polynomial of degree at most 3 is irreducible exactly when it does not factor into lower-degree monic factors, so for degree 1 it must be linear, for degree 2 it must have no root in the field, and for degree 3 it must have no linear factor at all.
Over F2 the degree-1 monic polynomials are simply x and x+1, and both are irreducible because they cannot be factored any further.

Any monic quadratic over F2 is x^2+ax+b; the only one with no root in F2 is x^2+x+1, since evaluating at 0 and 1 gives 1 and 1 respectively, so it cannot factor into linear factors and is therefore irreducible.

The monic cubics are x^3+x^2+x+1, x^3+x^2+1, x^3+x+1 and x^3+1 (and the ones with fewer terms), and the only ones without a root in F2 are x^3+x+1 and x^3+x^2+1; checking that plugging in 0 and 1 gives nonzero results shows there is no linear factor, so they are irreducible.
Moving to F3, the degree-1 monic polynomials are x, x-1, and x-2, which are automatically irreducible because they are linear.

For degree 2 we can check the discriminant criterion: a monic quadratic x^2+ax+b over F3 is irreducible iff a^2−4b is not a square in F3, and since the squares are 0,1 the only choices giving 2 mod 3 are x^2+1, x^2+x+2, and x^2+2x+2, so these are the only irreducible quadratics.

For degree 3 we impose that c≠0 so the polynomial does not vanish at x=0 and then require that the polynomials x^3+ax^2+bx+c have no root at x=1 or x=2; writing the evaluations gives the conditions a+b+c+1≠0 and a+2b+c+2≠0, which we check for all a,b∈{0,1,2} and c∈{1,2}.

Solving these congruences produces exactly eight triples (a,b,c), so the eight monic irreducible cubics over F3 are x^3+2x+1, x^3+x^2+2x+1, x^3+2x^2+1, x^3+2x^2+x+1, x^3+2x+2, x^3+x^2+2, x^3+x^2+x+2, and x^3+2x^2+2x+2, each of which has no root and hence no linear factor.

problem: Section 9.4, Exercise 7: Prove that ℝ[x]/(x^2 + 1) is a field which is isomorphic to the complex numbers.
steps: Work in the ring R[x] of polynomials with real coefficients and consider the ideal generated by x^2 + 1; this ideal is maximal because x^2 + 1 has no real roots so it is irreducible over R, hence R[x]/(x^2+1) is a field.
The residue class of x in the quotient, denoted [x], satisfies the relation [x]^2 = -1 because x^2 + 1 lies in the ideal, so [x] behaves exactly like the imaginary unit i in C.

Every element of R[x]/(x^2+1) can be represented by a linear polynomial a + bx with a, b in R, since higher powers of x reduce via x^2 = -1 to a linear form; thus the quotient is a two-dimensional real vector space with basis {1, [x]}.
Define a map φ: R[x]/(x^2+1) → C by sending the class of a + bx to a + bi; this map respects addition because it is linear over R and the operations on coefficients match.

Verify that φ preserves multiplication: the product in the quotient uses the relation [x]^2 = -1, which agrees with i^2 = -1 in C, so multiplication of representatives corresponds directly to complex multiplication.
Since every complex number has a unique preimage of form a + bx, φ is bijective, and because it respects ring operations it is an isomorphism, proving R[x]/(x^2+1) ≅ C.

problem: Section 1.6, Exercise 4: Prove that the multiplicative groups ℝ - {0} and ℂ - {0} are not isomorphic.
steps: Assume for contradiction that there is a group isomorphism φ: R − {0} → C − {0} between the multiplicative groups of nonzero reals and nonzero complex numbers.
Recall that every isomorphism preserves orders of elements, so if g ∈ R − {0} has finite order n then φ(g) has the same order n in C − {0}, and vice versa via φ^{-1}.
In C − {0} there is a concrete element of finite order greater than 2, namely i, since i^4 = 1 but i^2 = −1 ≠ 1 and i ≠ 1.
Let a ∈ R − {0} be the pre-image φ^{-1}(i); then a must also have order 4 because φ preserves orders.
Therefore a satisfies a^4 = 1 in the reals; the only real solutions to x^4 = 1 are x = 1 and x = −1.
But 1 has order 1 and −1 has order 2 in R − {0}, so neither has order 4, contradicting the existence of a real element of order 4.
This contradiction shows that no such isomorphism φ can exist, hence R − {0} and C − {0} are not isomorphic as groups.

problem: Section 1.6, Exercise 5: Prove that the additive groups ℝ and ℚ are not isomorphic.
steps: Assume toward contradiction that there is a group isomorphism φ: (ℝ,+) → (ℚ,+).
Because φ is an isomorphism it is bijective, so the cardinality of ℝ must equal the cardinality of ℚ.
Recall that ℚ is countable: every rational number can be written as a reduced fraction m/n with m ∈ ℤ and n ∈ ℕ, and ℤ × ℕ is countable, so ℚ inherits that countability.
On the other hand ℝ is uncountable, which can be seen by Cantor’s diagonal argument applied to decimal (or binary) expansions of real numbers in [0,1], so there is no bijection from ℤ to ℝ.
This contradicts the bijectivity forced by φ, so no such isomorphism exists and hence (ℝ,+) and (ℚ,+) are not isomorphic.

problem: Section 2.3, Exercise 3: Find all generators for Z/48Z.
steps: Recall that Z/48Z is a cyclic group of order 48 under addition, and an element [a] is a generator precisely when its order is 48.
The order of [a] in the additive group is 48/gcd(a,48), so [a] has full order 48 if and only if gcd(a,48)=1.
Therefore the generators are exactly the residues mod 48 that are relatively prime to 48.
Factor 48=16·3, so a residue is coprime to 48 exactly when it is odd and not divisible by 3.
List the odd residues mod 48 and discard the ones divisible by 3 to get: 1, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, 25, 29, 31, 35, 37, 41, 43, 47.
Conclude that these sixteen classes are all the generators of Z/48Z since each satisfies gcd(a,48)=1 and therefore has order 48.

problem: Section 2.3, Exercise 4: Find all generators for Z/202Z.
steps: Z/202Z is a cyclic group under addition generated by [1], so every element can be written as [k] for some integer k and the order of [k] equals 202 divided by gcd(k, 202).
Thus [k] generates the whole group if and only if gcd(k, 202) = 1, because that forces ord([k]) = 202.
Factor 202 = 2 * 101, with 101 prime, hence the only prime divisors of 202 are 2 and 101.
Therefore gcd(k, 202) = 1 precisely when k is not divisible by 2 and not divisible by 101, i.e., k is an odd integer not divisible by 101.
The generators of Z/202Z are the residue classes [k] with 1 ≤ k ≤ 201 satisfying those divisibility conditions, which gives φ(202) = 100 such classes.
In other words, the generators are the classes [k] where k is any integer congruent mod 202 to an odd number that is not congruent to 0 mod 101.

problem: Section 2.4, Exercise 1: Prove that if H is a subgroup of G then ⟨H⟩ = H.
steps: Let ⟨H⟩ denote the subgroup generated by H; by definition ⟨H⟩ is the intersection of all subgroups of G that contain H.
Since every subgroup containing H must also contain all the elements of H, the intersection ⟨H⟩ contains H, so H ⊆ ⟨H⟩.
Now, because H itself is assumed to be a subgroup of G that contains H, it is one of the subgroups appearing in the intersection defining ⟨H⟩.
Any intersection of subgroups is contained in each of the subgroups being intersected, so ⟨H⟩ ⊆ H because H is one such subgroup.
Combining the two inclusions H ⊆ ⟨H⟩ and ⟨H⟩ ⊆ H we conclude ⟨H⟩ = H, as required.

problem: Section 3.1, Exercise 2: Let φ: G → H be a homomorphism of groups with kernel K and let a, b ∈ φ(G). Let X be the fiber above a and let Y be the fiber above b, i.e., X = φ^-1(a), Y = φ^-1(b). Fix an element u of X (so φ(u) = a). Prove that if XY = Z in the quotient group G/K and w is any member of Z, then there is some v ∈ Y such that uv = w. [Show u^-1 w ∈ Y.]
steps: Because rp is a homomorphism and u lies in the fiber X = rp^{-1}(a), the fiber is a coset of the kernel: X = uK, since every element of X differs from u by an element of K.
Similarly, pick an element y0 in Y = rp^{-1}(b); then Y = y0K, because all fibers of rp are cosets of K.
In the quotient group G/K the product XY equals Z, and since X = uK and Y = y0K this means Z = (uK)(y0K) = (uy0)K.
Now take an arbitrary element w of Z; because Z = (uy0)K we have w = uy0 k for some k in K.
Set v = y0 k; then v belongs to y0K = Y, and because k lies in K one has u^{-1} w = y0 k = v, showing u^{-1} w lies in Y.
Finally u v = u(y0 k) = uy0 k = w, as required, so any w in Z arises by multiplying u by some v in Y.

problem: Section 3.1, Exercise 3: Let A be an abelian group and let B be a subgroup of A. Prove that A 1 B is abelian. Give an example of a non-abelian group G containing a proper normal subgroup N such that GIN is abelian.
steps: Let A be abelian and let B be a subgroup; to show A/B is abelian it suffices to check that coset multiplication is commutative.
Recall that in the quotient group the product of cosets is given by (a+B)(a'+B)=(a+a')+B, which is well defined because B is a subgroup.
Since A is abelian we have a+a'=a'+a for any a,a′ in A, so (a+a')+B=(a'+a)+B.
Thus (a+B)(a'+B)=(a'+B)(a+B) for every pair of cosets, showing that A/B is abelian.
For an example of a non-abelian group with an abelian quotient, take G=S3 (the permutations of three objects), which is non-abelian because it has non-commuting transpositions.
Let N=A3 be the subgroup of even permutations; N is normal in S3 because it has index 2 and consists of the identity plus the 3-cycles.
The quotient S3/N has order 2 and hence is cyclic, so S3/N is abelian despite S3 itself being non-abelian.

problem: Section 1.7, Exercise 2: Show that the additive group Z acts on itself by z · a = z + a for all z, a ∈ Z.
steps: We are asked to verify that the additive group Z acts on itself via the rule z·a = z + a for all z, a ∈ Z, so we must check the two axioms of a group action: identity acting trivially and compatibility with the group operation.
The identity element of Z under addition is 0, so compute 0·a = 0 + a = a; this shows the identity of Z leaves every element of Z unchanged.
Next we must show that for any z, z' ∈ Z and any a ∈ Z, we have (z + z')·a = z·(z'·a).
Compute (z + z')·a using the given rule: (z + z')·a = (z + z') + a = z + (z' + a) by associativity of addition in Z.
On the other hand, first applying z' gives z'·a = z' + a, and then applying z gives z·(z'·a) = z + (z' + a), so the two expressions agree.
Thus the compatibility condition holds, and we have checked both axioms.
Therefore the rule z·a = z + a defines a valid action of the additive group Z on itself.

problem: Section 1.7, Exercise 3: Show that the additive group ℝ acts on the x, y-plane ℝ × ℝ by r · (x, y) = (x + ry, y).
steps: We are given the additive group IR and a prescription r · (x, y) := (x + ry, y) for each r ∈ IR and (x, y) ∈ IR × IR, so the first task is to check that this rule certainly lands back in IR × IR for every choice of r, x, y, which is obvious because addition and multiplication of real numbers stay in IR.

Next we verify the identity axiom: taking the identity element 0 in the additive group IR and applying it to an arbitrary point (x, y) gives 0 · (x, y) = (x + 0·y, y) = (x, y), so the identity acts trivially.
To check the compatibility axiom we compute (r + s) · (x, y) = (x + (r + s)y, y) = (x + ry + sy, y).
We also compute s · (x, y) = (x + sy, y) and then apply r to the result, giving r · (s · (x, y)) = r · (x + sy, y) = ((x + sy) + r·y, y) = (x + sy + ry, y), which agrees with the previous expression.
Since addition of real numbers is commutative the two expressions coincide, so (r + s) · (x, y) = r · (s · (x, y)) for all r, s ∈ IR and (x, y) ∈ IR × IR.
With both axioms verified, the rule r · (x, y) = (x + ry, y) defines a bona fide action of the additive group IR on the plane IR × IR.

problem: Section 4.1, Exercise 2: Let G be a permutation group on the set A (i.e., G ≤ S_A), let σ ∈ G and let a ∈ A. Prove that σG_aσ^{-1} = G_{σ(a)}. Deduce that if G acts transitively on A then ⋂_{σ ∈ G} σG_aσ^{-1} = 1.
steps: Let G act on A by permutations, and for α∈A denote by G_α the stabilizer {g∈G:g·α=α}; we aim to prove gG_α g^{-1}=G_{g·α} for every g∈G.
If h belongs to gG_α g^{-1}, then h=gkg^{-1} with k∈G_α, so h(g·α)=gk·α=g·α because k fixes α, hence h lies in G_{g·α} and gG_α g^{-1}⊆G_{g·α}.
Conversely, if h∈G_{g·α} then h(g·α)=g·α, so g^{-1}hg fixes α and belongs to G_α, which gives h∈gG_α g^{-1}; thus G_{g·α}⊆gG_α g^{-1}.
Combining the two inclusions yields the desired equality gG_α g^{-1}=G_{g·α}.
When the action is transitive, for each β∈A there exists g∈G with g·α=β, so every stabilizer G_β is a conjugate of G_α, and the total intersection ⋂_{β∈A}G_β equals ⋂_{g∈G}gG_α g^{-1}.
But any element in ⋂_{β∈A}G_β fixes every point of A; since G is a permutation group, the only permutation that fixes all points is the identity.
Therefore the intersection is just the trivial subgroup, proving ⋂_{β∈A}G_β={1}, as required.

problem: Section 4.3, Exercise 1: Suppose G has a left action on a set A, denoted by g · a for all g ∈ G and a ∈ A. Denote the corresponding right action on A by a · g. Prove that the (equivalence) relations ∼ and ∼′ defined by a ∼ b if and only if a = g · b for some g ∈ G and a ∼′ b if and only if a = b · g for some g ∈ G are the same relation (i.e., a ∼ b if and only if a ∼′ b).
steps: We are given that G acts on A on the left, so for each g in G and a in A we have an element g·a in A, and the right action a·g is defined in the usual way by a·g := g^{-1}·a to keep the two actions compatible.
Using this definition, the relation ~ is a~b iff there exists g in G such that a = g·b, and the relation ~' is a~'b iff there exists h in G with a = b·h = h^{-1}·b.
To show ~ implies ~', start with a = g·b. Setting h := g^{-1}, we get b·h = h^{-1}·b = g·b = a, so a~'b holds whenever a~b does.
Conversely, assume a~'b, so a = b·h = h^{-1}·b for some h. Taking g := h^{-1}, we have a = g·b, hence a~b, which shows ~' implies ~.
Therefore the two relations coincide: a~b if and only if a~'b, so the equivalence relations defined via the left action and via the induced right action are identical.

problem: Section 4.5, Exercise 1: Prove that if P ∈ Syl_p(G) and H is a subgroup of G containing P then P ∈ Syl_p(H). Give an example to show that, in general, a Sylow p-subgroup of a subgroup of G need not be a Sylow p-subgroup of G.
steps: Write |G| = p^n m with p not dividing m, so that |P| = p^n since P is a Sylow p-subgroup of G.
Let H be a subgroup of G with P ⊆ H, whence |H| divides |G| and therefore its p-part divides p^n.
Since |P| divides |H| by inclusion, it follows that p^n divides |H| as well.
Thus the highest power of p dividing |H| is at least p^n, while from divisibility of |H| into |G| it cannot exceed p^n, forcing equality.
Therefore the p-part of |H| is p^n, and P has that order, so P is a Sylow p-subgroup of H.
For an example, take G = S4 and let p = 2, and let H be the Klein four subgroup V4 ⊂ S4.
Then Sylow 2-subgroups of H are of order 4 (in fact H itself), whereas Sylow 2-subgroups of G have order 8.
Hence a Sylow 2-subgroup of H need not be a Sylow 2-subgroup of G, which shows the general statement fails in the reverse direction.

problem: Section 5.2, Exercise 1: In each of parts (a) to (e) give the number of nonisomorphic abelian groups of the specified order - do not list the groups: (a) order 100, (b) order 576, (c) order 1 155, (d) order 42875, (e) order 2704.
steps: Finite abelian groups of order n are determined by the invariant factor or elementary divisor decompositions, so the number of nonisomorphic groups equals the product over the primes dividing n of the number of partitions of the exponent of that prime in n.
This follows because each primary component is classified by a partition of the corresponding exponent, and different primes contribute independently to the direct product decomposition.
For order 100 we have 100=2^2·5^2, so the two primary components have exponents 2 and 2.
There are two partitions of 2 (namely 2 and 1+1), so each of the prime powers contributes a factor of 2, and the total number of nonisomorphic groups of order 100 is 2·2=4.
For order 576 we write 576=2^6·3^2, so the primary exponents are 6 for 2 and 2 for 3.
A partition of 6 has 11 possibilities while a partition of 2 has 2, giving 11·2=22 different groups of order 576.
For order 1155 we factor 1155=3·5·7·11, so every prime appears only to the first power.
Each exponent 1 has exactly one partition, hence there is only one nonisomorphic abelian group of order 1155.
For order 42875 we note that 42875=5^3·7^3, so both primes have exponent 3.
Because there are three partitions of 3 (3, 2+1, 1+1+1), the total number of groups of order 42875 is 3·3=9.
Finally, 2704=52^2=(2^2·13)^2=2^4·13^2; the partitions of 4 number 5 and those of 2 number 2, giving 5·2=10 nonisomorphic abelian groups of order 2704.

problem: Section 7.1, Exercise 2: Prove that if u is a unit in R then so is -u.
steps: Assume u is a unit in R, so there exists v in R with uv = 1 (the multiplicative identity).
Observe that multiplying both sides of uv = 1 by -1 gives (-u)v = -1 and similarly u(-v) = -1, but we really need a product equal to 1.
Instead consider the product (-u)(-v); since multiplication is bilinear over rings, (-u)(-v) = (−1 · u)(−1 · v) = (−1)(−1)(uv).
But (−1)(−1) = 1 in any ring, and uv = 1 by assumption, so (-u)(-v) = 1.
Therefore -v is a multiplicative inverse for -u, and hence -u is also a unit.

problem: Section 7.3, Exercise 1: Prove that the rings 2Z and 3Z are not isomorphic.
steps: The ring 2Z is an additive subgroup of Z generated by 2, and similarly 3Z is generated by 3, so both rings are infinite cyclic as abelian groups.
Assume for contradiction that there is a ring isomorphism φ:2Z→3Z, so φ is in particular an additive isomorphism between the cyclic groups.
An additive isomorphism of cyclic groups sends a generator to a generator, hence φ(2) must be a generator of 3Z, that is, φ(2)=3 or φ(2)=-3.
Since φ is a ring homomorphism, we have φ(4)=φ(2·2)=φ(2)φ(2)=φ(2)^2.
On the other hand φ(4)=φ(2+2)=φ(2)+φ(2)=2φ(2), so the multiplicative and additive descriptions give 2φ(2)=φ(2)^2.
Substituting φ(2)=3 yields 6=9, while φ(2)=-3 yields -6=9, and neither holds, contradicting the existence of φ.
Therefore no ring isomorphism can exist between 2Z and 3Z, so the rings are not isomorphic.

problem: Section 7.4, Exercise 1: Let L_j be the left ideal of M_n(R) consisting of arbitrary entries in the jth column and zero in all other entries and let E_{ij} be the element of M_n(R) whose i, j entry is 1 and whose other entries are all 0. Prove that L_j = M_n(R)E_{ij} for any i.
steps: Fix i; take an arbitrary matrix A in Mn(R) and compute the product A Eij entrywise: the (k, l) entry of A Eij is ∑m Am,l (Eij)m,l, but Eij has only a 1 in position (i, j), so this sum reduces to Ak,i when l = j and vanishes for l ≠ j.
Hence A Eij is a matrix whose only potentially nonzero column is the jth column, and that jth column is exactly the ith column of A; in particular every such product lies in Lj, so Mn(R)Eij ⊆ Lj.

To prove the reverse inclusion, let B ∈ Lj be arbitrary; thus all columns except the jth are zero, and the jth column can be arbitrary. Define a matrix A by copying the jth column of B into the ith column of A and setting all other columns of A equal to zero.

Then by the same product computation, (A Eij) has the jth column equal to the ith column of A, which equals the jth column of B, and all other columns zero; therefore A Eij = B, which shows B ∈ Mn(R)Eij.
Since an arbitrary element of Lj lies in Mn(R)Eij and vice versa, we conclude Lj = Mn(R)Eij.

problem: Section 8.1, Exercise 1: For each of the following five pairs of integers a and b, determine their greatest common divisor d and write d as a linear combination ax + by of a and b. (a) a = 20, b = 13. (b) a = 69, b = 372. (c) a = 11391, b = 5673. (d) a = 507885, b = 60808. (e) a = 91442056588823, b = 779086434385541 (the Euclidean Algorithm requires only 7 steps for these integers).
steps: Because ℤ is a PID the ideal generated by any pair (a,b) is principal, so finding d = gcd(a,b) and the linear combination ax + by is equivalent to running the Euclidean algorithm and tracing back the remainders for each pair.
For (a) we have 20 = 13 + 7, 13 = 7 + 6, 7 = 6 + 1, and 6 = 6·1, so the gcd is 1.
For (c) the divisions 11391 = 2·5673 + 45, 5673 = 126·45 + 3, and 45 = 15·3 show that gcd = 3.
For (e) the prescribed seven Euclidean steps end with gcd 1,403,109,613, and back-substitution gives 1,403,109,613 = 277522·91442056588823 − 32573·779086434385541.

problem: Section 8.2, Exercise 2: Prove that any two nonzero elements of a P.I.D. have a least common multiple ( cf. Exercise 1 1, Section 1).
steps: Let R be a principal ideal domain and let a,b be nonzero elements of R whose lcm we wish to exhibit.
Introduce the ideals generated by a and b individually; since R is a PID, we can take generators for (a) and (b), and these ideals are principal by assumption.
Observe that the intersection of the ideals (a) and (b) is again an ideal, hence principal; denote a generator by d, so that (a)∩(b) = (d).
Because d lies in both (a) and (b), it must be divisible by any common multiple of a and b, making d a candidate for the lcm; in particular, any other common multiple m satisfies d | m.
Show that d itself is a common multiple: since d ∈ (a), there exists r with d = ra, similarly d = sb for some s, so a | d and b | d.
Therefore d is a common multiple divisible by every other common multiple, hence serves as the least common multiple of a and b in R.
Conclude that any two nonzero elements of a PID possess a least common multiple, as required.

problem: Section 9.2, Exercise 6: Describe (briefly) the ring structure of the following rings: (a) Z[x]/(2), (b) Z[x]/(x), (c) Z[x]/(x^2), (d) Z[x, y]/(x^2, y^2, 2). Show that a^2 = 0 or 1 for every a in the last ring and determine those elements with a^2 = 0. Determine the characteristics of each of these rings (cf. Exercise 26, Section 7.3).
steps: Note that Z[x]/(2) is the quotient of the polynomial ring by the principal ideal generated by integer 2, so it is isomorphic to (Z/2Z)[x], the polynomial ring over the field of two elements, and its characteristic is 2.
The quotient Z[x]/(x) simply sets x=0, so it is isomorphic to Z, which has characteristic 0 and the usual ring structure; I write this because the notation Z[x]j(x) is equivalent to Z[x]/(x).
Similarly, Z[x]/(x^2) consists of polynomials reduced modulo x^2, so every element can be written as a+bx with a,b in Z and x^2=0, giving a ring of dual numbers over Z with characteristic 0.

In Z[x,y]/(x^2,y^2,2) every element is represented by a linear combination a+bx+cy+dxy with coefficients in Z but modded out by x^2, y^2, and 2, so in fact coefficients live in Z/2Z and we have relations x^2=y^2=0; hence every element satisfies a^2=0 or equals the multiplicative identity depending on whether its constant term is 0 or 1.

To see which elements square to zero, note (a+bx+cy+dxy)^2 expands using x^2=y^2=0, giving a^2 plus cross terms; since coefficients are mod 2, the square equals 0 exactly when a=0, so the nilpotent elements are those with zero constant term, and the other elements square to the identity in the quotient because 1^2=1.

The characteristic of Z[x,y]/(x^2,y^2,2) is 2 because 2=0 in the coefficient ring, so repeated addition of 1 gives zero after two steps, and similarly for Z[x]/(x^2) and Z[x]/(x) the characteristic remains 0 because their coefficients are in Z, while Z[x]/(2) inherits characteristic 2 from Z/2Z.

problem: Section 9.2, Exercise 7: Determine all the ideals of the ring Z[x] / (2, x + 1).
steps: We begin with the ring R := Z[x]/(2, x+1) and aim to describe its ideal structure, so it suffices to understand the nature of R itself as a quotient of Z[x].

Because one of the generators of the ideal is 2, the canonical reduction-of-coefficients map Z[x] → (Z/2)[x] factors through the quotient by (2), giving an isomorphism Z[x]/(2) ≡ (Z/2)[x]; under this map the ideal (2, x+1) becomes the image of the principal ideal generated by x+1.
By the third isomorphism theorem we therefore have R ≡ (Z/2)[x]/(x+1), since (2, x+1)/(2) corresponds to (x+1) in the quotient (Z/2)[x].
The polynomial x+1 has degree one over the field Z/2, so the ideal (x+1) is maximal in (Z/2)[x], whence (Z/2)[x]/(x+1) is a field isomorphic to Z/2 via evaluation at the root x = -1.
Consequently R ≡ Z/2 is a field, and every nonzero ideal of R must be the whole ring while the zero ideal is the only other ideal.
Hence the only ideals of Z[x]/(2, x+1) are the zero ideal and the entire ring itself.

problem: Exercise 1.5.9: Show that a metric space (X, d) is compact if and only if every sequence in X has at least one limit point.
steps: Let (X, d) be a compact metric space.
Assume for contradiction that there exists a sequence {x_n} in X with no limit points.
This implies that for each point x in X, there exists a neighborhood U_x such that U_x contains only finitely many points of the sequence {x_n}.
Now consider the open cover of X consisting of the sets U_x for each x in X.
Since the space is compact, this open cover must have a finite subcover.
However, a finite subcover would imply that there exists a point in X that captures infinitely many terms of the sequence {x_n}, contradicting the assumption of no limit points.
Thus, our initial assumption is false, indicating that every sequence in X must have at least one limit point.
Now, let us prove the converse: if every sequence in X has at least one limit point, we want to show that (X, d) is compact.
Using the sequential compactness criterion, we can show that every sequence in X has a convergent subsequence, implying that X is compact.

problem: Exercise 1.5.11: Let (X, d) have the property that every open cover of X has a finite subcover. Show that X is compact.
steps: Let (X, d) be a topological space where every open cover of X has a finite subcover.
Recall that a space is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover.
Take an arbitrary open cover of X, denoted by {U_i} where i is in some index set I.
By the property of (X, d), since {U_i} is an open cover of X, it must have a finite subcover.
Let {U_{i_1}, U_{i_2}, ..., U_{i_n}} be the finite subcover of the open cover {U_i}.
Since {U_{i_1}, U_{i_2}, ..., U_{i_n}} covers X, this means that every point in X is contained in at least one U_{i_j} for j = 1, 2, ..., n.
Thus, we have shown that every open cover of X admits a finite subcover.
Hence, by the definition of compactness, X is compact.

problem: Exercise 1.5.13: Let E and F be two compact subsets of R. Show that the Cartesian product E x F is a compact subset of R^2.
steps: Recall that a subset of a metric space is compact if it is closed and bounded.
Since E is a compact subset of R, it is closed and bounded.
Similarly, since F is a compact subset of R, it is closed and bounded.
The Cartesian product E x F consists of all points (x, y) where x is in E and y is in F.
To show that E x F is bounded, note that if x is in E and y is in F, both x and y are within some bounds.
Combine the bounds of E and F to establish bounds for the points in E x F.
Next, we need to show that E x F is closed; consider a convergent sequence of points (x_n, y_n) in E x F.
Since E and F are compact, their projections will converge to limits in E and F respectively.
Thus, the limit point of (x_n, y_n) is in E x F, proving that E x F is closed.

problem: Exercise 1.5.14: Let (X, d) be a metric space, let E be a non-empty compact subset of X, and let x_0 be a point in X. Show that there exists a point x in E such that d(x_0, x) = inf {d(x_0, y) : y in E}.
steps: Let R = inf { d(x_0, y) : y in E }.
For each n >= 1, choose x^(n) in E such that d(x_0, x^(n)) <= R + 1/n.
Since E is compact, the sequence (x^(n)) converges to some point x in E.
Because the map y -> d(x_0, y) is continuous, we have d(x_0, x) = lim_{n->infinity} d(x_0, x^(n)) = R.
Therefore x is a point of E that realizes the distance infimum.

problem: Exercise 1.5.15: Let (X, d) be a compact metric space. Suppose (K_alpha) is a collection of closed sets with the finite intersection property. Show that the entire collection has non-empty intersection, and show by counterexample that this fails if X is not compact.
steps: Let (X, d) be a compact metric space and let K_alpha be a collection of closed sets in X with the finite intersection property.

The finite intersection property states that for any finite selection of sets K_{alpha_1}, K_{alpha_2}, ..., K_{alpha_n} from the collection, the intersection K_{alpha_1} ∩ K_{alpha_2} ∩ ... ∩ K_{alpha_n} is non-empty.
Since X is compact, every open cover of X has a finite subcover.
Consider the collection of complements of the closed sets, which are open sets in X.

For any finite selection of indices, the complement of the intersection of the closed sets is the union of the complements, i.e., (K_{alpha_1} ∩ K_{alpha_2} ∩ ... ∩ K_{alpha_n})^c = K_{alpha_1}^c ∪ K_{alpha_2}^c ∪ ... ∪ K_{alpha_n}^c.
Since the intersection of the closed sets is non-empty by the finite intersection property, their complements cannot cover the entire space X, meaning the union does not equal X.
By the compactness of X, this implies that there exists a point x in X that belongs to all closed sets K_alpha.
Thus, the collection of closed sets K_alpha has a non-empty intersection.

To show the failure of this property without compactness, consider the interval (0, 1) in the real line, which is not compact, and take the collection of closed sets [1/n, 1] for n = 1, 2, 3, ... which has the finite intersection property but the intersection is empty.

problem: Exercise 2.2.11: Let f: R^2 -> R be defined by f(x,y)=xy/(x^2+y^2) when (x,y)!=(0,0), and f(x,y)=0 otherwise. Show that for each fixed x the map y -> f(x,y) is continuous, and for each fixed y the map x -> f(x,y) is continuous, but f is not continuous on R^2.
steps: Fix x != 0. Then as a function of y, f(x,y) = xy/(x^2+y^2) is a quotient of continuous functions, and the denominator never vanishes, so y -> f(x,y) is continuous.
If x = 0, then f(0,y) = 0 for all y, so the function of y is constant and therefore continuous.
Thus for every fixed x, the map y -> f(x,y) is continuous on R.
Similarly, if y != 0 then x -> f(x,y) = xy/(x^2+y^2) is again a quotient of continuous functions with nonzero denominator, so it is continuous.
If y = 0, then f(x,0) = 0 for all x, so the function of x is constant and therefore continuous.
Thus for every fixed y, the map x -> f(x,y) is continuous on R.
To test continuity of f at (0,0), approach the origin along the line y = x. Then f(x,x) = x^2/(2x^2) = 1/2 whenever x != 0.
Approach instead along the line y = -x. Then f(x,-x) = -x^2/(2x^2) = -1/2 whenever x != 0.
These two path limits are different, so the limit of f(x,y) as (x,y) -> (0,0) does not exist. Therefore f is not continuous at the origin.

problem: Exercise 2.2.12: Let f: R^2 -> R be defined by f(x,y) := x^2/y when y != 0, and f(x,y) := 0 when y = 0. Show that lim_{t->0} f(tx, ty) = f(0,0) for every (x, y) in R^2, but that f is not continuous at the origin.
steps: We start with the function f defined as f(x,y) = x^2/y when y != 0 and f(x,y) = 0 when y = 0.
We need to compute the limit of f(tx, ty) as t approaches 0 for any fixed (x, y) in R^2.
For y ≠ 0, we have f(tx, ty) = (tx)^2 / (ty) = t^2 x^2 / (ty) = (t x^2) / y.
As t approaches 0, (t x^2) / y approaches 0 since y is a constant and t x^2 goes to 0.
Therefore, we find that lim_{t->0} f(tx, ty) = 0 for any fixed (x, y) where y ≠ 0.
When y = 0, we have f(tx, 0) = 0 by the definition of f.
Thus, in both cases (y ≠ 0 and y = 0), we find that lim_{t->0} f(tx, ty) = 0.
Since f(0, 0) = 0, this shows that lim_{t->0} f(tx, ty) = f(0, 0) for every (x, y) in R^2.
To show that f is not continuous at the origin, we need to check if the limit as (x, y) approaches (0, 0) equals f(0, 0).

problem: Exercise 2.3.4: Let (X, d_X), (Y, d_Y), (Z, d_Z) be metric spaces, and let f: X -> Y and g: Y -> Z be uniformly continuous. Show that g o f is uniformly continuous.
steps: Let ε > 0 be given.
Since g is uniformly continuous, there exists δ_Y > 0 such that for all y_1, y_2 in Y, if d_Y(y_1, y_2) < δ_Y, then d_Z(g(y_1), g(y_2)) < ε.
Since f is uniformly continuous, there exists δ_X > 0 such that for all x_1, x_2 in X, if d_X(x_1, x_2) < δ_X, then d_Y(f(x_1), f(x_2)) < δ_Y.
Now, take arbitrary x_1, x_2 in X such that d_X(x_1, x_2) < δ_X.
By the uniform continuity of f, it follows that d_Y(f(x_1), f(x_2)) < δ_Y.
Next, since d_Y(f(x_1), f(x_2)) < δ_Y, we can apply the uniform continuity of g.
Therefore, we have d_Z(g(f(x_1)), g(f(x_2))) < ε.
Since this is true for arbitrary x_1, x_2 in X with d_X(x_1, x_2) < δ_X, it follows that g o f is uniformly continuous.
Thus, we conclude that g o f: X -> Z is uniformly continuous.

problem: Exercise 2.3.6: Show that the addition function (x, y) -> x + y and the subtraction function (x, y) -> x - y are uniformly continuous from R^2 to R, but the multiplication function (x, y) -> xy is not. Conclude corresponding closure properties for uniformly continuous functions.
steps: Definition of Uniform Continuity: A function F : R^2 -> R is uniformly continuous if for every epsilon > 0 there exists delta > 0 such that distance < delta implies output difference < epsilon.
Proof for Addition Function: |(x1+y1)-(x2+y2)| <= |x1-x2| + |y1-y2|, so addition is uniformly continuous.
Proof for Subtraction Function: |(x1-y1)-(x2-y2)| <= |x1-x2| + |y1-y2|, so subtraction is uniformly continuous.
Proof for Multiplication Function: For any delta > 0, choose (x1,y1)=(n,1) and (x2,y2)=(n+delta,1) for large n.
Calculating Difference: |x1y1-x2y2| = |n-(n+delta)| = delta.
Behavior as n -> infinity: delta remains constant while the input distance is delta, so for epsilon = 1 there exists no delta small enough such that uniform continuity holds.
Conclusion: addition and subtraction are uniformly continuous, while multiplication is not.

problem: Exercise 2.4.2: Let f : X -> Y be a function from a connected metric space (X, d) to a metric space (Y, d_disc) with the discrete metric. Show that f is continuous if and only if it is constant.
steps: Let f : X -> Y be a function where X is a connected metric space and Y has the discrete metric.
Recall that in the discrete metric, the distance d_disc(y1, y2) is 0 if y1 = y2 and 1 if y1 ≠ y2.
The definition of continuity at a point x in X states that for every ε > 0, there exists a δ > 0 such that if d(x, x') < δ, then d_disc(f(x), f(x')) < ε.
In the discrete metric, d_disc(f(x), f(x')) < ε implies that f(x) = f(x') when ε ≤ 1.
For f to be continuous at any point x in X, we need to ensure that if points x' are arbitrarily close to x, they must map to the same point in Y.
If f is not constant, there exist points x1, x2 in X such that f(x1) ≠ f(x2).
Since X is connected, there exists a path or a subset that connects x1 and x2, leading to points that will map to different values in Y, violating the continuity criteria.
Therefore, if f is continuous, it must be that f(x) is constant for all x in X.
Conversely, if f is constant, it trivially satisfies the continuity condition because all images under f are the same point in Y.

problem: Exercise 2.4.6: Let (X, d) be a metric space, and let (E_alpha) be a collection of connected sets with non-empty common intersection. Show that their union is connected.
steps: Let (X, d) be a metric space and let (E_alpha) be a collection of connected sets with a non-empty common intersection.
Denote the common intersection of all sets E_alpha as C, which is non-empty by assumption.

Since each E_alpha is connected, there do not exist disjoint non-empty open sets U_alpha and V_alpha in X such that E_alpha is contained in U_alpha ∪ V_alpha and E_alpha intersects both U_alpha and V_alpha.
Consider the union of all sets E_alpha, denoted as U = ∪ E_alpha.
Assume for the sake of contradiction that U is not connected.
Then there exist disjoint non-empty open sets U_1 and V_1 in X such that U is contained in U_1 ∪ V_1.
By the properties of unions, it follows that there exist some index alpha such that E_alpha intersects both U_1 and V_1.
Since E_alpha is connected, it must lie entirely within one of the sets U_1 or V_1, which contradicts the assumption that E_alpha intersects both.
Therefore, the assumption that U is not connected must be false, which implies that the union U = ∪ E_alpha is connected.

problem: Exercise 2.4.8: Let (X, d) be a metric space, and let E be a subset of X. Show that if E is connected, then the closure of E is also connected. Is the converse true?
steps: Let E be a connected subset of the metric space (X, d).
We need to prove that the closure of E, denoted by cl(E), is also connected.
Assume for contradiction that cl(E) is not connected.
Then there exist two non-empty open sets U and V in X such that cl(E) is contained in U union V, U intersects V is empty, and U intersects cl(E) is not empty, and V intersects cl(E) is not empty.
Since E is dense in its closure, the open sets U and V must also intersect E non-trivially, meaning both U intersects E and V intersects E are not empty.
This implies that we can separate E into two non-empty disjoint open sets, contradicting the assumption that E is connected.
Thus, our assumption that cl(E) is not connected must be wrong, hence cl(E) is connected.
Therefore, if E is connected, then cl(E) is also connected.
The converse is not necessarily true, as a disconnected set can have a connected closure.

problem: Exercise 3.2.1: Let f : R -> R and define shifted functions f_a(x) := f(x-a). Show that f is continuous iff every sequence of shifts by a_n -> 0 converges pointwise to f, and f is uniformly continuous iff every such sequence converges uniformly to f.
steps: Assume f is continuous at a point x in R.
By the definition of continuity, for any ε > 0, there exists a δ > 0 such that if |x - y| < δ, then |f(x) - f(y)| < ε.
Let a_n be a sequence converging to 0, then f_a_n(x) = f(x - a_n).
As a_n approaches 0, we have x - a_n approaching x, thereby |f_a_n(x) - f(x)| should converge to 0.
This implies pointwise convergence of f_a_n to f for every x in R.
Now assume that every sequence of shifts by a_n converges pointwise to f.
By the definition of uniform continuity, for any ε > 0, we must also show that the convergence is uniform, which follows from the pointwise convergence condition applied to the entire space.

problem: Exercise 3.2.3: Suppose f_n converges pointwise to f on X, and h : R -> R is continuous. Show that h \circ f_n converges pointwise to h \circ f on X.
steps: Let X be a set and let f_n : X -> R converge pointwise to f : X -> R.
This means that for every x in X, f_n(x) converges to f(x) as n approaches infinity.
We want to show that h \circ f_n converges pointwise to h \circ f, which means we need to show that for every x in X, h(f_n(x)) converges to h(f(x)).
Since h is continuous, for any ε > 0 there exists a δ > 0 such that if |y - z| < δ, then |h(y) - h(z)| < ε.
Choose ε > 0, and since f_n converges pointwise to f, there exists an N such that for all n ≥ N, |f_n(x) - f(x)| < δ for every x in X.
Thus for n ≥ N, we have |h(f_n(x)) - h(f(x))| < ε because of the continuity of h at f(x).
This holds true for every x in X, which implies that h \circ f_n converges pointwise to h \circ f on X.
Therefore, we conclude that h \circ f_n converges pointwise to h \circ f on X.

problem: Exercise 3.3.8: Suppose f_n -> f uniformly and g_n -> g uniformly on X, and the sequences (f_n), (g_n) are uniformly bounded. Prove that f_n g_n converges uniformly to fg.
steps: Since f_n -> f uniformly, for any epsilon > 0, there exists an N1 such that for all n >= N1, |f_n(x) - f(x)| < epsilon/2 for all x in X.
Since g_n -> g uniformly, for any epsilon > 0, there exists an N2 such that for all n >= N2, |g_n(x) - g(x)| < epsilon/(2M) for all x in X, where M is an upper bound for |g_n(x)| for all n.
The uniform boundedness of (f_n) and (g_n) means there exist constants K1 and K2 such that |f_n(x)| <= K1 and |g_n(x)| <= K2 for all n and x in X.
Let N = max(N1, N2). For all n >= N, we have both |f_n(x) - f(x)| < epsilon/2 and |g_n(x) - g(x)| < epsilon/(2K1) for all x in X.
Using the property of products, we can estimate |f_n(x)g_n(x) - fg(x)| as follows: |f_n(x)g_n(x) - fg(x)| = |f_n(x)g_n(x) - f_n(x)g(x) + f_n(x)g(x) - fg(x)|.
Applying the triangle inequality, we can separate this into two terms: |f_n(x)(g_n(x) - g(x))| + |(f_n(x) - f(x))g(x)|.
For the first term, |f_n(x)(g_n(x) - g(x))| can be bounded by K1 * |g_n(x) - g(x)| < K1 * (epsilon/(2K1)) = epsilon/2.
For the second term, |(f_n(x) - f(x))g(x)| can be bounded by |f_n(x) - f(x)| * |g(x)| <= (epsilon/2) * K2 < epsilon/2 for sufficiently large n.
Combining these estimates gives |f_n(x)g_n(x) - fg(x)| < epsilon for all x in X, proving that f_n g_n converges uniformly to fg.

problem: Exercise 3.8.3: Let f : R -> R be a compactly supported, continuous function. Show that f is bounded and uniformly continuous.
steps: Since f is a compactly supported function, there exists a closed and bounded interval [a, b] such that f(x) = 0 for all x outside of [a, b].
The continuity of f on the closed interval [a, b] implies, by the Extreme Value Theorem, that f attains its maximum and minimum on this interval.
Let M = max_{x in [a, b]} |f(x)|. Then, for all x in R, |f(x)| <= M.
Thus, f is bounded because there exists a constant M such that |f(x)| <= M for all x in R.
Next, we show that f is uniformly continuous on R. Since f is continuous on the compact set [a, b], it is uniformly continuous on [a, b].
For any ε > 0, there exists a δ > 0 such that for any x, y in [a, b], if |x - y| < δ, then |f(x) - f(y)| < ε.
Outside the interval [a, b], since f(x) = 0, for any x, y outside this interval, we also have |f(x) - f(y)| = 0 < ε.
Therefore, f is uniformly continuous on R because it is uniformly continuous on [a, b] and continuous elsewhere with no discontinuities.
In conclusion, since f is both bounded and uniformly continuous, we have shown the required properties.

problem: Exercise 3.8.8: Let f : [0,1] -> R be continuous, and suppose that integral_0^1 f(x) x^n dx = 0 for all n >= 0. Show that f must be the zero function.
steps: Since f is continuous on the closed interval [0, 1], it is also uniformly continuous.
Given that integral_0^1 f(x) x^n dx = 0 for all n >= 0, we consider the set of functions {x^n} for n = 0, 1, 2, ... as a dense subset in the space of continuous functions on [0, 1].
The functions x^n converge uniformly to 0 on [0, 1] as n approaches infinity, implying that for any ε > 0, there exists an N such that for all n > N, |x^n| < ε for all x in [0, 1].
By the continuity of f and the fact that integral_0^1 f(x) x^n dx = 0, we can use the Stone-Weierstrass theorem to assert that f must be orthogonal to all polynomials on [0, 1].
The span of polynomials is dense in the space of continuous functions on [0, 1], hence if f is orthogonal to all polynomials, it must be the case that f(x) = 0 everywhere on [0, 1].
To show this explicitly, suppose for contradiction that there exists a point c in [0, 1] such that f(c) ≠ 0.
By the continuity of f, there exists a δ > 0 such that |f(x) - f(c)| < |f(c)|/2 for all x in (c - δ, c + δ) intersected with [0, 1].

This would imply that for sufficiently large n, the integral integral_c-delta^c+delta f(x) x^n dx would yield a non-zero value, contradicting our initial condition that this integral is zero for all n.
Thus, we conclude that f must indeed be the zero function for all x in [0, 1].

problem: Exercise 4.5.4: Let f(x) := exp(-1/x) for x > 0 and f(x) := 0 for x <= 0. Prove that f is infinitely differentiable, all derivatives at 0 vanish, but f is not real analytic at 0.
steps: Define the function f(x) as f(x) = exp(-1/x) for x > 0 and f(x) = 0 for x <= 0.
To show that f is infinitely differentiable, we need to consider the derivatives of f at x > 0 and at x = 0.
For x > 0, f is a composition of continuously differentiable functions, thus it is infinitely differentiable on (0, ∞).
Next, we compute the derivatives of f at x = 0 using the definition of the derivative.
For each n-th derivative, we evaluate the limit: f^(n)(0) = lim (h -> 0) (f^(n)(h)).
Since f(h) = exp(-1/h) approaches 0 as h approaches 0 from the right, all higher-order derivatives also approach 0.
Thus, we conclude that f^(n)(0) = 0 for all n >= 0, showing all derivatives at 0 vanish.
To show that f is not real analytic at 0, we note that a function is real analytic at a point if its Taylor series converges to the function in a neighborhood of that point.
However, the Taylor series of f at 0, which is simply the series of zeros, does not converge to f(x) for x > 0, indicating that f is not real analytic at 0.

problem: Exercise 4.5.7: Let f : R -> (0, infinity) be a positive, real analytic function such that f'(x) = f(x) for all x. Show that f(x) = C e^x for some positive constant C.
steps: Given that f : R -> (0, infinity) is a positive, real analytic function and f'(x) = f(x) for all x, we can analyze the implications of the derivative equation.
Since f'(x) = f(x), we can rearrange this to form the differential equation f'(x) - f(x) = 0.
The general solution to the differential equation of the form y' - y = 0 is of the form y(x) = Ce^(kx) for some constants C and k.
In our case, since f is positive and defined for all x in R, we will consider the specific solution f(x) = Ce^(x) where k = 1.
Next, we note the condition that f(x) must be positive for all x in R implies that the constant C must be positive.
To confirm that f(x) = C e^x satisfies the derivative condition, we differentiate: f'(x) = C e^x, which matches f(x) since f(x) = C e^x.
We conclude that the form of f(x) is consistent with the derivative condition across its entire domain.
Thus, we have shown that the function f can be expressed as f(x) = C e^x for some positive constant C.

problem: Exercise 4.5.9: Let P(x) be a polynomial, and let c > 0. Show that there exists a real number N > 0 such that e^(cx) > |P(x)| for all x > N.
steps: Let P(x) be a polynomial of degree n, so P(x)=a_n x^n+...+a_0 with a_n != 0.
As x -> infinity, the leading term dominates, so |P(x)| ~ |a_n| x^n.
The exponential function e^(cx) grows faster than any polynomial as x -> infinity.
We compare e^(cx) with |a_n| x^n.
Consider lim_{x->infinity} e^(cx) / (|a_n| x^n) = lim 1/(|a_n| x^n e^{-cx}). As x -> infinity, e^{-cx} decays exponentially, while |a_n| x^n grows polynomially. Thus, the limit evaluates to 0.
Since the limit approaches 0, there exists M > 0 such that for all x > M, e^(cx) > |a_n| x^n.
Hence e^(cx) > |P(x)| for all sufficiently large x.

problem: Exercise 4.5.10: Let f : (0, +infinity) x R -> R be the exponential function f(x, y) := x^y. Show that f is continuous.
steps: We need to show that the function f(x, y) = x^y is continuous for all (x, y) in the domain (0, +infinity) x R.
Recall that a function is continuous at a point (a, b) if for every epsilon > 0, there exists a delta > 0 such that if ||(x, y) - (a, b)|| < delta, then |f(x, y) - f(a, b)| < epsilon.
We can express f(x, y) = x^y as f(x, y) = e^(y ln(x)), where ln(x) is the natural logarithm of x.
Both the exponential function e^u and the natural logarithm ln(x) are continuous functions in their respective domains.
Thus, the function g(x) = ln(x) is continuous on (0, +infinity) and the function h(y) = y is continuous on R.
The composition of continuous functions is continuous, hence the function h(y) ln(x) is continuous on (0, +infinity) x R.
Since the exponential function is continuous, the composite function e^(y ln(x)) is also continuous on the same domain.
Therefore, f(x, y) = x^y is continuous for all (x, y) in (0, +infinity) x R.

problem: Exercise 2.2.8: Let (X, d_X) and (Y, d_Y) be metric spaces. Define d_{X x Y}((x,y),(x',y')) := d_X(x,x') + d_Y(y,y'). Show that this is a metric and deduce coordinatewise analogues of convergence and continuity.
steps: To show that d_{X x Y} is a metric, we must verify four properties: non-negativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and the triangle inequality.
First, we check non-negativity: For any (x,y) and (x',y') in X x Y, both d_X(x,x') and d_Y(y,y') are non-negative, hence d_{X x Y}((x,y),(x',y')) = d_X(x,x') + d_Y(y,y') ≥ 0.

Next, we check the identity of indiscernibles: d_{X x Y}((x,y),(x',y')) = 0 implies d_X(x,x') + d_Y(y,y') = 0. Since both terms are non-negative, this implies d_X(x,x') = 0 and d_Y(y,y') = 0, thus x = x' and y = y', so (x,y) = (x',y').
Then, we verify symmetry: d_{X x Y}((x,y),(x',y')) = d_X(x,x') + d_Y(y,y') = d_Y(y',y) + d_X(x',x) = d_{X x Y}((x',y'),(x,y)).

Finally, we show the triangle inequality: For any (x,y), (x'',y''), and (x',y') in X x Y, we have d_{X x Y}((x,y),(x',y')) = d_X(x,x') + d_Y(y,y') and d_{X x Y}((x,y),(x'',y'')) + d_{X x Y}((x'',y''),(x',y')) = (d_X(x,x'') + d_Y(y,y'')) + (d_X(x'',x') + d_Y(y'',y')).

Applying the triangle inequality in (X, d_X) and (Y, d_Y), we find d_X(x,x') ≤ d_X(x,x'') + d_X(x'',x') and d_Y(y,y') ≤ d_Y(y,y'') + d_Y(y'',y'), allowing us to conclude that d_{X x Y} satisfies the triangle inequality.
Since all four properties are satisfied, d_{X x Y} is a metric on X x Y.

From this metric, we can deduce coordinatewise analogues of convergence: a sequence ((x_n, y_n)) converges to (x,y) in the product space if and only if x_n converges to x in X and y_n converges to y in Y.

Additionally, for continuity: a function f: X -> Y is continuous at x if and only if for every sequence x_n converging to x, the sequence f(x_n) converges to f(x) in Y, which corresponds to the continuity in the product space.

problem: Exercise 4.7.4: Let x, y be real numbers such that x^2 + y^2 = 1. Show that there is exactly one real number theta in (-pi, pi] such that x = sin(theta) and y = cos(theta).
steps: Given x^2 + y^2 = 1, we recognize this equation represents a circle of radius 1 centered at the origin in the xy-plane.
We can parameterize the points (x, y) on the circle using trigonometric functions, specifically x = sin(theta) and y = cos(theta).
This gives us the relation sin^2(theta) + cos^2(theta) = 1, which is valid for all real theta.
To find theta, we can express x and y in terms of the angle theta: x = sin(theta) and y = cos(theta).
Now, we need to show the uniqueness of theta in the interval (-pi, pi].
The functions sin(theta) and cos(theta) are periodic and each range over [-1, 1].

However, within the interval (-pi, pi], each value of x (which corresponds to sin(theta)) and y (which corresponds to cos(theta)) can only map to a unique angle due to the properties of the sine and cosine functions.
Thus, for any (x, y) satisfying x^2 + y^2 = 1, there exists exactly one theta in the interval (-pi, pi] that satisfies x = sin(theta) and y = cos(theta).
This establishes the required uniqueness of theta.

problem: Exercise 4.7.5: Show that if r, s > 0 and theta, alpha are real numbers such that r e^{i theta} = s e^{i alpha}, then r = s and theta = alpha + 2 pi k for some integer k.
steps: Assume that r e^{i theta} = s e^{i alpha} for r, s > 0 and theta, alpha in R.
Rearranging gives e^{i theta} = (s/r) e^{i alpha}.
Since r > 0, we can express s/r as a positive real number.
Let x = s/r, where x > 0, then we have e^{i theta} = x e^{i alpha}.
Taking magnitudes of both sides gives |e^{i theta}| = |x e^{i alpha}|.
Since |e^{i theta}| and |e^{i alpha}| are both 1, we have 1 = x, thus x = 1.
This implies that s/r = 1, leading to r = s.
Now consider the arguments: theta = alpha + 2 pi k for some integer k, because the exponential function is periodic with period 2 pi.
Thus, we have shown that if r e^{i theta} = s e^{i alpha}, then r = s and theta = alpha + 2 pi k.

problem: Exercise 4.7.7: For any real number theta and integer n, prove the de Moivre identities for cos(n theta) and sin(n theta).
steps: Let theta be a real number and n be an integer.
Recall Euler's formula, which states that e^(i * theta) = cos(theta) + i * sin(theta).
Using Euler's formula, we can express cos(n * theta) and sin(n * theta) in terms of exponentials: e^(i * n * theta) = cos(n * theta) + i * sin(n * theta).
Now, we can write e^(i * n * theta) as (e^(i * theta))^n by applying the properties of exponents.
Substituting e^(i * theta) into the equation, we have (cos(theta) + i * sin(theta))^n.
Next, we apply the Binomial theorem to expand (cos(theta) + i * sin(theta))^n.
The expansion gives us a sum of terms of the form C(n, k) * (cos(theta))^(n-k) * (i * sin(theta))^k for k from 0 to n.
Separating real and imaginary parts from the sum, we find terms that contribute to cos(n * theta) and sin(n * theta).
Thus, we conclude that cos(n * theta) and sin(n * theta) can be expressed in terms of the sums of these separated terms, confirming the de Moivre identities.

problem: Exercise 5.1.3: Show that C(R/Z; C) with the sup norm metric d_infty is a metric space. Furthermore, show that this metric space is complete.
steps: Define the space C(R/Z; C) as the set of continuous functions from the circle R/Z to the complex numbers C.
Introduce the sup norm metric d_infinity, defined as d_infinity(f, g) = sup { |f(x) - g(x)| : x in R/Z } for any two functions f, g in C(R/Z; C).
Verify that d_infinity satisfies the properties of a metric: non-negativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and triangle inequality.
Prove that d_infinity is non-negative by showing that |f(x) - g(x)| is always greater than or equal to 0 for all x in R/Z.
Demonstrate the identity of indiscernibles by showing that d_infinity(f, g) = 0 if and only if f = g for all x in R/Z.
Establish symmetry by confirming that d_infinity(f, g) = d_infinity(g, f) for all f, g in C(R/Z; C).
Show the triangle inequality holds by using the inequality |f(x) - h(x)| ≤ |f(x) - g(x)| + |g(x) - h(x)| and taking the supremum over x.
To show completeness, take a Cauchy sequence of functions {f_n} in C(R/Z; C) with respect to the metric d_infinity.
Demonstrate that the pointwise limit of the Cauchy sequence exists and is continuous, thus confirming that the limit function belongs to C(R/Z; C).

problem: Exercise 1.1.8: Let \( d_{l^2}(x, y) := \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2 \right)^{1/2} \) and \( d_{l^1}(x, y) := \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - y_i| \) on \( \mathbf{R}^n \). Prove the two inequalities \( d_{l^2}(x, y) \le d_{l^1}(x, y) \le \sqrt{n}\, d_{l^2}(x, y) \) for all \( x, y \in \mathbf{R}^n \). (Hint: for the first inequality, square both sides. For the second inequality, use the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality \( \left| \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i b_i \right| \le \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i^2 \right)^{1/2} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i^2 \right)^{1/2} \).)
steps: The two inequalities in (1.1) are the reverse triangle inequality ||x|−|y||≤|x−y| and the standard triangle inequality |x−y|≤|x|+|y|, so I set out to prove each one in turn.
To prove the reverse triangle inequality, I observe that both sides are nonnegative, so squaring them is harmless and gives (|x|−|y|)^2≤(x−y)^2.
Expanding both sides gives |x|^2−2|x||y|+|y|^2 on the left and x^2−2xy+y^2 on the right, and since |x|^2=x^2 and |y|^2=y^2 this inequality reduces to −2|x||y|≤−2xy.
Dividing by −2 (which reverses the inequality) yields xy≤|x||y|, and that is obvious because |x||y|=|xy|≥xy for arbitrary real numbers x and y.
Thus squaring both sides shows ||x|−|y||≤|x−y|, completing the first inequality.
For the second inequality I appeal to Exercise (1.1.5), which established the triangle inequality |a+b|≤|a|+|b| for arbitrary real numbers a and b.
Applying that result with a=x and b=−y gives |x−y|=|x+(−y)|≤|x|+|−y|, and the fact that |−y|=|y| turns this into |x−y|≤|x|+|y|.
This completes the proof of both inequalities in (1.1).

problem: Exercise 1.5.4: Let \( (R, d) \) be the real line with the standard metric. Give an example of a continuous function \( f: R \to R \), and an open set \( V \subseteq R \), such that the image \( f(V):= \{ f(x): x \in V \} \) of \( V \) is not open.
steps: Recall that a function from R to R is continuous everywhere if it is constant, for instance f(x)=0 for all x, which is continuous since limits equal function values.
Take V to be any nonempty open subset of R, for example V=R itself or an open interval like (−1,1).
Compute the image f(V), noting that every x in V is mapped to 0, so f(V)={0}.
Observe that {0} is not open in R because any open set containing 0 must include an interval around 0, while {0} does not include any other points.
Conclude that even though f is continuous and V is open, the image f(V)={0} fails to be open, giving the required example.

problem: Exercise 1.5.5: Let \( (R, d) \) be the real line with the standard metric. Give an example of a continuous function \( f: R \to R \), and a closed set \( F \subseteq R \), such that \( f(F) \) is not closed.
steps: Recall that a continuous image of a closed set need not be closed in general metric spaces, so we seek a specific example in the reals to exhibit this behaviour.
Consider the function \(f(x)=\arctan(x)\) defined on all of \(R\); this function is continuous because it is differentiable everywhere with derivative \(1/(1+x^2)\).
The domain \(R\) itself is closed in the standard metric on \(R\), so we may take \(F=R\) as our closed set.

Now analyze the image \(f(F)=\{\arctan(x): x\in R\}\). Since \(\arctan(x)\) increases strictly and \(\lim_{x	o\infty}\arctan(x)=\pi/2\), \(\lim_{x	o-\infty}\arctan(x)=-\pi/2\), it follows that \(f(F)=(-\pi/2,\pi/2)\).
The set \((-\pi/2,\pi/2)\) is not closed because its endpoints \(\pm\pi/2\) are limit points that are not contained in the set.
Therefore we have provided an example of a continuous function \(f\) and a closed set \(F\subseteq R\) such that \(f(F)\) is not closed.

problem: Exercise 1.5.12: Let \((X, d_{\text{disc}})\) be a metric space with the discrete metric \(d_{\text{disc}}\). (a) Show that \(X\) is always complete. (b) When is \(X\) compact, and when is \(X\) not compact? Prove your claim. (Hint: the Heine–Borel theorem will be useless here since that only applies to Euclidean spaces.)
steps: Recall that the discrete metric is defined by d_disc(x,y)=1 when x≠y and 0 when x=y, so any ball of radius less than 1 around a point contains only that point while balls of radius at least 1 contain the whole space.

To check completeness, consider an arbitrary Cauchy sequence (x_n); taking ε=1 shows that there is an N beyond which d_disc(x_m,x_n)<1, forcing x_m=x_n for all m,n≥N, so the tail of the sequence is constant.
Thus the sequence eventually equals some point x, and since x_n=x for all large n, the sequence converges to x, showing every Cauchy sequence converges in X.

For compactness, observe that in the discrete metric every singleton {x} is open, so any open cover of a finite set has a finite subcollection automatically (just pick one open set covering each point).
Therefore any finite discrete space is compact because we can always select finitely many open sets to cover each point.

If X is infinite, cover it by the collection of singleton balls {x} (for each x∈X), each of which is open, and note that no finite subcollection can cover X because finitely many singletons only cover finitely many points.
Hence an infinite discrete metric space is not compact, so X is compact if and only if it is finite.

problem: Exercise 2.2.3: Show that if \(f: X \to \mathbb{R}\) is a continuous function, so is the function \(|f|: X \to \mathbb{R}\) defined by \(|f|(x):= |f(x)|\).
steps: Recall that the composition of continuous functions is continuous, so it suffices to write |f| as a composition of simpler continuous maps.

Observe that the absolute value map a: ℝ→ℝ given by a(t)=|t| is continuous because it is continuous on (-∞,0] and [0,∞) and these restrictions agree at 0, which is a standard result for real functions.
Since f:X→ℝ is assumed continuous and a:ℝ→ℝ is continuous, the composition a∘f:X→ℝ is continuous by the general theorem on compositions of continuous functions.
By definition (a∘f)(x)=a(f(x))=|f(x)|, this shows that |f| equals the continuous composition a∘f.
Therefore |f|:X→ℝ is continuous, completing the proof.

problem: Exercise 2.2.4: Let \(\pi_1: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}\) and \(\pi_2: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}\) be the functions \(\pi_1(x, y):= x\) and \(\pi_2(x, y):= y\) (these two functions are sometimes called the coordinate functions on \(\mathbb{R}^2\)). Show that \(\pi_1\) and \(\pi_2\) are continuous. Conclude that if \(f: \mathbb{R} \to X\) is any continuous function into a metric space \((X, d)\), then the functions \(g_1: \mathbb{R}^2 \to X\) and \(g_2: \mathbb{R}^2 \to X\) defined by \(g_1(x, y):= f(x)\) and \(g_2(x, y):= f(y)\) are also continuous.
steps: Equip R^2 with the usual Euclidean distance d((x,y),(x0,y0)) = sqrt((x-x0)^2+(y-y0)^2), and recall that a function h from a metric space to another is continuous at a point when for every ε>0 there is δ>0 such that d(point, point0)<δ implies the target distance is less than ε.

Fix any point (x0,y0) in R^2 and any ε>0; then for a general point (x,y) we have |π1(x,y)-π1(x0,y0)| = |x-x0| ≤ sqrt((x-x0)^2+(y-y0)^2) = d((x,y),(x0,y0)), so choosing δ=ε ensures |π1(x,y)-π1(x0,y0)|<ε whenever d((x,y),(x0,y0))<δ, proving π1 is continuous everywhere.
A similar estimate holds for π2 since |π2(x,y)-π2(x0,y0)| = |y-y0| ≤ d((x,y),(x0,y0)), so the same δ=ε argument shows π2 is continuous as well.
Thus both coordinate functions π1 and π2 are continuous on R^2 with the standard metric.
Given any continuous f: R→X, define g1 = f∘π1 and g2 = f∘π2; continuity of π1 and π2 together with continuity of f implies each composition g1 and g2 is continuous on R^2.

problem: Exercise 2.2.9: Let \( f : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R} \) be a function, and let \( (x_0, y_0) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \). If \( f \) is continuous at \( (x_0, y_0) \), show that \( \lim_{x \to x_0} \limsup_{y \to y_0} f(x, y) = \lim_{x \to x_0} \liminf_{y \to y_0} f(x, y) = f(x_0, y_0) \) and also \( \lim_{y \to y_0} \limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x, y) = \lim_{y \to y_0} \liminf_{x \to x_0} f(x, y) = f(x_0, y_0) \). (Recall that \( \limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) := \inf_{r > 0} \sup_{|x - x_0| < r} f(x) \) and \( \liminf_{x \to x_0} f(x) := \sup_{r > 0} \inf_{|x - x_0| < r} f(x) \).) In particular, whenever both iterated limits exist, \( \lim_{x \to x_0} \lim_{y \to y_0} f(x, y) = \lim_{y \to y_0} \lim_{x \to x_0} f(x, y) \). (The iterated limits need not exist in general; for example, \( f(x, y) = y \sin \frac{1}{x} \) when \( xy \neq 0 \) and \( f(x, y) = 0 \) otherwise.) Discuss the comparison with the following example: for \( E = [1, 2) \subset \mathbb{R} \), one has \( \operatorname{int}(E) = (1, 2) \), \( \operatorname{ext}(E) = (-\infty, 1) \cup (2, \infty) \), and \( \partial E = \{1, 2\} \), so one boundary point lies in \( E \) and the other does not.
steps: Recall that continuity of f at (x0,y0) means that for every ε>0 there exists δ>0 such that |(x,y)-(x0,y0)|<δ implies |f(x,y)-f(x0,y0)|<ε, and in particular this controls f(x,y) whenever x is close to x0 and y close to y0.

Fix x near x0 and consider the function φ_y(x)=f(x,y) as y→y0; continuity implies that sup_{|y−y0|<r} f(x,y) and inf_{|y−y0|<r} f(x,y) both approach f(x,y0) as r→0, using the definitions; hence lim_{y→y0} f(x,y)=f(x,y0) for each fixed x near x0.
Now take x→x0 while keeping in mind that for each such x we have shown lim_{y→y0} f(x,y)=f(x,y0), so the outer limit lim_{x→x0}(lim_{y→y0} f(x,y)) becomes lim_{x→x0} f(x,y0).
Continuity in the x-variable at (x0,y0) now gives lim_{x→x0} f(x,y0)=f(x0,y0), so the iterated limit with y inner and x outer equals f(x0,y0).
By the same symmetry argument (now fixing y first and taking the limit in x), lim_{y→y0}(lim_{x→x0} f(x,y)) equals f(x0,y0) as well.
Since both iterated limits equal f(x0,y0), they equal each other, yielding lim_x lim_y f(x,y)=lim_y lim_x f(x,y)=f(x0,y0).

The result also matches the discussion in Example 1.2.7, where the existence of sequential limits could fail when the iterated limits disagree; here continuity prevents such pathologies and guarantees both iterated limits exist and coincide with f(x0,y0).

problem: Exercise 2.3.3: Show that every uniformly continuous function is continuous, but give an example that shows that not every continuous function is uniformly continuous.
steps: Recall that a function f: X → Y between metric spaces is uniformly continuous if for every ε > 0 there exists δ > 0 such that whenever d_X(x, y) < δ we have d_Y(f(x), f(y)) < ε, and the key point is that δ depends only on ε, not on x or y themselves.
Fix ε > 0 and consider a uniformly continuous function f. The δ that works for ε also works for any single point x₀ in X, so whenever d_X(x, x₀) < δ we also have d_Y(f(x), f(x₀)) < ε.
This shows that f is continuous at the arbitrary point x₀, because for the same ε we have found a δ linked to x₀ through the uniform continuity condition.
Since x₀ was arbitrary, f is continuous on all of X, proving that uniform continuity implies continuity.
To exhibit a continuous function that is not uniformly continuous, consider f: ℝ → ℝ defined by f(x) = x², which is continuous everywhere because it is a polynomial.
Suppose, for contradiction, that x² were uniformly continuous on ℝ, so there exists δ > 0 such that |x - y| < δ implies |x² - y²| < 1, say.

Choose x large, for example x = 1/δ, and take y = x + δ/2 so that |x - y| = δ/2 < δ, yet |x² - y²| = |(x - y)(x + y)| = (δ/2)(2x + δ/2) which grows without bound as x increases, contradicting the assumed bound of 1.
Therefore x² is not uniformly continuous on ℝ despite being continuous, completing the counterexample.

problem: Exercise 2.3.5: Let \((X, d_X)\) be a metric space, and let \(f: X \to \mathbb{R}\) and \(g: X \to \mathbb{R}\) be uniformly continuous functions. Show that the pairing \((f, g): X \to \mathbb{R}^2\) defined by \((f, g)(x):= (f(x), g(x))\) is uniformly continuous.
steps: By hypothesis f and g are uniformly continuous on X, so for each ε>0 there exists δε>0 such that d_X(x,y)<δε implies |f(x)-f(y)|<ε, and likewise there exists δ′ε>0 such that d_X(x,y)<δ′ε implies |g(x)-g(y)|<ε.
To study (f,g):X→R^2, recall the metric on R^2 is the Euclidean metric, so for two points (f(x),g(x)) and (f(y),g(y)) we have d_{R^2}((f(x),g(x)),(f(y),g(y)))=√((f(x)-f(y))^2+(g(x)-g(y))^2).
Using the standard inequality √(A^2+B^2)≤|A|+|B|, we obtain d_{R^2}((f(x),g(x)),(f(y),g(y)))≤|f(x)-f(y)|+|g(x)-g(y)|.
Given ε>0, choose δ= min{δε/2, δ′ε/2} say, so that whenever d_X(x,y)<δ we have simultaneously |f(x)-f(y)|<ε/2 and |g(x)-g(y)|<ε/2 by the respective uniform continuity assumptions.
Then for such x,y the inequality from step 3 yields d_{R^2}((f(x),g(x)),(f(y),g(y)))< ε/2+ε/2=ε.
Thus whenever d_X(x,y)<δ the image points are within ε in R^2, showing (f,g) is uniformly continuous.
