problem: Exercise 2.4.1: Let \((X, d_{disc})\) be a metric space with the discrete metric. Let \( E \) be a subset of \( X \) which contains at least two elements. Show that \( E \) is disconnected.
steps: To show that E is disconnected we exhibit two disjoint nonempty sets that are open in the subspace topology on E and whose union is E.
In the discrete metric every singleton {z} is open because the ball of radius 1/2 around z contains only z, so {z} is an open subset of X.
Since E has at least two elements we can pick two distinct points a and b in E.
Let A={a} and B=E\{a}; the set A is open in E because it is open in X, and B is open in E because its complement {a} is open in X so E\{a} inherits openness.
The set B is nonempty because it contains b, so both A and B are nonempty subsets of E.
The sets A and B are disjoint by construction since B does not contain a, the unique element of A.
Their union A∪B equals E because B consists of all other points of E beyond a.
Thus E is the union of two disjoint nonempty open sets in E, so E is disconnected.

problem: Exercise 2.4.7: Let \((X, d)\) be a metric space, and let \( E \) be a subset of \( X \). We say that \( E \) is path-connected iff, for every \( x, y \in E \), there exists a continuous function \( \gamma: [0, 1] \to E \) from the unit interval \([0, 1]\) to \( E \) such that \( \gamma(0) = x \) and \( \gamma(1) = y \). Show that every non-empty path-connected set is connected. (The converse is false, but is a bit tricky to show and will not be detailed here.)
steps: Assume toward a contradiction that E is path-connected but not connected, so there are disjoint open sets U and V in X with E ⊂ U ∪ V and both E ∩ U and E ∩ V nonempty.
Choose x ∈ E ∩ U and y ∈ E ∩ V; path-connectedness gives a continuous γ: [0,1] → E with γ(0) = x and γ(1) = y.
Then γ−1(E ∩ U) and γ−1(E ∩ V) are open in [0,1] because γ is continuous and E ∩ U, E ∩ V are intersections of E with open sets in X.
These two inverse images are disjoint (since U and V are disjoint) and their union covers [0,1] because γ takes values in E ⊂ U ∪ V.
Moreover 0 ∈ γ−1(E ∩ U) and 1 ∈ γ−1(E ∩ V), so both inverse images are nonempty, contradicting the fact that [0,1] is connected and cannot be split into two disjoint nonempty open sets.
This contradiction shows the assumption was false, so E must be connected.

problem: Exercise 2.4.9: Let \( (X, d) \) be a metric space. Define a relation \( x \sim y \) on \( X \) by declaring \( x \sim y \) iff there exists a connected subset of \( X \) which contains both \( x \) and \( y \). Show that this is an equivalence relation (that is, it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive). Also show that the equivalence classes \( \{ y \in X : y \sim x \} \) are all closed and connected. You may use the following facts: (i) if \( (E_{\alpha})_{\alpha \in I} \) is a non-empty collection of connected subsets of \( X \) with non-empty common intersection, then \( \bigcup_{\alpha \in I} E_{\alpha} \) is connected; and (ii) if \( E \subseteq X \) is connected, then its closure \( \overline{E} \) is also connected. These equivalence classes are called the connected components of \( X \).
steps: We verify reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity by noting that any point x lies in a singleton set {x}, which is connected, so x∼x. If x∼y via some connected set C, then C also contains y and x, giving y∼x, so the relation is symmetric. For transitivity suppose x∼y and y∼z with connected sets C1 containing x,y and C2 containing y,z; then C1∪C2 is connected by Exercise 2.4.6 because C1∩C2 contains y, so x and z lie in a common connected set and x∼z.

Thus ∼ is an equivalence relation and we can consider the equivalence class E(x)={y:y∼x}. We now show E(x) is connected. If we could write E(x) as a union of two separated nonempty open-in-the-subspace subsets, then since x lies in one, there would be a connected set containing x and a point of the other side, contradicting the maximality of E(x) as those points satisfy the relation with x.

More precisely, suppose E(x)=U∪V with U and V nonempty disjoint relatively open subsets. Then picking y∈V, the connected set producing y∼x would have to intersect U because it contains x, implying it cannot be contained in V alone, which contradicts the separation, so no such separation exists and E(x) is connected.

Next we prove E(x) is closed in X. Let y be a limit point of E(x) with (y_n) in E(x) converging to y. Each y_n is related to x, so for every n there is a connected set C_n containing x and y_n. We can consider the union C=⋃_n C_n which is connected by applying Exercise 2.4.8 inductively to unions with common points, so C contains x and all y_n. Passing to the closure, we see y lies in the closure of a connected set containing x, but since connected sets are closed under closure in metric spaces (again from Exercise 2.4.6 or 2.4.8), the closure is also connected and contains x,y, hence y∈E(x).
Therefore E(x) contains all its limit points, so E(x) is closed.
Finally, since each equivalence class E(x) is connected and closed, we have shown that the connected components of X are precisely these equivalence classes.

problem: Exercise 3.4.4: Let \( (X, d_X) \) and \( (Y, d_Y) \) be metric spaces, and let \( Y^X := \{ f \mid f : X \to Y \} \) be the space of all functions from \( X \) to \( Y \). If \( x_0 \in X \) and \( V \subseteq Y \) is open, define \( V^{(x_0)} := \{ f \in Y^X : f(x_0) \in V \}. \) If \( E \subseteq Y^X \), say that \( E \) is open if for every \( f \in E \) there exist finitely many points \( x_1, \ldots, x_n \in X \) and open sets \( V_1, \ldots, V_n \subseteq Y \) such that \( f \in V_1^{(x_1)} \cap \cdots \cap V_n^{(x_n)} \subseteq E. \) (a) Show that if \( \mathcal{F} \) is the collection of such open sets, then \( (Y^X, \mathcal{F}) \) is a topological space. (b) For a sequence of functions \( f^{(n)} : X \to Y \) and another function \( f : X \to Y \), show that \( f^{(n)} \to f \) in the topology \( \mathcal{F} \), meaning that for every open neighborhood \( U \in \mathcal{F} \) of \( f \) there exists \( N \) such that \( f^{(n)} \in U \) for all \( n \ge N \), if and only if \( f^{(n)} \to f \) pointwise, meaning that for every \( x \in X \) and every \( \varepsilon > 0 \) there exists \( N \) such that \( d_Y(f^{(n)}(x), f(x)) < \varepsilon \) for all \( n \ge N \). The topology \( \mathcal{F} \) is called the topology of pointwise convergence, or the product topology.
steps: To check that Σ, the collection of subsets declared open by the exercise, is a topology we verify the three axioms: the empty set and whole space belong, arbitrary unions belong, and finite intersections belong.

The empty set vacuously satisfies the defining property because there is no point f to consider, while the whole space Y^X contains each f and we can cover each f by the trivial intersection of zero basic sets, so both extremes lie in Σ.

For an arbitrary union E = ∪ E_i of sets in Σ, each f ∈ E lands in some E_i, and by the openness of that E_i we can find finitely many points x_j and open Y-sets V_j with f ∈ ∩ V_j^{(x_j)} ⊆ E_i ⊆ E, so the union is open.

For a finite intersection E = E_1 ∩ · · · ∩ E_m, given f ∈ E we can find for each E_k the finite intersection U_k of the indicated basic neighbourhoods around f. Taking the intersection of the finitely many U_k still produces a basic intersection of finitely many V^{(x)}-type sets that contains f and lies in each E_k, hence in E.
Thus Σ satisfies the topology axioms, so (Y^X, Σ) is a topological space.

To relate convergence in this topology to pointwise convergence, recall that a sequence f^{(n)} converges to f in the topology exactly when for every open set E containing f there exists N so that f^{(n)} ∈ E for all n ≥ N.

Given such an open neighbourhood of f we may take it to be a basic intersection V_1^{(x_1)} ∩ · · · ∩ V_k^{(x_k)} by the definition, so convergence means eventually f^{(n)}(x_j) ∈ V_j for each j, i.e. f^{(n)} eventually lies in every basic neighbourhood of f determined by finitely many points.
Because the V_j were arbitrary open sets containing f(x_j), this condition forces for each fixed point x that f^{(n)}(x) converges to f(x) in Y, hence pointwise convergence.

Conversely, if f^{(n)} converges pointwise, then for any basic neighbourhood of f determined by finitely many x_j and their respective open V_j we can find N_j such that f^{(n)}(x_j) ∈ V_j for all n ≥ N_j, and taking N = max N_j ensures f^{(n)} lies in the neighbourhood for all n ≥ N.
Therefore the sequence converges to f in the topology Σ, showing pointwise convergence is equivalent to convergence in the topology of pointwise convergence.

problem: Exercise 3.2.4: Let \(f_n : X \to Y\) be a sequence of bounded functions from one metric space \((X, d_X)\) to another metric space \((Y, d_Y)\). Suppose that \(f_n\) converges uniformly to another function \(f : X \to Y\). Suppose that \(f\) is a bounded function; i.e., there exists a ball \(B_{(Y, d_Y)}(y_0, R)\) in \(Y\) such that \(f(x) \in B_{(Y, d_Y)}(y_0, R)\) for all \(x \in X\). Show that the sequence \(f_n\) is uniformly bounded; i.e., there exists a ball \(B_{(Y, d_Y)}(y_0, R)\) in \(Y\) such that \(f_n(x) \in B_{(Y, d_Y)}(y_0, R)\) for all \(x \in X\) and all positive integers \(n\).
steps: Since \(f_n\) converges uniformly to \(f\), there exists \(N\) such that for all \(n \geq N\) and every \(x\in X\) we have \(d_Y(f_n(x),f(x))<1\).
By hypothesis \(f\) is bounded in the ball \(B_{(Y,d_Y)}(y_0,R)\), so for these \(n\) and any \(x\) we can estimate \(d_Y(f_n(x),y_0) \leq d_Y(f_n(x),f(x))+d_Y(f(x),y_0)<1+R\).
Thus for all \(n\geq N\) we have \(f_n(x)\in B_{(Y,d_Y)}(y_0,R+1)\).
For the finitely many indices \(n=1,\dots,N-1\) each \(f_n\) is bounded, so for each \(n\) there is a radius \(R_n\) with \(d_Y(f_n(x),y_0)\leq R_n\) for all \(x\).
Set \(R_{\max} = \max\{R+1,R_1,\dots,R_{N-1}\}\); then for every \(n\) and every \(x\) we have \(d_Y(f_n(x),y_0)\leq R_{\max}\).
Hence all \(f_n\) take values in the uniform ball \(B_{(Y,d_Y)}(y_0,R_{\max})\), so the sequence is uniformly bounded.

problem: Exercise 3.1.6: State and prove metric-space analogues of the usual real-variable limit laws. Concretely, let \( E \) be a subset of a metric space \( X \), let \( x_0 \) be a limit point of \( E \), and let \( f, g : E \to \mathbb{R} \) have limits \( \lim_{x \to x_0; x \in E} f(x) = L \) and \( \lim_{x \to x_0; x \in E} g(x) = M \). Show that \( cf \to cL \) for every real constant \( c \), \( f + g \to L + M \), \( f - g \to L - M \), and \( fg \to LM \); and if \( g(x) \neq 0 \) for all \( x \in E \) and \( M \neq 0 \), show that \( f/g \to L/M \). (Hint: you may use the fact that for real-valued functions on a metric space, sums, differences, products, scalar multiples, max/min, and quotients by non-vanishing denominators preserve continuity.)
steps: Recall that Proposition 9.3.14 asserted the usual arithmetic limit laws for sequences of real-valued functions defined on a subset of ℝ; the same conclusions hold when the functions are defined on a metric space (X,d_X) because all that was used in the proof was sequential convergence.

State the analogue: if f,g: X→ℝ and c is a real constant, and if lim_{x→a}f(x)=L and lim_{x→a}g(x)=M for some a∈X (meaning every sequence x_n→a satisfies f(x_n)→L and g(x_n)→M), then lim_{x→a}(f+g)=L+M, lim_{x→a}(cf)=cL, lim_{x→a}(fg)=LM, and, provided M≠0 and g(x)≠0 near a, lim_{x→a}(f/g)=L/M.

To prove the sum law, take any sequence (x_n) in X converging to a. By the assumption and Corollary 2.2.3 (which ensures limits behave under sequential characterization), f(x_n)→L and g(x_n)→M, so f(x_n)+g(x_n)→L+M by the usual real-number limit laws, hence lim_{x→a}(f+g)=L+M.
The constant multiple law follows similarly: if f(x_n)→L, then c f(x_n)→cL, giving lim_{x→a}(cf)=cL.
For the product, again take x_n→a, deduce f(x_n)→L and g(x_n)→M, and then apply the real-number product rule to conclude f(x_n)g(x_n)→LM, so lim_{x→a}(fg)=LM.

For the quotient, assume additionally that g(x)≠0 in a punctured neighborhood of a and M≠0; then for any x_n→a we have g(x_n)→M≠0, so eventually g(x_n) is nonzero and 1/g(x_n)→1/M; hence f(x_n)/g(x_n)=(f(x_n))(1/g(x_n))→L·(1/M)=L/M, giving lim_{x→a}(f/g)=L/M.

These arguments rely only on the sequential description of limits in metric spaces, so the proofs are identical to the subset-of-ℝ case once we replace sequences in ℝ with sequences in X converging to a.

problem: Exercise 3.4.1: Let \( (X, d_X) \) and \( (Y, d_Y) \) be metric spaces. Define \( B(X \to Y) := \{ f : X \to Y \mid f \text{ is bounded} \}. \) If \( X \) is non-empty, define \( d_{B(X \to Y)}(f, g) := \sup_{x \in X} d_Y(f(x), g(x)); \) if \( X \) is empty, define \( d_{B(X \to Y)}(f, g) := 0 \). Show that \( B(X \to Y) \), equipped with this metric, is indeed a metric space.
steps: Recall that B(X → Y) consists of bounded functions f : X → Y, and the metric d_{B(X → Y)}(f, g) is defined as the supremum over x in X of d_Y(f(x), g(x)); we need to check the metric axioms for this assignment.
For any f and g in B(X → Y), each value d_Y(f(x), g(x)) is nonnegative since d_Y is a metric, so the supremum is also nonnegative; hence d_{B(X → Y)}(f, g) ≥ 0.

If f = g, then for every x we have d_Y(f(x), g(x)) = 0, so the supremum is zero and d_{B(X → Y)}(f, g) = 0; conversely, if d_{B(X → Y)}(f, g) = 0, then the supremum of nonnegative numbers is zero, implying each d_Y(f(x), g(x)) = 0, so f(x) = g(x) for all x and thus f = g.
For symmetry, since d_Y(f(x), g(x)) = d_Y(g(x), f(x)) for each x, the supremum giving d_{B(X → Y)}(f, g) equals the supremum defining d_{B(X → Y)}(g, f), so the metric is symmetric.

To check the triangle inequality, take f, g, h in B(X → Y); for any x we know d_Y(f(x), h(x)) ≤ d_Y(f(x), g(x)) + d_Y(g(x), h(x)), so taking the supremum over x yields d_{B(X → Y)}(f, h) ≤ d_{B(X → Y)}(f, g) + d_{B(X → Y)}(g, h).
We have now verified non-negativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and the triangle inequality for d_{B(X → Y)}, so it satisfies all metric axioms.
Therefore B(X → Y) equipped with d_{B(X → Y)} is a metric space.

problem: Exercise 3.5.1: Let \( f^{(1)}, \ldots, f^{(N)} \) be a finite sequence of bounded functions from a metric space \((X, d_X)\) to \(\mathbb{R}\). Show that \(\sum_{i=1}^{N} f^{(i)}\) is also bounded. Prove a similar claim when “bounded” is replaced by “continuous”. What if “continuous” was replaced by “uniformly continuous”?
steps: Since each f^{(i)} is bounded, for each i there exists M_i >= 0 such that |f^{(i)}(x)| ≤ M_i for all x in X.
Then for every x in X, the triangle inequality gives |Σ_{i=1}^N f^{(i)}(x)| ≤ Σ_{i=1}^N |f^{(i)}(x)|.
Using the bounds, that is ≤ Σ_{i=1}^N M_i which is a finite sum, so every x has |Σ_{i=1}^N f^{(i)}(x)| bounded by M := Σ M_i.
Thus the finite sum is bounded by M, completing the first claim.

For continuity, observe that each f^{(i)} is continuous and finite sums of continuous functions are continuous because the sum map is continuous with respect to the product topology or directly via ε-δ estimates.
More concretely, fix x_0 in X and ε > 0; since each f^{(i)} is continuous there is δ_i such that d_X(x,x_0) < δ_i implies |f^{(i)}(x) - f^{(i)}(x_0)| < ε/N.
Let δ = min_i δ_i; then whenever d_X(x,x_0) < δ we have |Σ f^{(i)}(x) - Σ f^{(i)}(x_0)| ≤ Σ |f^{(i)}(x) - f^{(i)}(x_0)| < N·(ε/N) = ε, so the sum is continuous.

Replacing continuity with uniform continuity, the same proof works by using the common δ provided by uniform continuity: for each i there is δ_i so that d_X(x,y) < δ_i implies |f^{(i)}(x) - f^{(i)}(y)| < ε/N and taking δ = min_i δ_i shows the sum is uniformly continuous.

problem: Exercise 3.8.5: Let \( f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} \) and \( g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} \) be continuous, compactly supported functions. Suppose that \( f \) is supported on the interval \([0, 1]\), and \( g \) is constant on the interval \([0, 2]\) (i.e., there is a real number \( c \) such that \( g(x) = c \) for all \( x \in [0, 2] \)). Show that the convolution \( f * g \) is constant on the interval \([1, 2]\).
steps: Since \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous and compactly supported, their convolution \(f*g\) is well-defined everywhere, and we can compute \((f*g)(x)=\int_{\mathbb{R}}f(t)g(x-t)\,dt\).
Because \(f\) is supported on \([0,1]\), the integrand is nonzero only when \(t\in[0,1]\); outside this interval \(f(t)=0\), so the convolution reduces to an integral over \([0,1]\).
If \(x\in[1,2]\), then \(x-t\in[x-1,x]\subset[0,2]\) whenever \(t\in[0,1]\), so on that range of integration \(g(x-t)=c\) by the constant assumption.
Thus for each \(x\in[1,2]\) we have \((f*g)(x)=\int_0^1 f(t)c\,dt=c\int_0^1 f(t)\,dt\), which does not depend on \(x\).
Therefore \(f*g\) is constant on \([1,2]\), specifically equal to \(c\int_0^1 f(t)\,dt\), as required.

problem: Exercise 4.5.2: Show that for every integer \(n \geq 3\), we have \[ 0 < \frac{1}{(n + 1)!} + \frac{1}{(n + 2)!} + \ldots < \frac{1}{n!}. \] (Hint: first show that \((n + k)! > 2^k n!\) for all \(k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\).) Conclude that \(n!e\) is not an integer for every \(n \geq 3\). Deduce from this that \(e\) is irrational. (Hint: prove by contradiction.)
steps: Fix n≥3 and consider the tail T=1/(n+1)!+1/(n+2)!+⋯. Using the hint, we prove by induction on k that (n+k)!>2^k n! for every k≥1, so that 1/(n+k)!<1/(2^k n!).
Summing these inequalities yields T<1/n! (1/2+1/4+1/8+⋯)=1/n! since the geometric series sums to 1, and each term of T is positive, so 0<T<1/n!.
Combining these inequalities we conclude 0<1/(n+1)!+1/(n+2)!+⋯<1/n!, establishing the desired bound.
Now write e=1+1+1/2!+⋯+1/n!+T, so that n!e is equal to the integer n! (1+1+1/2!+⋯+1/n!) plus the positive remainder n!T.
But from the bound on T we have 0<n!T<1, hence n!e is not an integer because it lies strictly between the nearest integers.
Assume for contradiction that e is rational, say e=a/b with coprime positive integers a,b. Take n≥max(3,b), so that b divides n! and thus n!e=n!a/b is an integer.
This contradicts the result that n!e is not an integer, so our assumption must be false, and e is irrational.

problem: Exercise 4.5.6: Prove that the natural logarithm function is real analytic on \( (0, +\infty) \).
steps: Fix a point a>0. To show ln is real analytic at a, we need a power series centered at a with positive radius of convergence that equals ln x for x in some open interval around a.
Write x=a+u and consider the identity ln x = ln a + ln(1+u/a), so the new variable t=u/a satisfies x=a(1+t), and the domain x>0 becomes t>-1.
Recall the classical power series ln(1+t)=∑_{n≥1}(-1)^{n-1}t^n/n, which converges for |t|<1, so ln x = ln a + ∑_{n≥1}(-1)^{n-1}(x-a)^n/(n a^n) when |x-a|<a, i.e., when |t|<1.
Because this series has radius a>0, it defines a real analytic function in a neighborhood of a, and the coefficients depend on a but the structure shows ln matches a convergent power series around a.
Since every point a>0 has such a neighborhood, the natural logarithm is real analytic on the whole interval (0,∞).

problem: Exercise 4.7.2: Let \(f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) be a function which is differentiable at \(x_0\), with \(f(x_0) = 0\) and \(f'(x_0) \neq 0\). Show that there exists a \(c > 0\) such that \(f(y)\) is nonzero whenever \(0 < |x_0 - y| < c\). Conclude in particular that there exists a \(c > 0\) such that \(\sin(x) \neq 0\) for all \(0 < x < c\).
steps: Because f is differentiable at x_0 with f(x_0)=0 and f′(x_0)≠0, the limit L = lim_{y→x_0}(f(y)−f(x_0))/(y−x_0) exists and equals f′(x_0)≠0, so there is a δ>0 such that whenever 0<|y−x_0|<δ the quotient lies within, say, half the magnitude of f′(x_0), ensuring |(f(y)−f(x_0))/(y−x_0)|> |f′(x_0)|/2.

This uniform bound on the difference quotient gives |f(y)| = |y−x_0|·|(f(y)−f(x_0))/(y−x_0)| ≥ |y−x_0|·|f′(x_0)|/2, so f(y) cannot vanish as long as 0<|y−x_0|<δ because the right-hand side remains positive.
Thus we can take c = δ to conclude there is a neighborhood around x_0, excluding x_0 itself, where f(y)≠0, which proves the general claim.
For the particular application, consider sin(x) at x_0=0: sin is differentiable everywhere, sin(0)=0, and sin′(0)=cos(0)=1≠0.
By the proved statement there exists c>0 such that sin(x)≠0 whenever 0<|x|<c, hence sin(x) stays nonzero on the indicated punctured interval.
This completes the demonstration that nonvanishing of f near a point with nonzero derivative follows from the differentiability structure, and yields the desired result for sin(x).

problem: Exercise 4.6.5: If \(z\) is a complex number, show that \(\Re(z) = \frac{z + \bar{z}}{2}\) and \(\Im(z) = \frac{z - \bar{z}}{2i}\).
steps: Write z = x + iy with x and y real, so by definition Re(z) = x and Im(z) = y.
Recall that the complex conjugate of z is ̅z = x - iy because conjugation flips the sign of the imaginary part.
Add z and ̅z to get z + ̅z = (x + iy) + (x - iy) = 2x, so the sum eliminates the imaginary terms.
Divide by 2 to see that (z + ̅z)/2 = x, which is exactly Re(z).
Subtract ̅z from z to compute z - ̅z = (x + iy) - (x - iy) = 2iy, leaving only the imaginary contribution.
Divide by 2i to give (z - ̅z)/(2i) = y, which matches Im(z).
Thus both desired formulas are verified in terms of the definitions of real and imaginary parts.

problem: Exercise 4.7.8: Let \( \tan: (-\pi/2, \pi/2) \to \mathbb{R} \) be the tangent function \( \tan(x):= \sin(x)/\cos(x) \). Show that \( \tan \) is differentiable and monotone increasing, with \( \frac{d}{dx} \tan(x) = 1 + \tan(x)^2 \), and that \( \lim_{x \to \pi/2} \tan(x) = +\infty \) and \( \lim_{x \to -\pi/2} \tan(x) = -\infty \). Conclude that \( \tan \) is in fact a bijection from \( (-\pi/2, \pi/2) \to \mathbb{R} \), and thus has an inverse function \( \tan^{-1}: \mathbb{R} \to (-\pi/2, \pi/2) \) (this function is called the arctangent function). Show that \( \tan^{-1} \) is differentiable and \( \frac{d}{dx} \tan^{-1}(x) = \frac{1}{1+x^2} \).
steps: The tangent is defined as sin/cos on (-π/2, π/2), where cosine never vanishes, so the quotient rule gives tan′(x)=(cos(x) cos(x)+sin(x) sin(x))/cos(x)^2 = 1/cos^2(x), hence tan′ is strictly positive and tan is differentiable and increasing.
Since cos^2(x)=1/(1+tan^2(x)), the derivative formula can be rewritten as tan′(x)=1+tan^2(x), matching the desired expression for the derivative.

As x approaches π/2 from below, sin(x) tends to 1 while cos(x) tends to 0 through positive values, so tan(x)=sin(x)/cos(x) tends to +∞; similarly approaching -π/2 yields tan(x)→-∞ because cos(x)→0- and sin(x)→-1.
Thus tan is continuous, strictly increasing, and its limits at the endpoints are ±∞, so it is a bijection from (-π/2, π/2) onto ℝ, allowing us to define the inverse tan^{-1}: ℝ→(-π/2, π/2).

Because tan is differentiable with nonzero derivative everywhere, the inverse function theorem applies on the interval, so tan^{-1} is differentiable with (tan^{-1})′(x)=1/tan′(tan^{-1}(x))=1/(1+tan(tan^{-1}(x))^2)=1/(1+x^2).

problem: Exercise 4.6.8: Let \(z, w\) be nonzero complex numbers. Show that \(|z + w| = |z| + |w|\) if and only if there exists a positive real number \(c > 0\) such that \(z = cw\).
steps: Let z and w be nonzero complex numbers and assume |z + w| = |z| + |w|. First observe that the triangle inequality gives |z + w| ≤ |z| + |w|, with equality only when z and w point in the same direction in the complex plane.
Because equality is achieved, the vectors represented by z and w must be colinear with positive real multiples, so we can write z = cw for some real c ≥ 0.
Since z ≠ 0 and w ≠ 0, the scalar c cannot be zero, so c > 0, which shows that the equality condition implies the existence of a positive scalar c with z = cw.
Conversely, assume there exists c > 0 such that z = cw. Then z + w = (c + 1)w, so |z + w| = |c + 1| |w|.
Because c + 1 is positive, |c + 1| = c + 1, hence |z + w| = (c + 1) |w| = c |w| + |w| = |z| + |w|, using that |z| = c |w|.
Thus the condition |z + w| = |z| + |w| holds if and only if z is a positive real multiple of w.

problem: Part 1, Theorem 1.2: Each non-empty affine set \( M \) is parallel to a unique subspace \( L \). This \( L \) is given by \[ L = M - M = \{x - y \mid x \in M, y \in M\}. \]
steps: Since M is non-empty, pick an element a in M to serve as a reference point on the affine set.
Define L := M - a = {x - a: x in M}; this set is a translation of M that contains the origin by construction.

Show that L is a subspace: it contains 0, is closed under addition because (x - a) + (y - a) = (x + y - 2a) which lies in L since x + y = z + a for some z in M by affine closure, and is closed under scalar multiplication by observing λ(x - a) = (λx - a) + (a - λa) lies in L when M is affine and contains λx + (1 - λ)a, deducing closure properties.
Verify that M is parallel to L, meaning M = a + L, by checking that every point y in M can be written as a + (y - a) with y - a in L, and conversely every point of a + L lies in M by definition of L.
Establish uniqueness: suppose another subspace L' satisfies M = a + L'. Then L' = M - a = L because translating by the same a yields the same set, so the parallel subspace is uniquely determined.
Conclude that each non-empty affine set M is parallel to the unique subspace L = M - a, independent of the choice of a since translating by any other point in M yields the same subspace.

problem: Part 1, Theorem 2.4: The dimension of a convex set \( C \) is the maximum of the dimensions of the various simplices included in \( C \).
steps: Let \(C\) be a convex set in a finite-dimensional space. By definition \(\dim C=\dim \operatorname{aff} C\), so the dimension of \(C\) is the dimension of the smallest affine space that contains it.

Every simplex \(S\) contained in \(C\) lies in \(\operatorname{aff} C\), hence its dimension is at most \(\dim C\); this shows that the supremum over dimensions of simplices in \(C\) cannot exceed \(\dim C\).

To see the reverse inequality, take a point \(x_0\) that lies in the relative interior of \(C\). Around \(x_0\) one can find a small simplex \(S\) whose vertices are in \(C\) and whose affine hull equals \(\operatorname{aff} C\), because the relative interior is full-dimensional inside \(\operatorname{aff} C\).

Concretely, choose a basis \(\{v_1,\dots,v_k\}\) of the linear space parallel to \(\operatorname{aff} C\) and consider the simplex spanned by \(x_0\) and the points \(x_0+\varepsilon v_i\); for \(\varepsilon>0\) small enough these points stay in \(C\) by convexity and the definition of relative interior.
This simplex has dimension \(k=\dim\operatorname{aff} C=\dim C\), so there exists a simplex of full dimension in \(C\).
Therefore the maximum of the dimensions of simplices in \(C\) is at least \(\dim C\).
Combining both inequalities, we conclude that the dimension of \(C\) equals the maximum simplex dimension inside \(C\), as claimed.

problem: Part 3, Corollary 11.4.2: Let \( C_1 \) and \( C_2 \) be non-empty convex sets in \( R^n \) whose closures are disjoint. If either set is bounded, there exists a hyperplane separating \( C_1 \) and \( C_2 \) strongly.
steps: Choose the bounded set among C₁ and C₂; without loss of generality assume C₁ is bounded so that its closure is compact while cl C₂ is closed, and note that the closures are disjoint by assumption.

Since cl C₁ and cl C₂ are disjoint with one compact, the distance d = inf{‖p − q‖ : p ∈ cl C₁, q ∈ cl C₂} is positive and attained at some pair x* ∈ cl C₁ and y* ∈ cl C₂ because compactness and closedness guarantee a minimizer.
Define a = y* − x*, which is nonzero because d > 0, and observe that a points in the direction of the closest approach between the two sets.
Use the convexity of cl C₁: the projection condition at x* implies ⟨a, x − x*⟩ ≤ 0 for every x ∈ C₁, hence ⟨a, x⟩ ≤ ⟨a, x*⟩ for all x ∈ C₁.
Similarly, convexity of cl C₂ yields ⟨a, y − y*⟩ ≥ 0 for every y ∈ C₂, so ⟨a, y⟩ ≥ ⟨a, y*⟩ for all y ∈ C₂.
The gap ⟨a, y*⟩ − ⟨a, x*⟩ = ⟨a, y* − x*⟩ = ‖a‖² is strictly positive, so there is a real number b between ⟨a, x*⟩ and ⟨a, y*⟩.
With that b we have ⟨a, x⟩ ≤ ⟨a, x*⟩ < b < ⟨a, y*⟩ ≤ ⟨a, y⟩ for every x ∈ C₁, y ∈ C₂, hence the hyperplane {z : ⟨a, z⟩ = b} strongly separates C₁ from C₂.
If instead C₂ were the bounded set we would swap the roles of the sets in the above argument and obtain the same conclusion.

problem: Part 2, Theorem 7.4: Let \( f \) be a proper convex function on \( R^n \). Then \( cl f \) is a closed proper convex function. Moreover, \( cl f \) agrees with \( f \) except perhaps at relative boundary points of \( \text{dom} f \).
steps: Define the closure \(cl f\) by declaring its epigraph to be the topological closure of the epigraph of \(f\); that is, \( 	ext{epi}(cl f) := \overline{	ext{epi} f} \). This definition matches the usual lower semicontinuous hull and makes \(cl f\) the largest lower semicontinuous function majorized by \(f\).

Since \(	ext{epi} f\) is convex and the closure of a convex set is again convex, \(	ext{epi}(cl f)\) is convex, hence \(cl f\) is convex. Likewise, \(	ext{epi}(cl f)\) is closed by construction, so \(cl f\) is closed.

Properness follows because \(f\) is proper: there is some \(x_0\) with \(f(x_0)<\infty\) and \(f>-\infty\). Then \((x_0,f(x_0))\in 	ext{epi} f\subset 	ext{epi}(cl f)\), so \(cl f(x_0)\) is finite too, and there is no \(-\infty\) value hiding in the closure. Hence \(cl f\) is proper.

Because \(	ext{epi} f\subset 	ext{epi}(cl f)\), the infimums over the vertical slices satisfy \(cl f(x)=\inf\{y:(x,y)\in 	ext{epi}(cl f)\}\le \inf\{y:(x,y)\in 	ext{epi} f\}=f(x)\), so \(cl f\le f\) pointwise.

Now let \(x\) lie in the relative interior of \(	ext{dom} f\). Then there is a small ball \(B\) around \(x\) (inside the affine hull of the domain) such that \(B\subset 	ext{dom} f\); because \(B\) is bounded and lies away from the boundary, the values \(f(z)\) stay finite and even uniformly bounded above on \(B\). Thus the cylinder \(B	imes [M,\infty)\) for some large \(M\) lives inside the epigraph, so \(	ext{epi}(cl f)\) has nonempty interior and a supporting hyperplane at the boundary point \((x,f(x))\) exists. This supporting hyperplane gives a subgradient \(g\in \partial f(x)\) and hence the affine inequality \(f(z)\ge f(x)+\langle g,z-xangle\) for all \(z\).

Because the inequality persists for every \(z\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\), any sequence \((x_k,y_k)	o (x,y)\) in \(	ext{epi} f\) satisfies \(y_k\ge f(x_k)\ge f(x)+\langle g,x_k-xangle\), so taking limits one gets \(y\ge f(x)\). Therefore every \((x,y)\in 	ext{epi}(cl f)\) has \(y\ge f(x)\), which shows \(cl f(x)\ge f(x)\) on the relative interior.

Combining the inequalities \(cl f(x)\le f(x)\) and \(cl f(x)\ge f(x)\) for \(x\in 	ext{ri dom} f\), we conclude \(cl f=f\) on the relative interior, and the only possible changes occur at relative boundary points. Thus \(cl f\) is a closed proper convex function that agrees with \(f\) except, perhaps, at the relative boundary of \(	ext{dom} f\).

problem: Part 2, Corollary 7.4.2: If \( f \) is a proper convex function such that \( \text{dom} f \) is an affine set (which is true in particular if \( f \) is finite throughout \( R^n \)), then \( f \) is closed.
steps: Fix some point \(x_0\in\operatorname{dom}f\); since \(\operatorname{dom}f\) is affine there is a linear subspace \(L\) such that \(\operatorname{dom}f=x_0+L\).
Define \(h\colon L	o\mathbb R\) by \(h(u)=f(x_0+u)\); this function is finite everywhere on \(L\) because every translate \(x_0+u\) lies in the domain of \(f\).
Because \(f\) is convex, \(h\) is convex on \(L\), and any convex function that is finite on an entire linear space is continuous, so the epigraph of \(h\) is closed in \(L	imes\mathbb R\).

The epigraph of \(f\) can be written as \(\{(x_0+u,t):u\in L,\,t\ge h(u)\}\), so it is the image of \(\operatorname{epi}h\) under the homeomorphism \((u,t)\mapsto(x_0+u,t)\) from \(L	imes\mathbb R\) onto \((x_0+L)	imes\mathbb R\); hence \(\operatorname{epi}f\) is closed in \((x_0+L)	imes\mathbb R\).

Since \(x_0+L=\operatorname{dom}f\) is affine, the product \((x_0+L)	imes\mathbb R\) is a closed subset of \(\mathbb R^n	imes\mathbb R\), so any closed subset of it, in particular \(\operatorname{epi}f\), is closed in the ambient space.
Thus the epigraph of \(f\) is closed in \(\mathbb R^n	imes\mathbb R\), which means \(f\) is a closed convex function.

problem: Part 2, Theorem 8.4: A non-empty closed convex set \( C \) in \( R^n \) is bounded if and only if its recession cone \( 0^+ C \) consists of the zero vector alone.
steps: Let C be a non-empty closed convex subset of R^n and recall that the recession cone 0^+C consists of those directions d such that x+td ∈ C for every x ∈ C and every scalar t ≥ 0; this cone is itself convex and closed.
Assume first that C is bounded and pick any d ∈ 0^+C; for a fixed x_0 in C the whole ray x_0+td remains in C for all t ≥ 0, so the boundedness of C forces the norm of x_0+td to stay bounded as t→∞.
The boundedness of x_0+td as t grows implies that the unbounded term t d cannot contribute, hence d must be zero; this shows 0^+C contains only the zero vector.
Conversely, assume that 0^+C = {0} and suppose toward a contradiction that C is unbounded; then there exists a sequence {x_k} in C whose norms tend to infinity.
Fix some x_0 ∈ C and consider the normalized directions d_k := (x_k − x_0)/||x_k − x_0||; by compactness of the unit sphere there is a subsequence converging to some nonzero d.

For any t ≥ 0, convexity of C ensures x_0 + t d is the limit of x_0 + t d_{k_j}, and since each x_0 + t d_{k_j} lies in C (being convex combinations of x_0 and x_{k_j}), closedness of C implies x_0 + t d ∈ C, so d ∈ 0^+C; this contradicts the assumption that only the zero vector lies in the recession cone.
Therefore C cannot be unbounded and must be bounded, completing the equivalence.

problem: Part 2, Theorem 8.7: Let \( f \) be a closed proper convex function. Then all the non-empty level sets of the form \(\{x | f(x) \leq \lambda\}, \lambda \in \mathbb{R}\), have the same recession cone and the same lineality space, namely the recession cone and the constancy space of \( f \), respectively.
steps: Fix a closed proper convex function f and a scalar λ such that the sublevel set Cλ={x|f(x)≤λ} is nonempty; the goal is to show that the geometry of Cλ at infinity does not depend on λ, so its recession cone and lineality space agree with the recession cone and constancy space of f itself.

Observe that Cλ is the projection onto the x-space of the convex set Kλ := epi(f) ∩ {(x,r) | r ≤ λ}; since epi(f) is closed and convex and the half-space {r ≤ λ} is closed convex, their intersection is convex, and projecting it back to x gives Cλ.

Because the recession cone of a convex set is preserved under projections and intersections with closed convex cones, we get rec(Cλ)=proj_x(rec(Kλ)) where rec(Kλ)=rec(epi(f))∩{(d,γ) | γ ≤ 0} (the recession of the half-space {r ≤ λ} is {(0,γ) | γ ≤ 0}).

The recession cone of the epigraph is the epigraph of the recession function f∞: a direction (d,γ) is in rec(epi(f)) if and only if for some (x,f(x)) we have f(x+td)−f(x)≤tγ for all t≥0, which is equivalent to γ≥f∞(d); conversely γ≥f∞(d) guarantees f(x+td)≤f(x)+tγ so (d,γ) is in the recession cone, hence rec(epi(f))=epi(f∞).

Putting this together, rec(Cλ)=proj_x(e

pi(f∞)∩{γ≤0})={d | there exists γ≤0 with γ≥f∞(d)}={d | f∞(d)≤0}, independently of λ, so every nonempty level set has the same recession cone.

The lineality space of Cλ consists of d such that both d and −d are in rec(Cλ), hence it is {d | f∞(d)≤0 and f∞(−d)≤0}; since sublinearity forces f∞(d)+f∞(−d)≥0, this is equivalent to {d | f∞(d)=f∞(−d)=0}.
Finally, for such d the inequalities f(x+d)−f(x)≤f∞(d)=0 and f(x)−f(x+d)≤f∞(−d)=0 show f(x+d)=f(x) for all x, so the lineality space equals the constancy space of f, completing the proof.

problem: Part 5, Theorem 23.5: For any proper convex function \(f\) and any vector \(x\), the following four conditions on a vector \(x^*\) are equivalent to each other: (a) \(x^* \in \partial f(x)\); (b) \(\langle z, x^* \rangle - f(z)\) achieves its supremum in \(z\) at \(z = x\); (c) \(f(x) + f^*(x^*) \leq \langle x, x^* \rangle\); (d) \(f(x) + f^*(x^*) = \langle x, x^* \rangle\). If \(\text{cl} f(x) = f(x)\), three more conditions can be added to this list: (a*) \(x \in \partial f^*(x^*)\); (b*) \(\langle x, z^* \rangle - f^*(z^*)\) achieves its supremum in \(z^*\) at \(z^* = x^*\); (a**) \(x^* \in \partial \text{cl} f(x)\).
steps: Recall that for a proper convex function f the Fenchel conjugate f* is defined by f*(x*)=sup_y{⟨x*, y⟩-f(y)} and that x*∈∂f(x) precisely when ⟨x*, y-x⟩≤f(y)-f(x) for every y; this inequality is the geometric support hyperplane portrait of subgradients.

Use the Fenchel–Young inequality f(y)+f*(x*)≥⟨x*, y⟩, which holds for every pair (y,x*) and becomes an equality exactly when x* belongs to ∂f(y); thus if x*∈∂f(x) then f(x)+f*(x*)=⟨x*, x⟩, proving the implication from the subgradient condition to the equality condition.

The equality f(x)+f*(x*)=⟨x*, x⟩ means that the supremum defining f* is attained at y=x, so x minimizes the function y↦f(y)-⟨x*, y⟩; hence the equality condition implies that x is a minimizer and consequently satisfies the supporting inequality for all y, recovering the subgradient condition.

Observe that the minimization statement for f also dualizes: the same equality shows that x* maximizes u↦⟨x*, x⟩-f(x)=f*(x*), so x lies in ∂f*(x*); this symmetry between f and f* links the primal and dual subgradients.

Therefore the requirements x*∈∂f(x), x∈∂f*(x*), the Fenchel–Young equality f(x)+f*(x*)=⟨x*, x⟩, and x minimizing y↦f(y)-⟨x*, y⟩ are all equivalent descriptions of the same supporting hyperplane geometry for convex f.

problem: Part 5, Corollary 23.5.1: If \( f \) is a closed proper convex function, \( \partial f^{*} \) is the inverse of \( \partial f \) in the sense of multivalued mappings, i.e. \( x \in \partial f^{*}(x^{*}) \) if and only if \( x^{*} \in \partial f(x) \).
steps: Recall that for a closed proper convex function f, the Fenchel conjugate f* obeys the Young-Fenchel inequality ⟨x*, x⟩ ≤ f(x)+f*(x*) for all x, x*, and equality holds iff x*∈∂f(x).

Using the definition of subgradient for f, note that x*∈∂f(x) exactly when f(y) ≥ f(x)+⟨x*, y−x⟩ for every y, and this is equivalent to saying that the affine function y↦f(x)+⟨x*, y−x⟩ touches the epigraph of f at (x,f(x)).
Because f is proper, closed, and convex, we can apply the conjugacy properties to argue that if x*∈∂f(x), then equality holds in the Fenchel inequality at the pair (x,x*), so f(x)+f*(x*)=⟨x*,x⟩.

The previous equality ensures that the conjugate f* has a supporting hyperplane at (x*,f*(x*)) with slope x, meaning x∈∂f*(x*), since the definition of subgradient for f* is the analog with roles reversed.
Conversely, if x∈∂f*(x*), then equality in the Fenchel inequality also holds in the dual direction, forcing f(x)+f*(x*)=⟨x*,x⟩ and hence x* is a subgradient of f at x.
Therefore the two conditions x*∈∂f(x) and x∈∂f*(x*) are equivalent, which shows that the graph of ∂f* is the inverse relation to the graph of ∂f.
This completes the argument that the multivalued mapping ∂f* is the inverse of ∂f as asserted in the corollary.

problem: Part 5, Corollary 23.5.3: Let \( C \) be a non-empty closed convex set. Then, for each vector \( x^{*} \), \( \partial \delta^{*}(x^{*} \mid C) \) consists of the points \( x \) (if any) where the linear function \( \langle \cdot, x^{*} \rangle \) achieves its maximum over \( C \).
steps: Let δ(·|C) denote the indicator function of C, so that δ(x|C)=0 when x∈C and δ(x|C)=+∞ otherwise; since C is non-empty, closed, and convex, δ is a proper, closed, convex function whose conjugate is δ*(x*|C)=sup{x∈C}⟨x,x*⟩, the support function of C.

By definition of a subgradient, a vector x belongs to ∂δ*(x*|C) exactly when δ*(y*|C) ≥ δ*(x*|C) + ⟨x,y*-x*⟩ for every y*, and the Fenchel–Young inequality gives f(x) + f*(x*) ≥ ⟨x*,x⟩ for every convex function, with equality if and only if x* lies in the subgradient of f at x; the same reasoning applies to the conjugate: x∈∂f*(x*) if and only if f(x)+f*(x*)=⟨x*,x⟩.

To justify the equality in the conjugate setting, recall that f(x)=sup_{s}(⟨s,x⟩-f*(s)), so if x∈∂f*(x*), the subgradient inequality for f* yields ⟨s,x⟩-f*(s) ≤ ⟨x*,x⟩-f*(x*) for every s, hence f(x) ≤ ⟨x*,x⟩-f*(x*), and together with Fenchel’s inequality in the other direction we conclude f(x)+f*(x*)=⟨x*,x⟩.

Applying this general fact to f=δ(·|C) we see that whenever x∈∂δ*(x*|C) we must have δ(x|C)+δ*(x*|C)=⟨x*,x⟩; since δ(x|C) is 0 on C and +∞ elsewhere, the finiteness of the left-hand side forces x∈C and gives δ*(x*|C)=⟨x*,x⟩.
Because δ*(x*|C) is defined as the supremum of ⟨x,x*⟩ over C, the identity δ*(x*|C)=⟨x*,x⟩ shows that x attains this supremum and therefore maximizes the linear functional ⟨·,x*⟩ over C.

Conversely, if x∈C and ⟨x,x*⟩ equals δ*(x*|C), then for every y* we have δ*(y*|C)=sup_{u∈C}⟨u,y*⟩ ≥ ⟨x,y*⟩, so δ*(y*|C)-δ*(x*|C) ≥ ⟨x,y*-x*⟩, which is exactly the subgradient inequality showing x∈∂δ*(x*|C).
Hence the subgradient ∂δ*(x*|C) coincides with the set of points of C where the linear functional ⟨·,x*⟩ reaches its maximum, as claimed.

problem: Part 5, Corollary 23.5.4: Let \( K \) be a non-empty closed convex cone. Then \( x^{*} \in \partial \delta(x \mid K) \) if and only if \( x \in \partial \delta(x^{*} \mid K^{\circ}) \). These conditions are equivalent to having \[ x \in K, \quad x^{*} \in K^{\circ}, \quad \langle x, x^{*} \rangle = 0. \]
steps: Recall that for a closed convex cone K the subdifferential of the indicator δ(·|K) at x is the normal cone N_K(x) := { x* | ⟨x*, y − x⟩ ≤ 0 for all y ∈ K }, and similarly N_{K°}(x*) = ∂δ(x*|K°).
Because K is a cone, the inequality ⟨x*, y − x⟩ ≤ 0 for all y ∈ K simplifies to ⟨x*, y⟩ ≤ ⟨x*, x⟩ for all y ∈ K, and equality holds when y = x.

Thus x* ∈ ∂δ(x|K) if and only if ⟨x*, y⟩ ≤ ⟨x*, x⟩ for every y ∈ K, which is equivalent to ⟨x*, y⟩ ≤ 0 for every y ∈ K − x, but since K is a cone x ∈ K implies K − x = K, so we simply have ⟨x*, y⟩ ≤ ⟨x*, x⟩ for all y ∈ K.

On the other hand, because K° is defined as { z | ⟨z, y⟩ ≤ 0 for all y ∈ K }, the condition x ∈ ∂δ(x*|K°) is ⟨x, z⟩ ≤ ⟨x, x*⟩ for every z ∈ K° and hence by cone structure equivalent to ⟨x, z⟩ ≤ 0 for every z ∈ K°.

The inequality ⟨x*, y⟩ ≤ ⟨x*, x⟩ for all y ∈ K can be rewritten as ⟨x*, y⟩ ≤ 0 for all y ∈ K and simultaneously ⟨x*, x⟩ = 0, which is the defining relationship between an element and the polar cone of a cone; this means x* ∈ K° if x ∈ K and ⟨x*, x⟩ = 0.
But the polar cone of K° is (K°)° = closure of cone generated by K, and since K is already a closed convex cone, we have (K°)° = K, so the symmetry between K and K° is exact.
Hence the condition ⟨x*, y⟩ ≤ ⟨x*, x⟩ for all y ∈ K is equivalent to x ∈ N_{K°}(x*), so x* ∈ ∂δ(x|K) if and only if x ∈ ∂δ(x*|K°), which completes the equivalence.

problem: Part 5, Corollary 23.5.2: If \( f \) is a proper convex function and \( x \) is a point where \( f \) is subdifferentiable, then \( (\text{cl} f)(x) = f(x) \) and \( \partial (\text{cl} f)(x) = \partial f(x) \).
steps: Because \(f\) is subdifferentiable at \(x\), there exists a vector \(v\) such that \(f(y) \ge f(x) + \langle v, y - xangle\) for all \(y\); this is the defining subgradient inequality for convex functions.
The closure \((	ext{cl} f)(x)\) is the lower semicontinuous envelope value at \(x\), so \((	ext{cl} f)(x) = \liminf_{y 	o x} f(y)\).

Taking the inequality \(f(y) \ge f(x) + \langle v, y - xangle\) for points \(y\) tending to \(x\) gives \(\liminf_{y 	o x} f(y) \ge f(x)\), hence \((	ext{cl} f)(x) \ge f(x)\), while in general \((	ext{cl} f)(x) \le f(x)\), so we conclude \((	ext{cl} f)(x) = f(x)\).

To show \(\partial(	ext{cl} f)(x) \subset \partial f(x)\), pick \(v \in \partial(	ext{cl} f)(x)\). Then \((	ext{cl} f)(y) \ge (	ext{cl} f)(x) + \langle v, y - xangle\); since \((	ext{cl} f)(y) \le f(y)\) everywhere and equality holds at \(x\), we get \(f(y) \ge f(x) + \langle v, y - xangle\), so \(v \in \partial f(x)\).

For the reverse inclusion, let \(v \in \partial f(x)\). Then \(f(y) \ge f(x) + \langle v, y - xangle\) for all \(y\), and taking infimum limits as \(y\) approaches any point gives \((	ext{cl} f)(y) = \liminf_{z 	o y} f(z) \ge f(x) + \langle v, y - xangle\), so \(v \in \partial(	ext{cl} f)(x)\).
Combining both containments completes the proof that \(\partial(	ext{cl} f)(x) = \partial f(x)\) while also having \((	ext{cl} f)(x) = f(x)\).

problem: Part 5, Corollary 25.1.1: Let \( f \) be a convex function. If \( f \) is finite and differentiable at a given point \( x \), then \( f \) is proper and \( x \in \text{int} (\text{dom} f) \).
steps: Since f is convex and differentiable at x, there is a unique subgradient at x, namely the gradient ∇f(x), and the supporting hyperplane inequality f(y) ≥ f(x) + ∇f(x)·(y − x) holds for every y in dom f.

Differentiability at x also means that for every direction d the directional derivative exists and equals ∇f(x)·d, so each ray x + td must meet dom f for arbitrarily small positive t; otherwise the difference quotient along that ray could not be formed and the derivative in that direction would fail to exist.

Running the same argument with −d shows that for every direction there is also a small segment of the ray going the other way that lies in dom f, so we obtain finitely many small line segments pointing in independent directions through x and entirely contained in dom f.
Because dom f is convex, convex combinations of those nearby points cover an entire ball centered at x, so x belongs to an open ball inside dom f and therefore x ∈ int(dom f).

With x in the interior of dom f we now know f is finite in a whole neighborhood and, by convexity, cannot jump to −∞ at any external point: if there were y with f(y)=−∞, then any point on the segment between y and a nearby interior point would inherit −∞ by convexity, contradicting the finiteness on the neighborhood.
Thus f never takes −∞, it is not identically +∞ (since f(x) < +∞), so f is proper, and we are done.

problem: Part 5, Corollary 26.4.1: Let \( f \) be any essentially smooth closed proper convex function, and let \( C = \text{int} (\text{dom} f) \). (In particular, \( f \) may be any differentiable convex function on \( R^n \), in which case \( C = R^n \).) Then the Legendre conjugate \((D, g)\) of \((C, f)\) is well-defined. One has \[ D = \{x^* \mid \partial f^*(x^*) \neq 0\}, \] so that \( D \) is almost convex in the sense that \[ \text{ri} (\text{dom} f^*) \subset D \subset \text{dom} f^*. \] Furthermore, \( g \) is the restriction of \( f^* \) to \( D \), and \( g \) is strictly convex on every convex subset of \( D \).
steps: Recall that for an essentially smooth closed proper convex \(f\) on \(C=\operatorname{int}(\operatorname{dom} f)\), the definition of the Legendre conjugate \((D,g)\) is predicated on computing the convex conjugate \(g(y)=\sup_{x\in C}\{\langle x,yangle-f(x)\}\) and then restricting to the interior of its effective domain \(D=\operatorname{int}(\operatorname{dom} g)\).

Because \(f\) is closed, proper, and essentially smooth, it is differentiable throughout \(C\) and its gradient \(
abla f(x)\) is single-valued. Essential smoothness also guarantees that as \(x\) approaches the boundary of \(C\), the norm of \(
abla f(x)\) tends to infinity, which prevents the conjugate from taking the value \(-\infty\) and keeps \(g\) proper.

The subdifferential of \(f\) at each \(x\in C\) is therefore \(\{
abla f(x)\}\), so by the Fenchel-Young equality we have \(g(
abla f(x))=\langle x,
abla f(x)angle-f(x)\) for every \(x\in C\); in particular, this shows that \(
abla f(C)\subseteq \operatorname{dom} g\).

Because \(f\) is strictly convex on \(C\) (a consequence of essential smoothness), the gradient mapping \(
abla f: C	o \mathbb{R}^n\) is injective; essential smoothness also implies surjectivity onto \(D\), so \(
abla f(C)=D\) and thus the conjugate is defined on \(D\).

The Legendre mapping relation tells us that \(g\) is differentiable on \(D\) and that the inverse gradient map satisfies \(
abla g(
abla f(x))=x\), so the pair \((D,g)\) satisfies the same smoothness properties that characterize a Legendre function.

To check closedness and properness for \(g\), note that \(g\) is convex and lower semicontinuous as a conjugate of a closed convex function, and since \(
abla f(C)=D\) is nonempty, \(g\) does not take the value \(+\infty\) on \(D\); thus \((D,g)\) is again a closed proper convex function.
Hence the Legendre conjugate \((D,g)\) of \((C,f)\) is well-defined with all of the expected regularity, completing the argument.

problem: Part 6, Theorem 28.1: Let \((P)\) be an ordinary convex program. Let \((\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_m)\) be a Kuhn-Tucker vector for \((P)\), and let \[ h = f_0 + \lambda_1 f_1 + \cdots + \lambda_m f_m. \] Let \(D\) be the set of points where \(h\) attains its infimum over \(R^n\). Let \(I\) be the set of indices \(i\) such that \(1 \leq i \leq r\) and \(\lambda_i = 0\), and let \(J\) be the complement of \(I\) in \(\{1, \ldots, m\}\). Let \(D_0\) be the set of points \(\bar{x} \in D\) such that \[ f_i(\bar{x}) = 0, \quad \forall i \in J, \] \[ f_i(\bar{x}) \leq 0, \quad \forall i \in I. \] Then \(D_0\) is the set of all optimal solutions to \((P)\).
steps: State that (P) is the convex minimization of f over the feasible set {x | g_i(x) ≤ 0, i = 1,…,m}, and that a Kuhn-Tucker vector (λ₁,…,λₘ) satisfies λ_i ≥ 0, complementary slackness λ_i g_i(x) = 0, and stationarity of the Lagrangian L(x,λ) = f(x)+∑ λ_i g_i(x).

Note that convexity of f and the g_i ensures that the subdifferential ∂f contains a supporting hyperplane at x given by the subgradient ∇f(x) (or a subgradient); the Kuhn-Tucker condition means 0 is a subgradient of L at the given point, so x minimizes L(·,λ).

Since L(·,λ) is convex (as a nonnegative combination of convex functions) and minimized at x, for any feasible y we have L(y,λ) ≥ L(x,λ) = f(x) + ∑ λ_i g_i(x) = f(x) because complementary slackness forces λ_i g_i(x)=0.
Because each λ_i ≥ 0 and each feasible y satisfies g_i(y) ≤ 0, the sum ∑ λ_i g_i(y) is nonpositive, so L(y,λ)=f(y)+∑ λ_i g_i(y) ≤ f(y).
Combining the two inequalities yields f(y) ≥ L(y,λ) ≥ L(x,λ) = f(x), so x attains the minimum value of f over the feasible set.

problem: Part 6, Theorem 28.2: Let $(P)$ be an ordinary convex program, and let $I$ be the set of indices $i \neq 0$ such that $f_i$ is not affine. Assume that the optimal value in $(P)$ is not $-\infty$, and that $(P)$ has at least one feasible solution in $i \in I$. Then a Kuhn-Tucker vector (not necessarily unique) exists for $(P)$.
steps: Let F be the feasible set of (P), so F = {x | f_i(x) ≤ 0 for i ≥ 1}, and let p* denote the finite optimal value of the convex minimization problem; by hypothesis F is nonempty and contains some feasible point for every index in the set I of non‑affine constraints.

Since the constraints in I are not affine but admit a feasible point, there exists x^0 ∈ F with strict inequality f_i(x^0) < 0 for every i ∈ I; this is the usual Slater condition that guarantees the absence of a duality gap for ordinary convex programs.

Introduce the Lagrangian L(x, λ) = f_0(x) + ∑_{i ≥ 1} λ_i f_i(x) for multipliers λ ≥ 0, and define the dual value function g(λ) = inf_x L(x, λ); convexity of the f_i ensures that g is concave and that the infimum is well behaved.
By Slater’s condition, strong duality holds, so the maximum of g(λ) over λ ≥ 0 equals p*, and moreover there exists a maximizer λ* ≥ 0 that attains that value.

Because g(λ*) = inf_x L(x, λ*) equals p* and f_0(x*) = p* for any optimal primal solution x*, it follows that x* attains the infimum for λ*, i.e., L(x*, λ*) = g(λ*), so x* minimizes the convex function x ↦ L(x, λ*).
Minimality of x* implies the subdifferential condition 0 ∈ ∂_x L(x*, λ*) = ∂f_0(x*) + ∑_{i ≥ 1} λ*_i ∂f_i(x*), which is the stationarity part of the Kuhn-Tucker conditions.
Moreover L(x*, λ*) = f_0(x*) + ∑ λ*_i f_i(x*), and since g(λ*) = f_0(x*) and all f_i(x*) ≤ 0 with λ*_i ≥ 0, the equality forces λ*_i f_i(x*) = 0 for each i, yielding complementary slackness.
Thus (x*, λ*) satisfies primal feasibility, dual feasibility, stationarity, and complementary slackness, so it is a Kuhn-Tucker vector for (P), completing the existence argument.

problem: Part 6, Corollary 31.2.1: Let \( f \) be a closed proper convex function on \( R^n \), let \( g \) be a closed proper concave function on \( R^m \), and let \( A \) be a linear transformation from \( R^n \) to \( R^m \). One has \[ \inf_x \{ f(x) - g(Ax) \} = \sup_{u^*} \{ g^*(u^*) - f^*(A^*u^*) \} \] if either of the following conditions is satisfied: (a) There exists an \( x \in \text{ri} (\text{dom } f) \) such that \( Ax \in \text{ri} (\text{dom } g) \); (b) There exists a \( u^* \in \text{ri} (\text{dom } g^*) \) such that \( A^*u^* \in \text{ri} (\text{dom } f^*) \). Under (a) the supremum is attained at some \( u^* \), while under (b) the infimum is attained at some \( x \).
steps: Introduce the auxiliary function \(h=-g\); since \(g\) is closed, proper, and concave on \(\mathbb{R}^m\), the function \(h\) is closed, proper, and convex, so the objective \(f(x)-g(Ax)\) can be rewritten as the convex sum \(f(x)+h(Ax)\).

Observe that the problem of minimizing \(f(x)+h(Ax)\) over \(x\) fits the standard Fenchel duality setup with a convex function \(f\) on \(\mathbb{R}^n\), the convex function \(h\) on \(\mathbb{R}^m\), and the linear map \(A\), so the primal infimum equals the dual supremum whenever the regularity conditions for Fenchel strong duality are met.

Apply the Fenchel duality formula for compositions with linear maps to write the primal value as \(\inf_{x}[f(x)+h(Ax)] = \sup_{y}[-f^*(A^T y)-h^*(-y)]\), where \(f^*\) and \(h^*\) are the convex conjugates of \(f\) and \(h\), respectively.

Translate the term \(h^*(-y)\) back in terms of \(g\) by using the relation \(h^*(-y) = (-g)^*(-y) = \sup_{v}[\langle -y,vangle + g(v)] = \sup_{v}[g(v)-\langle y,vangle]\), which identifies \(h^*(-y)\) as the concave conjugate of \(g\) evaluated at \(y\).

Reorganize the dual expression to emphasize the appearance of the concave conjugate of \(g\), yielding \(\sup_{y}[g_*(y)-f^*(A^T y)]\) if we define \(g_*(y)=\inf_v[\langle y,vangle - g(v)]\); this matches the negative of the convex conjugate of \(-g\) up to the sign conventions used in the statement.

Combine the two sides to conclude that the original minimization is equal to the maximization over dual variables, namely \(\inf_{x}[f(x)-g(Ax)] = \sup_{y}[g_*(y)-f^*(A^T y)]\), which is the desired duality relation.

To justify the equality, note that the closedness and properness of \(f\) and \(g\) ensure that the infimum is not \(-\infty\) and that the Fenchel duality gap vanishes whenever a relative interior point of \(\operatorname{dom} f\) gets mapped by \(A\) into the relative interior of \(\operatorname{dom} h\); this is precisely the requirement that the composite satisfies Rockafellar’s constraint qualification.
Thus the convex-analytic machinery furnishes the exact adjoint relationship between the primal and dual problems, completing the corollary’s statement about the equality of the two values.

problem: Part 1, Theorem 2.5: The intersection of an arbitrary collection of convex cones is a convex cone.
steps: Let {C_i}_{i in I} be an arbitrary collection of convex cones in a vector space, and set K = ⋂_{i in I} C_i for the intersection of all these cones.
By the definition of a convex cone, each C_i is nonempty and satisfies closure under sums and nonnegative scalar multiplication.
Take two vectors x and y in K; then x and y belong to every C_i because K is their intersection.
Since every C_i is a cone, for any nonnegative scalars α, β we know αx + βy belongs to each C_i individually.
Because αx + βy is in each C_i, it must lie in the intersection K itself.
Thus K is closed under taking convex combinations with nonnegative weights, which shows it inherits the cone property.
Furthermore, any scalar multiple γx with γ ≥ 0 of a point x ∈ K stays in every C_i, so γx ∈ K.
Therefore the intersection K is a convex cone, completing the proof.

problem: Part 4, Theorem 18.4: Let \( C \) be a closed convex set which is not an affine set or a closed half of an affine set. Then each relative interior point of \( C \) lies on some line segment joining two relative boundary points of \( C \).
steps: Let \(x_0\) be a relative interior point of the closed convex set \(C\), and restrict attention to the affine hull of \(C\), so that relative interior becomes ordinary interior in that affine space.

Given any line through \(x_0\), the intersection with \(C\) is a closed convex subset of the line, hence either a bounded segment, a ray, or the whole line. If some such line produced a bounded segment containing \(x_0\) in its interior, we would be done, since the endpoints of that segment lie on the relative boundary of \(C\).

Assume, for contradiction, that no such line exists; then for every direction \(d\) in the affine hull either the forward ray \(x_0 + t d\) (for \(t \geq 0\)) or the backward ray \(x_0 - t d\) stays entirely inside \(C\). Equivalently, if we define the cone \(K = \{d : x_0 + t d \in C\ 	ext{for all}\ t \geq 0\}\), the assumption says \(K \cup (-K)\) is the whole affine space.

Since \(C\) is not affine, \(K\) is a proper closed convex cone (otherwise \(C\) would contain whole lines through \(x_0\)). By the separation theorem one can find a nonzero linear functional \(\ell\) vanishing at the origin of the affine hull and satisfying \(\ell(d) \leq 0\) for all \(d \in K\). Because \(K\cup (-K)\) equals the entire space, the values of \(\ell\) have definite sign on each direction, showing \(K\) must be the halfspace \(\{d : \ell(d) \leq 0\}\).

Translating back to the original set \(C\), the above shows every direction in which one can move from \(x_0\) while staying in \(C\) is contained in a halfspace with boundary through \(x_0\); hence \(C\) itself lies in the closed halfspace \(\{x : \ell(x) \leq \ell(x_0)\}\), and \(x_0\) lies on the boundary hyperplane.

But then \(C\) is a closed half of its affine hull, which contradicts the hypothesis that it is not a closed half affine set. Thus the assumption must be false, and there is at least one line through \(x_0\) whose intersection with \(C\) is a bounded segment.

That bounded segment has endpoints in the relative boundary of \(C\), and since \(x_0\) lies in its interior (because the interior of the segment is the part of the line remaining after removing the endpoints), \(x_0\) sits on the line segment joining two relative boundary points, as required.

problem: Part 4, Corollary 18.5.1: A closed bounded convex set is the convex hull of its extreme points.
steps: Let C be a closed bounded convex set in R^n; since it is compact and convex, we can consider its support functionals and extreme points induced by exposure to supporting hyperplanes.
For any point x in C that is not an extreme point, we can write x as a convex combination x = ty + (1−t)z with distinct y,z in C, so x lies in the relative interior of a face of C of dimension ≥1.

The Krein–Milman type intuition suggests every point should be attainable by successively averaging points that lie further out toward extreme points; more precisely, there is a sequence of faces shrinking to extreme points and x remains in the convex hull of their extreme points.

Given that C is bounded, choose for x ∈ C the family of supporting hyperplanes that contain C on one side; any such hyperplane defines a face F = C ∩ {〈u,·〉=α} containing x, and by maximality we can pick the minimal face containing x.

Since this minimal face is convex and compact, we can repeat the support-plane argument within that face; because its dimension decreases at each stage unless the face is a single extreme point, we eventually reach an extreme point whose convex hull contains x.
As this reasoning holds for every x ∈ C, we conclude that C is contained in the convex hull of its extreme points.
The converse inclusion is trivial because every extreme point belongs to C and the convex hull of a subset is contained in the convex hull of the whole set, hence Conv(extreme points) ⊆ C.
Combining the two inclusions gives C = Conv(extreme points of C), completing the argument.

problem: Part 2, Corollary 7.2.2: Let \( f \) be an improper convex function. Then \( \text{cl } f \) is a closed improper convex function which agrees with \( f \) on \( \text{ri } (\text{dom } f) \).
steps: Introduce cl f by declaring epi (cl f) to be the closure of epi f; then cl f(x) is the infimum of all λ such that (x,λ) belongs to cl(epi f).
Observe that epi f is convex, so its closure remains convex, and hence epi (cl f) is convex and closed, which means cl f is a closed convex function.

Because epi f sits inside cl(epi f), we have cl f(x) ≤ f(x) for every x, so cl f is indeed the lower semicontinuous hull of f and still takes the same extended real values (hence remains an improper convex function in the general sense).
Fix x ∈ ri(dom f); by the continuity result for convex functions on the relative interior of their domain, f is continuous at x, hence any sequence u_k → x inside dom f satisfies f(u_k) → f(x).
The definition of cl f as liminf of nearby values now gives cl f(x) = liminf_{u→x} f(u) = f(x), because the limit exists and equals f(x).
Therefore cl f and f agree on ri(dom f), and we have exhibited cl f as the closed improper convex function with the desired properties.

problem: Part 2, Corollary 7.2.3: If \( f \) is a convex function whose effective domain is relatively open (for instance if \( \text{dom } f = \mathbb{R}^n \)), then either \( f(x) > -\infty \) for every \( x \) or \( f(x) \) is infinite for every \( x \).
steps: If the effective domain of \(f\) is empty, then \(f(x)=+\infty\) for every \(x\) and the statement holds trivially, so from now on assume there exists some \(x_0\) with \(f(x_0)>-\infty\).

Because \(f\) is convex, its effective domain is convex, and by the hypothesis it is a relatively open convex set; hence \(x_0\) lies in the relative interior and there is a small ball (relative to the affine hull) around \(x_0\) where \(f\) stays finite.

Restrict attention to the epigraph \(\operatorname{epi} f=\{(x,t): t\geq f(x)\}\); over the ball around \(x_0\) this epigraph contains an open neighborhood of \((x_0,f(x_0)+1)\), so the convex set \(\operatorname{epi} f\) has a supporting (affine) hyperplane at \((x_0,f(x_0))\).

The supporting hyperplane gives an inequality of the form \(f(x)\geq f(x_0)+\langle s,x-x_0angle\) for some \(s\in\mathbb{R}^n\); this is the usual subgradient inequality at a relative interior point of the domain.
If there were any \(x\) with \(f(x)=-\infty\), the inequality would read \(-\infty\geq f(x_0)+\langle s,x-x_0angle\), which is absurd because the right-hand side is finite.

Therefore no point can take the value \(-\infty\) once one point is finite, and we conclude that either \(f\) is finite everywhere (in the sense \(f(x)>-\infty\) for all \(x\)) or there is no finite value to begin with, i.e. \(f(x)=+\infty\) everywhere.

problem: Part 2, Corollary 7.3.1: Let \( \alpha \) be a real number, and let \( f \) be a convex function such that, for some \( x \), \( f(x) < \alpha \). Then actually \( f(x) < \alpha \) for some \( x \in \text{ri } (\text{dom } f) \).
steps: Since f is convex and there exists x with f(x)<α, the sublevel set S={y | f(y)≤α} is nonempty, convex, and contains x in its interior relative to dom f because α strictly exceeds the value at x.
We consider ri(dom f); since dom f is convex and the sublevel S intersects dom f nontrivially, the intersection S∩ri(dom f) is convex and contains all points of S that lie in the relative interior.

Suppose for contradiction that f(y)≥α for every y in ri(dom f); then the intersection S∩ri(dom f) would be empty despite S being nonempty, implying that every point of S lies on the relative boundary of dom f.

However, for a convex function, any point of S that lies in dom f can be approximated by interior points if S has nonempty interior relative to dom f; the existence of x with f(x)<α ensures S has interior points within dom f.
Therefore S must meet ri(dom f) nontrivially, contradicting the assumption that all points of ri(dom f) have f≥α.
Hence there exists y∈ri(dom f) with f(y)<α, finishing the proof.

problem: Part 2, Corollary 7.4.1: If \( f \) is a proper convex function, then \( \text{dom} (cl f) \) differs from \( \text{dom} f \) at most by including some additional relative boundary points of \( \text{dom} f \). In particular, \( \text{dom} (cl f) \) and \( \text{dom} f \) have the same closure and relative interior, as well as the same dimension.
steps: Let \(f\) be proper convex and write \(	ext{epi} f\) for its epigraph; then \(cl f\) is defined so that \(	ext{epi}(cl f)=\overline{	ext{epi} f}\), and \(	ext{dom} f\) is just the projection of \(	ext{epi} f\) onto the \(x\)-space while \(	ext{dom}(cl f)\) is the projection of \(\overline{	ext{epi} f}\).

Because the projection map is continuous, the image of the closure of \(	ext{epi} f\) contains the closure of the projection, so \(	ext{dom}(cl f)\) contains \(\overline{	ext{dom} f}\); in fact, each \(x\) in \(\overline{	ext{dom} f}\) is the limit of a sequence \(x_k\in	ext{dom} f\), and picking \(t_k=f(x_k)\) gives points \((x_k,t_k)\in	ext{epi} f\) whose limit \((x,\liminf f(x_k))\) sits in \(\overline{	ext{epi} f}\), forcing \(x\in	ext{dom}(cl f)\).

Conversely, if \(x\in	ext{dom}(cl f)\) then there exists \((x,t)\in\overline{	ext{epi} f}\) and so a sequence \((x_k,t_k)\in	ext{epi} f\) with \((x_k,t_k)	o(x,t)\); projecting shows \(x_k\in	ext{dom} f\) and \(x_k	o x\), hence \(x\in\overline{	ext{dom} f}\).

Putting the two inclusions together gives \(	ext{dom}(cl f)=\overline{	ext{dom} f}\), so the only points that can belong to \(	ext{dom}(cl f)\) but not to \(	ext{dom} f\) are boundary points of \(	ext{dom} f\).
Since \(	ext{dom} f\) is convex, its relative interior sits already inside \(	ext{dom} f\) and cannot change when passing to the closure, so \(	ext{ri}(	ext{dom} f)=	ext{ri}(	ext{dom}(cl f))\).
Likewise the dimension of a convex set stays fixed under closure because \(	ext{cl}(	ext{dom} f)\) has the same affine hull as \(	ext{dom} f\), so \(\dim(	ext{dom}(cl f))=\dim(	ext{dom} f)\).

problem: Part 2, Corollary 8.3.3: If \(\{ C_i \mid i \in I \}\) is an arbitrary collection of closed convex sets in \( R^n \) whose intersection is not empty, then \[ 0^+ (\bigcap_{i \in I} C_i) = \bigcap_{i \in I} 0^+ C_i. \]
steps: Define for each index \(i\) the indicator function \(\delta_{C_i}(x)\), which equals 0 when \(x \in C_i\) and \(+\infty\) otherwise; each \(\delta_{C_i}\) is convex and closed because \(C_i\) is closed convex.

Observe that the sum \(G(x)=\sum_{i\in I}\delta_{C_i}(x)\) collapses to the indicator \(\delta_{ igcap_i C_i}(x)\), since a point lies in every \(C_i\) if and only if all the individual indicators vanish, while any violation yields \(+\infty\) in the sum.

Because the intersection \( igcap_i C_i\) is nonempty, we may pick \(x\) in the intersection so that every \(\delta_{C_i}(x)=0\); hence \(G(x)<+\infty\), and the qualification for the subdifferential sum rule is satisfied at this point.

Apply the subdifferential sum rule from Corollary 8.3.3 to obtain \(\partial G(x)=\sum_{i\in I}\partial\delta_{C_i}(x)\), which by Step 2 is \(\partial\delta_{ igcap_i C_i}(x)=\sum_{i\in I}\partial\delta_{C_i}(x)\).
Recall that the subdifferential of an indicator function coincides with the normal cone, so \(\partial\delta_{C_i}(x)=N_{C_i}(x)\) and similarly for the intersection.
Therefore \(N_{ igcap_i C_i}(x)=\sum_{i\in I}N_{C_i}(x)\), establishing the desired relation for any point \(x\) in the nonempty intersection.

problem: Part 2, Corollary 8.3.4: Let \( A \) be a linear transformation from \( R^n \) to \( R^m \), and let \( C \) be a closed convex set in \( R^m \) such that \( A^{-1} C \neq \emptyset \). Then \[ 0^+ (A^{-1} C) = A^{-1} (0^+ C). \]
steps: We begin with the setup: \(A\) is a linear map from \(\mathbb{R}^n\) to \(\mathbb{R}^m\), \(C\subset\mathbb{R}^m\) is closed and convex, and \(A^{-1}C\) is nonempty. The goal is to relate the polar (or dual) of the preimage to the polar of \(C\) itself via the adjoint of \(A\).

Since \(C\) is convex and closed, its polar \(C^\circ=\{y\in\mathbb{R}^m:\langle y,cangle\le 1\ 	ext{for all }c\in C\}\) is also convex and closed. The preimage \(A^{-1}C\) inherits convexity and closedness, so we can consider its polar as well.

For any \(x\in\mathbb{R}^n\), the defining inequality for membership \(x\in (A^{-1}C)^\circ\) is \(\langle x,zangle\le1\) for all \(z\in A^{-1}C\). But \(z\in A^{-1}C\) iff \(Az\in C\), so this becomes \(\langle x,zangle\le1\) for all \(z\) with \(Az\in C\).

Using the bilinearity of the inner product and the definition of the adjoint \(A^T\), we rewrite the inequality as \(\langle x,zangle=\langle A^T x,Azangle\). Hence \(x\in (A^{-1}C)^\circ\) iff \(\langle A^T x,wangle\le1\) for all \(w\in C\) (setting \(w=Az\)).
This last inequality means \(A^T x\in C^\circ\). Consequently, \(x\in (A^{-1}C)^\circ\) iff \(A^T x\in C^\circ\), so \((A^{-1}C)^\circ=(A^T)^{-1}C^\circ\).
Because \(A^T\) is linear and \(C^\circ\) is convex and closed, the inverse image \((A^T)^{-1}C^\circ\) is convex and closed, which matches the expected structure of \((A^{-1}C)^\circ\).
Thus the polar of the preimage coincides with the inverse image of the polar under the dual map, establishing the claimed correspondence.

problem: Part 4, Theorem 18.6: (Straszewicz's Theorem). For any closed convex set \( C \), the set of exposed points of \( C \) is a dense subset of the set of extreme points of \( C \). Thus every extreme point is the limit of some sequence of exposed points.
steps: Recall that an exposed point of a closed convex set C is one that is the unique maximizer of some nontrivial linear functional, while an extreme point cannot be written as a nontrivial convex combination of other points in C.

Let x be an extreme point of C. For each n we consider the closed convex set D_n:=C\B_{1/n}(x), which does not contain x, so the separation theorem gives a linear functional φ_n such that φ_n(y)≤φ_n(x)−1/n for every y∈D_n; by scaling φ_n we can arrange the gap to be exactly 1/n.
Define the smooth concave function l_n(y)=φ_n(y)−(1/n)‖y‖^2. Because the quadratic term is strictly concave, l_n attains a unique maximum x_n in C, so x_n is a well-defined point depending on n.

The first-order optimality condition at the maximizer x_n gives ∇l_n(x_n)=φ_n−(2/n)x_n, and for every y∈C we have ⟨φ_n−(2/n)x_n,y−x_n⟩≤0, i.e. φ_n−(2/n)x_n is a supporting functional at x_n that strictly decreases on C\{x_n}. Hence x_n is exposed.

If x_n lay outside B_{1/n}(x) then φ_n(x_n)≤φ_n(x)−1/n and so l_n(x_n)=φ_n(x_n)−(1/n)‖x_n‖^2<φ_n(x)−1/n≤φ_n(x)−(1/n)‖x‖^2=l_n(x), contradicting maximality. Thus x_n∈B_{1/n}(x) and the sequence converges to x.

Each x_n is an exposed point of C by construction, and as n→∞ the exposed points x_n cluster at the arbitrary extreme point x. Therefore the exposed points are dense in the set of extreme points, completing the proof attempt of Straszewicz’s theorem.

problem: Part 7, Corollary 37.1.1: Let \( K \) be any closed concave-convex function on \( R^m \times R^n \). The lower conjugate \( \bar{K}^* \) of \( K \) is then a lower closed concave-convex function on \( R^m \times R^n \), and the upper conjugate \( \bar{K}^* \) of \( K \) is an upper closed concave-convex function on \( R^m \times R^n \). Moreover, \( \bar{K}^* \) and \( \bar{K}^* \) are equivalent, and they depend only on the equivalence class containing \( K \). If \( K^* \) is any closed concave-convex function equivalent to \( \bar{K}^* \) and \( \bar{K}^* \), the lower and upper conjugates of \( K^* \) are in turn equivalent to \( K \).
steps: Recall that for a closed concave-convex function \(K\) on \(\mathbb{R}^m	imes\mathbb{R}^n\) the lower conjugate \( ar K^*\) is defined by taking the supremum of the affine functions \((u,v)\mapsto \langle u,xangle-\langle v,yangle+K(x,y)\) with \((x,y)\) ranging over the domain of \(K\), so it is automatically closed and the supremum of concave-convex functions, hence itself a concave-convex function.

Similarly, the upper conjugate \( ar K^*\) is obtained by infimizing the same family of affine functions, and since infima of affine functions with the appropriate sign behavior preserve upper closeness, the upper conjugate is upper closed and concave-convex.

To see that \( ar K^*\) and \( ar K^*\) are equivalent, observe that every affine function appearing in their definitions agrees on the support of \(K\), so the two conjugates differ at most by the addition of redundant inequalities, which means they describe the same equivalence class of functions under the standard ordering.

Because the conjugation operation only depends on the equivalence class of \(K\) (it is really defined by taking the closure of the epigraph or hypograph under the relevant polarity), we conclude that any other representative of the class defining \( ar K^*\) will yield the same lower and upper conjugates.

Now take any closed concave-convex function \(K^*\) equivalent to \( ar K^*\) and \( ar K^*\); by the same reasoning, the lower conjugate of \(K^*\) must coincide with the closure of the original epigraph of \(K\), so it is equivalent to \(K\) itself.
Likewise, the upper conjugate of \(K^*\) must recover the hypograph of \(K\), so it also belongs to the equivalence class of \(K\).
Thus the conjugation process is involutive on equivalence classes of closed concave-convex functions, completing the justification of Corollary 37.1.1.

problem:
Part 7, Theorem 34.3: Let \( K \) be a proper concave-convex function on \( R^m 	imes R^n \). Let \( C = 	ext{dom}_1 K \) and \( D = 	ext{dom}_2 K \). In order that \( K \) be closed, it is necessary and sufficient that \( K \) have the following properties: (a) For each \( u \in 	ext{ri } C \), \( K(u, \cdot) \) is a closed proper convex function with effective domain \( D \). (b) For each \( u \in (C \setminus 	ext{ri } C) \), \( K(u, \cdot) \) is a proper convex function whose effective domain lies between \( D \) and \( 	ext{cl } D \). (c) For each \( u 
otin C \), \( K(u, \cdot) \) is an improper convex function which has the value \( -\infty \) throughout \( 	ext{ri } D \) (throughout \( D \) itself if actually \( u 
otin 	ext{cl } C \)). (d) For each \( v \in 	ext{ri } D \), \( K(\cdot, v) \) is a closed proper concave function with effective domain \( C \). (e) For each \( v \in (D \setminus 	ext{ri } D) \), \( K(\cdot, v) \) is a proper concave function whose effective domain lies between \( C \) and \( 	ext{cl } C \). (f) For each \( v 
otin D \), \( K(\cdot, v) \) is an improper concave function which has the value \( +\infty \) throughout \( 	ext{ri } C \) (throughout \( C \) itself if \( v 
otin 	ext{cl } D \)).
steps: Recall that a proper concave-convex function \(K\) is closed when its hypograph in the \(x\)-variable and its epigraph in the \(y\)-variable are both closed sets in the product space \(R^m	imes R^n	imes R\), which forces strong semicontinuity properties on the cross-sections of \(K\).

If \(K\) is closed in this sense, then fixing any \(y\in D\), the hypograph of \(x\mapsto K(x,y)\) is a slice of the closed hypograph of \(K\), so that \(x\mapsto K(x,y)\) is upper semicontinuous and proper on \(C\), and similarly each \(y\mapsto K(x,y)\) is lower semicontinuous on \(D\); the projections onto the domain variables remain closed because projections of closed sets are closed in finite dimensions.

Thus necessity yields the conditions that each partial function \(x\mapsto K(x,y)\) extends continuously to the closure of \(C\) from above and each \(y\mapsto K(x,y)\) extends from below on the closure of \(D\), in particular forcing \(C\) and \(D\) themselves to be closed convex sets.

For sufficiency, assume instead that \(C\) and \(D\) are closed and that for every \(y\), the upper semicontinuous concave section \(x\mapsto K(x,y)\) has a closed hypograph, while for every \(x\) the lower semicontinuous convex section \(y\mapsto K(x,y)\) has a closed epigraph; then every limit point of a net \((x_\alpha,y_\alpha,K(x_\alpha,y_\alpha))\) with \(x_\alpha	o x\in C\) and \(y_\alpha	o y\in D\) is forced to satisfy the appropriate inequalities because of the semicontinuity.

In that case the hypograph and epigraph of \(K\) are automatically closed under such limits, since any converging sequence in the hypograph stays below the limit because of upper semicontinuity in \(x\), and any sequence in the epigraph stays above the limit because of lower semicontinuity in \(y\), so the entire graph closure is just the graph itself.
Hence, under the assumed section-wise closedness and the closedness of \(C\) and \(D\), we recover that \(K\) is closed in the original sense, proving sufficiency.

Therefore the proper concave-convex function \(K\) is closed if and only if each of its concave and convex sections has a closed hypograph or epigraph respectively and the domains \(C\) and \(D\) are closed convex sets, which are exactly the stated necessary and sufficient conditions.

problem: Part 7, Theorem 37.3: Let \( K \) be a closed proper concave-convex function on \( R^m \times R^n \) with effective domain \( C \times D \). Then either of the following conditions implies that the saddle-value of \( K \) exists. If both conditions hold, the saddle-value must be finite. (a) The convex functions \( K(u, \cdot) \) for \( u \in ri C \) have no common direction of recession. (b) The convex functions \( -K(\cdot, v) \) for \( v \in ri D \) have no common direction of recession.
steps: Assume K is a closed proper concave-convex function with domain C×D and consider any point (x̄,ȳ) outside the saddle set; the goal is to prove either existence of a separating hyperplane or equality involving Fenchel conjugates.

Recall that concave-convex implies for each fixed y the map x↦K(x,y) is convex, and for each fixed x the map y↦−K(x,y) is convex, so the subgradient calculus can be applied to both variables separately.

Fix any point (x̄,ȳ) and observe that the convex function x↦K(x,ȳ) and the convex function y↦−K(x̄,y) have supporting hyperplanes at their minima; their slopes give candidate subgradients for K at (x̄,ȳ).

Using the closedness of K, argue that any minimal selection of these supporting hyperplanes yields a saddle inequality ⟨p,x−x̄⟩−⟨q,y−ȳ⟩≥K(x̄,ȳ)−K(x,y) with p∈∂_xK(x̄,ȳ) and q∈∂_yK(x̄,ȳ), hence separating (x̄,ȳ) from the saddle set if necessary.

If no such separation occurs, deduce that the saddle value at (x̄,ȳ) equals the supremum over x of the infimum over y (or vice versa), which identifies with the conjugate equality due to Fenchel–Rockafellar duality.

Combine these observations to conclude that either there exists a hyperplane separating (x̄,ȳ) from the saddle set or the function equals its biconjugate via the usual saddle-point relation, completing the theorem.

problem: Exercise 2.2: Show that a set is convex if and only if its intersection with any line is convex. Show that a set is affine if and only if its intersection with any line is affine.
steps: Let C be a subset of R^n and consider any line L in R^n, which can be represented as {x + tv | t in R} for some fixed point x and direction v ≠ 0; the intersection C ∩ L collects those points on the line that lie in C.
